index_threads.text: Let’s learn together! This tweet will be a thread of threads — lecture notes, essentially, on some of the formative papers in economics. I hope this is a helpful guide to being an economist. https://t.co/XxtgTVuNac

There are now so many papers in the thread — 73 of them! — that it’s become entirely unmanageable. I am starting a new thread here. Please consider sharing if you find economics interesting. https://t.co/addGDvQgx0

I write about papers, and collect them here. There are now well over 150 papers cataloged for you to learn about. I’m starting a new thread to keep it manageable. Remember, if you like my work, you’d love my blog. I publish 4-5 times a week.
https://t.co/ep27yo4acK https://t.co/8Tk6sbxZ90

papers.entry_text:
Krugman (1980) “Scale Economies, Product Differentiation, and the Pattern of Trade” https://t.co/mAu6pReZzH

https://t.co/4osi6fba43

detailed_thread.text: We’re going to go through all of these papers together! First off, Krugman (1980), which is the jumping off point for most modern trade theory work. Why do similar countries trade with each other? Would countries trade, even if identical? 1/ https://t.co/XxtgTVuNac

Yes, if you allow for increasing returns. The cost of production is assumed to take the form a + bx, where a and b are constants and x is the number of units. Thus, average cost is declining in number of units produced. https://t.co/MGyw67Ury5

The wage rate is connected to the size of the labor force. As we have more people, we unlock more and more economies of scale. This is the reason why trade is good — it is as if we suddenly increased in population.

Since there are trade costs between countries but not within, wages are higher in larger countries. To economize on trade costs, firms will locate production in the countries with biggest trade costs (the “home-market” effect). That contrasts with decreasing returns to scale —
—which predicts the opposite. A rule of thumb this gives us is that goods with sharply  decreasing returns to scale (agriculture, perhaps?) should exhibit the opposite effect.

The treatment of oligopoly deserves special mention. We avoid problems of indeterminacy by having each firm produce one good with an identical cost function, and making the goods large (so that it doesn’t effect the decisions of other firms). https://t.co/k8Rtm2SzSV

papers.entry_text:
Krugman (1981) “Intraindustry Specialization and the Gains from Trade”
https://t.co/PaLF1aMstQ

https://t.co/MtakC09Xig

detailed_thread.text: Paul Krugman would refer to his 1981 paper on intraindustry trade as the “killer app” of new trade theory. When does trade benefit everyone? When does it make some groups worse off? https://t.co/obP6ViwyYw

The important thing is that trade comes in two types, intra and interindustry trade. Interindustry trade is the standard form first described by Ricardo — countries have different endowments of factors, and can gain by specializing in some categories of goods.

Trade only occurs because of differences in factors — if they were identical, no one would pay the transportation costs to go from one country to another. 
Intraindustry trade requires increasing returns. In this world, trade happens even with identical factor endowments,

As in Krugman (1980), each firm produces only one product, and wages increase as countries get larger.

The contribution of Krugman (1981) is that intraindustry trade always makes everyone better off. It is as if the country got larger. On the other hand, interindustry trade …
Can make some factors worse off. The owners of the scarce factors lose out, compared to autarky. Whether it makes everyone better off depends simply on how similar the factors of production are.

We can think outside of the box, and apply this to immigration. Why do people dislike low-skilled immigration, even if it makes us better off overall? If the mix of skills differ, then it can change the distribution of income. On the other hand, immigration from
a country with the same skills as America will make us strictly better off. Thus, we can understand why immigration from Central America is much more controversial than immigration from Canada.
Paper here — unfortunately, it’s the joe, so paywalled. https://t.co/I1V94F5Y6G
jpe, sorry

papers.entry_text:
Melitz and Trefler, “Gains from Trade when Firms Matter” (2012)

https://t.co/wiksdq1iKI

https://t.co/ozzDeBKnwX

detailed_thread.text: When we started with the Krugman trade papers, we assumed that firms were homogenous. What happens when we allow firms to have different levels of productivity? The gains from trade increase, because the more productive firms get a larger share of business. 1/

Melitz and Trefler (2012) is a JPE article, which you should read to get the intuition before we jump into the formal modeling of this. The intuition can be boiled down to this — when markets get larger, the increase in market size benefits the more productive firms more… https://t.co/3jLoHqfpw1

than the increase in competition hurts them. You can also think that firms choose between different production plans, some of which involve low fixed costs and high marginal costs, and other high fixed costs and low marginal costs. Expanding market size benefits the latter.

We can also think about this in the context of innovation. Firms pay a fixed cost to do research, and get an idea they can replicate at little marginal cost. Thus, an increase in market size should increase the rate of innovation, without even considering the flow of ideas!

Their results also update Krugman (1981) — even intraindustry trade can create winners and losers. The least productive firms are going to exit the market, and if there are frictions, some people are going to be worse off.

It is important to remember that the gains from trade in this structure are always positive, though.

Next, we will cover Melitz (2003), an extension of Krugman (1980), and the foundational paper of the modern trade literature.

Melitz and Trefler, “Gains from Trade when Firms Matter” (2012)

https://t.co/wiksdq1iKI

papers.entry_text:
Marc Melitz, “The Impact of Trade on Intraindustry Reallocations and Aggregate Industry Productivity” (2003)   

https://t.co/ICTiNeNqso

https://t.co/c86LiPuZAJ

detailed_thread.text: Trade models like that of Krugman (1980) assume firms face homogenous cost functions, but this is plainly not true. Firms vary in their productivity, even within narrowly defined industries. What does that mean for the gains from trade? Let’s explore Melitz (2003) 1/ https://t.co/w06Balqqoq

Like Krugman (1980), which this model builds upon, we use Dixit-Stiglitz preferences, and have a constant elasticity of substitution between products. This implies that every firm charges a fixed percentage above its marginal cost, determined by the elasticity of substitution.

Costs, like Krugman, are a + bx. This time, however, firms vary in their b, the variable cost to produce a unit. Firms draw their b from a distribution, which has a finite mass but continues on infinitely. https://t.co/8corfQLRcg
When firms enter, some see their high marginal costs and choose to immediately exit. Everyone else produces, and firms with lower marginal produce more. https://t.co/A2fDbgdSU6

It is important to understand that the gains from trade from business reallocating to other firms is something of a trick! If there were no trade costs, then there would be no reallocation. Likewise, if trade costs are only “iceberg”, or a percentage of good’s value, no change.

We assume that firms face a fixed cost to go into business in another country, and that, unlike their entry decision, they know their own productivity. Only the most productive firms export, and some of the least productive firms exit.

You do not get this without fixed costs! Trade costs which are perfectly proportional to the value of the good don’t change things, which is why they are often favored analytically. (It’s really nice to have costs be invariant to scale).

I want you to also pay attention to the nature of the distribution which firms draw their productivity from. As Arkolakis, Costinot, and Rodriguez-Clare would show in 2012, that unbounded distribution actually makes it identical to Krugman’s original model.

And as Melitz and Redding would show in 2015, the gains from trade are very sensitive to that distributional assumption. Once you truncate that distribution, you no longer have a constant elasticity of substitution, and things get weird.
But that’s coming later. Until then, read Melitz closely and carefully, and try to understand why it works the way it does.

Marc Melitz, “The Impact of Trade on Intraindustry Reallocations and Aggregate Industry Productivity” (2003) 

https://t.co/ICTiNeNqso

papers.entry_text:
Dornbusch, Fisher, and Samuelson “Comparative Advantage, Trade, and Payments in a Ricardian Model with a Continuum of Goods” (1977)  https://t.co/fqxadBu70K https://t.co/OqbqYOuZRf
https://t.co/K1Lz60vGqU

detailed_thread.text: Interlude 1: the Dornbusch-Fisher-Samuelson model of Ricardian trade. Before moving to Eaton-Kortum 2002, we should start with its precursor. DFS solves a problem with Ricardian trade models — when you allow for there to be many goods, solving it is often intractable. 1/

DFS use the same trick Krugman uses — you have a continuum of goods. Preferences are strictly homothetic — in other words, the proportion of goods desired does not change as income changes. If you desire 3 apples for every 2 oranges, you will also want 6 to 4 and 12 to 8.

We have perfect competition, and constant returns to scale. Labor is the only input of production.

What DFS do cause trade is give countries different relative productivities over the continuum of goods, expressed as a function.

With that, we can suddenly think of it as just a supply and demand curve. Below is modeled an increase in the foreign population. It reduces the number of varieties produced at home, but increases home wages. https://t.co/Gy1vjHadPI

Adding trade costs, if “iceberg”, is simple. You simply take the equation determining who produces which, and multiply by the fraction of goods which will get through. Some of the marginal industries end up produced at home.

“Iceberg” trade costs, btw, are just assuming that trade costs take the form of a percentage of the good being lost in transit, like an iceberg melting. This makes trade costs proportionate to the value of the good, which makes everything invariant to income.

Where we’re going with this next is the “gravity” equation. The model predicts that size of trade flows will be predicted by country size and trade costs. We can use this in empirical work to estimate what trade flows would be in the absence of costs, and derive counterfactuals. https://t.co/G2R7WGjymV
This model is only for two countries. Eaton and Kortum will extend this to many countries.

Dornbusch, Fisher, and Samuelson “Comparative Advantage, Trade, and Payments in a Ricardian Model with a Continuum of Goods” (1977)

https://t.co/fqxadBu70K
https://t.co/OqbqYOuZRf

papers.entry_text:
Eaton and Kortum, “Technology, Geography, and Trade” (2002)

https://t.co/3YXfDB5O7A

detailed_thread.text: It’s now time to tackle Eaton and Kortum (2002). Be warned — this paper is very hard, and it will likely take you a while to get what’s going on. It is, nevertheless, one of the foundations of modern trade theory. 1/ https://t.co/FzamriqfdT

I am going to try and get after the intuition behind everything, rather than work through the equations. These tweets are not a substitute. I found Lydia Cox’s slides quite helpful. https://t.co/maTepDKEHs
DFS could deal only with one country pair. What if we want to deal with many countries? EK deal with this by treating technology and comparative advantage as realizations of a random variable.

Specifically, they treat it as a Frechet distribution. T is the absolute advantage, and theta is the dispersion of productivity across countries. T increasing is strictly welfare improving. Higher theta implies greater gains from trade. https://t.co/CtyAplo9eo

You then gotta estimate the parameters. The way they do it is use distance as a proxy for trade barriers (after controlling for language and such) and seeing how much trade flows are influenced by this. (You have to know prices for this first). https://t.co/dAQXAJ4SNg

EK only have one year of observations, but you can also totally use plausibly exogenous shocks. Dave Donaldson would do this in “Railroads of the Raj”, which is applied EK basically, where weather shocks change the cost of food and can be used to extract the parameters.
You’re going to need to work through this one, I’m afraid — I’m concerned I don’t understand it deeply enough myself, I’m afraid.

Eaton and Kortum, “Technology, Geography, and Trade” (2002)

https://t.co/GqbXMV9SPE
The funny thing is, this is — by far — the worst tweet thread of all the one’s I’ve written.

papers.entry_text:
Robert Feenstra, “Alternative Sources of the Gains from International Trade: Variety, Creative Destruction, and Markups” (2018)

https://t.co/Usi40tnxTu

detailed_thread.text: Interlude 2: Alternative Sources of the Gains from Trade. This paper is a short, light read, so I will not be commenting on the paper itself, but on some more general thoughts on the gains from trade. What are the benefits of openness? How is that different from free trade? 1/ https://t.co/fUdL5Bdv4D

Economists are strongly in favor of free trade, yet the estimates of the potential gains are often surprisingly small. A reasonable possibility is that, in our minds, “free trade” incorporates a bundle of characteristics which we might call “openness”.

For example, we take it for granted that ideas can spread across countries costlessly. But can we really expect that to be so? For starters, many ideas are innovations in management which cannot be easily communicated.
Bloom, Sadun, and Van Reenen have numerous papers on the differences in multinationals based upon their country of origin. Culture is not easily portable without people. https://t.co/snjN9huQI6

Or what about ideas themselves? Surely you can just read about what people are doing? Even here this isn’t the case! Marta Prato found that a co-inventor immigrating made both people more productive, as the migrant could communicate ideas back home. https://t.co/kYqlJ2GUXL

We know diffusion is important because there isn’t enough trade, under plausible assumptions of the dispersion of productivity, to explain growth. (This is ARC 2007 — to understand what is meant by theta, consult EK 2002! https://t.co/cOy7IhfFEX
Rodriguez-Clare estimates simply enormous gains from openness, as opposed to simply the trade in goods. https://t.co/mcSRazuNBo

Think about how much Japan grew after becoming open to the world. If you just look at data on tradable goods, you get an upper bound of 9 percent of GDP. It’s ideas that matter, and we can’t really model the diffusion of ideas except as a residual. https://t.co/XrbbEe85UP
You should treat the gains from trade as a lower bound of the gains that come from a nexus of associated behaviors.

papers.entry_text:
Arkolakis, Costinot, Rodriguez-Clare, “New Trade Models, Same Old Gains?” (2012) 

https://t.co/FyeKXLuPa9

https://t.co/wXHHwPNCJJ

detailed_thread.text: So far, we’ve been adding margins on which we can gain from trade. We’ve added changes in variety, from competition, and from reallocation between firms. But what if I told you that none of that mattered? What if I told you that they are *all the same model*? 1/ https://t.co/hGtxvSklFH

That’s the contention of Arkolakis, Costinot, and Rodriguez-Clare (henceforth ACR). They argue that, under some restrictive functional form assumptions, the gains from trade can be given entirely by the share of expenditures on imports, and the elasticity wrt trade costs. https://t.co/irRcOVcKcc
This makes measurement extremely easy — you can find the answer using only national level statistics. The micro doesn’t matter, only macro does.

The restrictions needed are pretty severe. You need a constant elasticity of substitution, so this result doesn’t apply to pro-competitive gains from trade, which require variable markups. (CES means that every firm’s markup is a fixed percentage of price). https://t.co/PN4tRNok0c

In order for the Melitz model to follow this, you need the productivities of the firms to be drawn from a Pareto distribution. Remember that in Melitz, firms don’t know their variable costs until they enter. The distribution cannot be truncated, though the area is finite.

The reason is that when you truncate the distribution, you no longer have a constant elasticity. You can’t use elasticities estimated from a cost change from 11 to 10 percent, and use it to estimate 1 to 0 percent.
Melitz and Redding (2015) would show the relevance of this, in suggested reading. Thus, it should be seen as a lower bound. It’s still one of the most beautiful results ever.
Arkolakis, Costinot, Rodriguez-Clare, “New Trade Models, Same Old Gains?” (2012) https://t.co/FyeKXLuPa9

papers.entry_text:
Arkolakis, Costinot, and Rodriguez-Clare, “The Elusive Pro-Competitive Effects of Trade” (2019)

https://t.co/VhGR4CyHeM

https://t.co/JOfGs8DWG4

detailed_thread.text: One of the possible gains from trade is exposing inefficient firms to competition. Competition is robustly associated with lower prices and markups. But what if I told you that allowing for competition actually *reduces* the gains from trade? 1/ https://t.co/Hzf6jsUGRk

So far, our use of a CES utility function has ruled out the possibility of pro-competitive gains from trade. Markups under CES are a fixed fraction of the marginal cost, so it’s not possible for them to vary.

Instead, you need a framework where people’s tradeoffs between different goods can vary over different margins. In other words, we rule out homothetic preferences. Homoethetic preferences are simply that the ratio of goods you demand is always the same.

So if you demand two apples for every orange, you would demand four apples for every two oranges, and so on. Non-homothetic preferences break that — you might have different marginal utilities for the goods, and buy a greater proportion at different incomes.

Melitz and Ottoviano (2008) is a good baseline here for what we’re doing. A larger market means a higher cut-off to produce at all, so the firms which do produce are more efficient. https://t.co/aYGOsfY58s

Arkolakis, Costinot, Donaldson, and Rodriguez-Clare build upon ACR (2012). The effect of competition on trade can be shown by the parameter eta, which can simply be estimated. Note that you still have to have the restrictive assumptions of ACR (2012). https://t.co/Gdu8nbXJqN

This is fully estimatable, from the elasticity of trade (as in ACR) and from the observed elasticity of markups p. But this isn’t the main result, which is that eta is positive! (Or in other words, it *reduces* the gains from trade!) What? Why? https://t.co/uXvpOkjB1d
The first thing that’s happening is that, while domestic firms reduce their markup, foreign firms can increase theirs. https://t.co/gTlHdfG6pP

Non-homotheticity is key here. Sure the least productive firms exit, but they only had a small role in what people consume. People reallocate their purchases to the firms which had the highest markups! https://t.co/1BQCh6ZQvn

Because of this, the gains from trade are lower than in the world with CES. It’s such a cool result. Note that, unlike Brander-Krugman (we’ll get to them) any reduction in gains from trade are not due to frictions, but are built in.

However, we mustn’t be carried away. The gains are smaller than expected, but they are smaller only because the rise in income changes purchasing behavior. Trade is still good! But, competition need not increase its goodness.

Arkolakis, Costinot, Donaldson, Rodriguez-Clare, “The Elusive Pro-Competitive Effects of Trade” (2019)

https://t.co/VhGR4CyHeM
https://t.co/szaIimrKPG I also found Dave Donaldson’s slides very helpful

papers.entry_text:
Brander and Krugman, “An International Dumping Model of International Trade” (1983)  

https://t.co/oFnDN9zGx3

https://t.co/dxwIwgIaXK

detailed_thread.text: When might reduced trade costs lower welfare? What conditions do we need to impose to get that result? The key is “frictions” — as we shall see, this is behind all examples of trade having costs and benefits. First, Brander and Krugman (1983) 1/ https://t.co/2tszwsVWkB

They consider two firms in two countries, Home and Away, each one of them a monopoly in their own country. Both of them are engaged in Cournot competition, which is where they choose their quantity to produce for each market, and then let the price fall where it may.

Goods produced in each company’s home market can be sold without transport cost; shipping a good from one market to another costs some amount. If there is no trade between the countries, then each firm produces as a monopoly.

If there are trade costs between the countries such that trade is possible, then each company produces goods for both markets. Because they pay a shipping cost, the total amount produced is now less. The consumer gains, but the loss to the producer is greater.
The key assumption here is that other firms are not free to enter. If they are, then free trade is *always* welfare increasing.

We can recast Krugman (1981) now in the lens of a friction. If there were only one factor of production, then opening trade will never create winners and losers, only winners. It is only the inability to transform one into the other which introduces distributional consequences.

In the next couple days, we will cover one of the most famous papers on winners and losers from trade — Autor-Dorn-Hanson on the “China Shock”. As others have shown, a substantial of its large effect size was because of our zoning laws. https://t.co/RDrSpviUtR
People have a hard time reskilling, obviously. But they would have a much easier time if it were easy to move!

Brander and Krugman, “An International Dumping Model of International Trade” (1983)

https://t.co/lhT4Kzb0WP

papers.entry_text:
Interlude 3: Costas Arkolakis, “Market Penetration Costs and the New Consumer Margin in International Trade” (2010)

https://t.co/doPnwBwec6

https://t.co/jM3UuVexsE

detailed_thread.text: The Melitz model provides a reason for increases in export opportunities to increase the production of more productive firms, but in many important respects, it does not fit the facts. How can we fix this? Costas Arkolakis proposes an elegant solution. 1/ https://t.co/6VnbodCuzK

In the Melitz (2003) model, firms pay a fixed cost to enter, and then receive a random productivity level from a Pareto distribution. When export opportunities arise, they pay another fixed cost, except this time they do know their productivities.

This gives several predictions: more productive firms produce more, less productive firms exit, and new firms have lower productivities and are more likely to exit. However, it also predicts that once firms export, they shouldn’t vary much in how much they produce.

Actual trade flows show that many firms export only small quantities of goods. Arkolakis rationalizes this by adding a rising marginal cost to find consumers within the new country. Firms face a fixed cost to export, and then have to pay to find others.

Note that if this cost is 0, then it is identical to the Melitz model. This gives us predictions which are totally born out in trade data — a decline in trade costs will induce a faster rate of growth in the least traded products.
It’s a small change, but an elegant one. I rather like papers like this.

Costas Arkolakis, “Market Penetration Costs and the New Consumer Margin in International Trade” (2010)

https://t.co/doPnwBwec6

papers.entry_text:
Autor, Dorn, and Hanson, “The China Syndrome: Local Labor Market Effects of Import Competition in the United States” (2013) 

https://t.co/seaRbSAtKm

https://t.co/FzslpIPjP7

detailed_thread.text: Autor, Dorn, and Hanson is the canonical shift-share paper, and something you should absolutely have read and understood if you want to do serious work in economics today. 1/ https://t.co/G5fQdVerN2

I am not discussing it so much for the empirical results as for the methods, but it follows in the footsteps of Krugman (1981) and Brander and Krugman. If you allow for frictions, trade will create winners and losers. How big are these?

They found (and this is made clear in the follow up work) that it’s not so much the place which is harmed, but specific workers. Many of the affected workers take disability and exit the labor force, with permanently reduced wages as a result. https://t.co/yOusXwgXkI

But, on to the methods. Using whole countries gives you very few observations and masks regional disparities. Using arbitrary geographic units will also give you a misleading picture. Instead they use commuting zones, which are defined as shared labor markets.

A shift-share instrument uses the difference in exposure to plausibly exogenous shocks as its source of variation. If China becomes much better at manufacturing carpets, then places which manufacture carpets are much more affected than others.

Of course, the industries which China specializes in could be endogenous to demand. If there’s a big surge in demand in the US for carpets, then it could be that both China and the US expand production in that sector. This would completely reverse the sign!

To get around this, ADH construct a panel of China’s exports to other high income countries. If exports of a given good increase to all countries, you can argue that this is due to China getting more productive.

Of course, this is vulnerable to trends in demand which are shared across countries. Here we can get out our gravity equations, which relate expected trade flows to size and “distance” (or trade costs). The residuals check out. https://t.co/yw2kT4foHf

It is also possible that what is happening is that the United States — or every high income country — is actually just suffering coincidental negative supply shocks. This is, however, extremely implausible. https://t.co/zQiPqGdqBj

Shift-share instruments are now used in so many contexts. You should open up Borusyak, Hull, and Jaravel (2025) now. (“A Practical Guide to Shift Share Instruments”)

https://t.co/Y0OdB4Ykme
There are two paths to answering questions — either use exogenous shifts, without having an opinion on why the shares are how they are, or take shares as exogenous. https://t.co/dGQXNhpDSs
(Adao et al, btw, would essentially take the Duflo et al (2004) critique of difference-in-difference estimators and apply it to shift-share. One must pool non-independent observations.)
So long as shifts are random, a weighted average of shifts is also random. https://t.co/nqryiTfS12
A checklist of things to do. (I really can’t add much here). https://t.co/PkyDm7txRw

You can really see how the two approaches are similar to each other here. Is people retiring a shock? Or is the share of people of retirement age the exogenous part? It gets us to the same answer. https://t.co/99GUVeE4OS
Just pick the one that makes the most sense in context. https://t.co/nHBAxwpgYH

On my blog soon, I will go over how the empirical results have held up. Can you take their estimates as general equilibrium effect sizes? How have the affected people and areas fared since. Until then, read it, and consider it.

Autor, Dorn, and Hanson, “The China Syndrome: Local Labor Market Effects of Import Competition in the United States” (2013) 

https://t.co/seaRbSAtKm
This may be of interest to @instrumenthull @ATabarrok @davidautor @XJaravel @borusyak @gordon_h_hanson and @ProfDavidDorn (I hope you’ll pardon me pinging)

papers.entry_text:
Paul Romer, “New Goods, Old Theory, and the Welfare Costs of Trade Restrictions” (1993)

https://t.co/BR5UtdAtzl

https://t.co/cx5pjLIbXe

detailed_thread.text: Paul Romer’s paper “New Goods, Old Theory, and the Welfare Costs of Trade Restrictions” is, secretly, one of the best rationalist blog posts of all time. 1/ https://t.co/MiJie75HfK

In terms of economics, he is concerned with what allowing for new goods means for the effects of distortions. In matters of philosophy, he wants us to move beyond the implicit “plenitude” of general equilibrium theory. https://t.co/IAQKISBd7z

What he means by this is the belief that all things have been invented. In general equilibrium theory ala Arrow and Debreu, every type of good is taken as given, and we can create a security to represent every state-contingent outcome. https://t.co/kgvyTw9TyT
If you grant that we have many possible, unknown paths, then the main feature of the world is sparsity. It is actually new goods which overwhelming matter for the world. https://t.co/fgu1j3YxY7
Why didn’t we do this? Partly because the math is intractable, but also because we are profoundly biased against seeing ourselves as an accident, not an inevitability. https://t.co/pUqfh7XsO3

He then gives his gloss on the pioneering work of Dupuit, an engineer who, in the 1840s, basically invented textbook microeconomics. Later authors focused on avoiding deadweight loss, but that’s not what Dupoit cared about.
Dupoit wanted to know which bridges should be build, not how to avoid distortions once built.

Romer turns now to trade restrictions, which could really be any distortion. People face a fixed cost to invent a good. If people do not anticipate the tariff, then normal micro holds. The welfare loss is small.
If, however, the tariff is anticipated, the welfare loss becomes much, much larger. Goods which would have been invented never are.
To Romer, then, focusing on static allocative efficiency is a mistake. What really matters is insufficient invention, caused by inventors getting but a small part of their surplus. I agree with him.

Paul Romer, “New Goods, Old Theory, and the Welfare Costs of Trade Restrictions” (1993)

https://t.co/VOhvFEOMDU

https://t.co/SJWJ2MkLRI

This is also worth a read.

papers.entry_text:
Unit 2: Economic Geography

Paul Krugman, “Increasing Returns and Economic Geography” (1991)

https://t.co/82nozrqWh4

detailed_thread.text: Economists have historically had much to say about trade between countries, but little on trade within countries. Why is industry sometimes concentrated? Why is it sometimes dispersed? A short thread on Krugman (1991) https://t.co/Kf0b7L53w7
Krugman is taking his insight of increasing returns explaining trade, so his model is going to look similar to his prior work, just with some factor mobility added.

There are two regions, A and B. There are two goods, agriculture and manufactures, and two types of workers to produce each. Agriculture is constant returns to scale, and manufacturing has increasing returns, with the cost structure a + bx as before.
It costs r to ship a good between the regions, with trade costs taking the form (1-r)x — or in other words, r percent of the good is destroyed in transport.

Suppose all manufacturers are in B. Is this an equilibrium? A manufacturer trying to move to A from B will do so only if the wage premium it has to pay to induce people to move is less than the gains from selling to the farmers.

So now we can intuitively grasp some things. As the share that manufactures form of expenditures increases, the wage premium increases. So too are the economies of scale, except these are from people having access to more varieties.

Transportation costs are more interesting. We can see obviously that if they are 0, concentration does not matter (this is since economies of scale is actually just love of variety in disguise, not spillovers as we normally think of them).
If they are 1, then complete dispersal occurs — people want to sell to the farmers. In the middle, though, it actually becomes an equilibrium of concentration.

We can sketch out a plausible history of the US. With high transport costs, manufacturing is dispersed. As it falls, it concentrates in the rust belt. Now that it’s fallen still further, we globalize.

Paul Krugman, “Increasing Returns and Economic Geography” (1991)

https://t.co/xwwolhfgft
Note that this is just the intuition! Go read the paper, it’s very short and clear. I simply don’t want to insight to be lost at the sight of intimidating squiggles.

papers.entry_text:
Paul Romer, “The Origins of Endogenous Growth” (1990)

https://t.co/bY4QjHSiLW

detailed_thread.text: Why did the theory of endogenous growth develop? What problems is it solving? Before we go into the details of how the models work, I want to provide the intellectual context. 1/ https://t.co/2dusNmtfva

Recall that, in the Solow model, output is a function of labor and capital, with it converging to a steady state. Technological progress is represented by an A multiplying the function. Capital has decreasing marginal returns.

This gives us some strong predictions which at the time were not true. Since capital has declining returns, the return to capital should be much higher in developing countries. Yet, we do not observe capital flowing from rich to poor!

Similarly, we should expect countries with a lower income (if we are explaining purely through the accumulation of capital) to grow much faster than rich countries. They plainly do not. https://t.co/uxixuYIeVy

Countries would have to save orders of magnitude less in order to produce that little. Clearly, the level of technology must differ between countries, and something must not be paid its marginal product. https://t.co/tuMPHZlK1B

The second strand of thought was the shift away from perfect competition. Perfect competition was simple and tractable, but gave us some bad conclusions, ruling out market power, firms, and economies of scale. https://t.co/UkQzliidF9

What neatly solves both problems is allowing for ideas to be the product of economic action. Thus, we can explain where all this missing marginal product is going — capital, which could be searching for ideas, is undercompensated.
This is the fundamental limit on growth. We do not ensure the marginal benefit of finding new ideas goes to that which creates them. Growth, then, is above all about how fix that.

Paul Romer, “The Origins of Endogenous Growth” (1994)

https://t.co/wZSWspBlR9

papers.entry_text:
Ezra Oberfield, “Inequality and Measured Growth” 

https://t.co/PGDPxldWYH

detailed_thread.text: Suppose two groups face persistently different rates of inflation year after year. Believe it or not, this tells us nothing about the rate of change in consumption inequality. It could be perfectly equal in every way. A short thread on why 1/ https://t.co/VtvGGuGXN1

Some context first from the Xavier Jaravel paper on the unequal gains from product innovation. He documents persistent differences in inflation experience between the rich and the poor — about half a percentage point, every year. https://t.co/JIUIImPZf5

This is largely coming through the introduction of new goods which cater to the rich, rather than prices falling per se; prices are quality adjusted, so more products implies greater utility. Obviously you need structural assumptions about how they enter into utility.

The channel for this is “induced innovation”. As demand increases, producers can invest in more efficient ways of producing the goods, or in paying the fixed cost of creating a differentiated variety. In the long run, the supply curve slopes downward.

Ezra Oberfield demonstrates how this can tell us nothing about actual inequality though. Let’s specify that everyone has an identical set of non-homothetic preferences. That is, people consume different proportions of goods as they grow richer, and only income effects matter.
Goods become cheaper for two reasons. First, there is a secular trend toward technology improving. Second, there is demand driven innovation — as we produce more, the price per unit falls.

A world in which everyone has equal income sees the same inflation and consumption growth for everyone. Now let’s suppose that there is inequality. The bundle of goods which the rich consume will fall in price at a faster rate.

Yet, the total rate at which goods fall in price remains the same. As poor people get richer, they begin to consume the same goods, at the new lower price. Goods fall in price when the poor are not consuming them, yet their growth rate in consumption is identical.

Put concretely: rich people buy Teslas, and they fall in price. When they fall in price, poor people buy them. Both are gaining, and at the same rate, but only the rich people experience their prices changing.
No one, to my knowledge, has pursued showing how meaningful this is. It is one of the big unanswered questions in economics.

https://t.co/WSkZTYKlkZ
https://t.co/Wvdb6Xn72N

You can read the papers here. @XJaravel @SorryToBeKurt @Ale_M_Peter

papers.entry_text:
Dave Donaldson, “The Railroads of the Raj” (2018)

https://t.co/8BXTg17DY2

detailed_thread.text: How important was the railroad, and why did it raise incomes? Dave Donaldson’s classic paper “Railroads of the Raj” found that access to the railroad raised real income by 16%, and trade accounted for half. Let’s understand how he got this! 1/ https://t.co/Usz5h2jJKY

India’s infrastructure, before the railroad construction campaigns began, was extremely poor. Transport was main through putting packs on the back of cattle, and driving them through fields, which was incredibly expensive. Railroads were both much cheaper, and much faster.

The railroads were built, first and foremost, for military reasons. A standard approach here would just be to regress “receives railroad” on places that were supposed to get it, and didn’t, and compare. https://t.co/lMu357idyB
He will, in fact, do this, as a placebo test, but he is interested in deeper questions. This approach cannot help understand why incomes would have rose, however, as there are many possible channels.

Instead, he has a four part strategy to estimate it. First, he has to estimate the costs of trade. To do this, he uses five varieties of salt which are manufactured only in one district, but are sold nationally. The difference in price between regions is the cost of trade.
This is much better than simply using distance, which doesn’t take into account all possible barriers. There are still links unaccounted for, so he imputes them with an algorithm.

Using these trade costs and observed trade flows across districts, he can then fill in the productivities of all the districts. Then, he can show how rainfall affects productivity in different districts. (Yes, he has daily rainfall data over the entirety of colonial India).
Now he does the regressing railroad access on income which I mentioned before. Here he finds that building the railroad would increase income by 16%. But how much is trade?

This essentially applied ACR (2012). A sufficient statistic for the gains from trade is share of expenditures on imported goods, raised by the elasticity with respect to trade costs. That elasticity can be estimated by using rainfall as shocks to productivity.
The gains from the railroad due to trade alone was 8%. Keep in mind that incomes during this period rose by only 30%. Trade really matters.
In a later paper, Donaldson would show that access to the railroad reduced the volatility of income, and thus reduced the rate of famine in India.
It’s genuinely one of the most astonishing papers I’ve ever read, and the finest example of extraordinary data collection tied to the best theory.

papers.entry_text:
Donaldson and Storeygard, “The View from Above”, (2016)

https://t.co/akVGex6yDT

detailed_thread.text: The widespread availability of satellite data has been extremely good for economic research. There are three main advantages: it can access information impossible to find by other means, it is extremely fine-grained, and it can cover a wide geographic area. Some cool studies 1/

The earliest use was nightlights, which can find effects even with very low resolution. Bleakley and Lin showed how settlement in the US was strongly affected by the now irrelevant “fall line”, and thus that agglomeration best explains cities. https://t.co/hy4ubIQb5m

Sadly night lights are going to no longer be a good indicator, as the newest lights aren’t picked up in satellite images. While we could use them, we were able to show dictators lie about GDP. But some more cool studies! https://t.co/bhpZ4LDYAZ

Take Burgess et al (2012). The Indonesian government wishes to reduce deforestation, but local officials can be bribed. Increasing the number of districts in a province, consistent with competition, allows for more logging and more deforestation. https://t.co/4Wbo0PE6bj

Satellite data has revolutionized our understanding of the effects of pollution. The vagaries of wildfire smoke are plausibly uncorrelated with other factors — thus, we can prove that air pollution caused tens of thousands of premature deaths. https://t.co/nCXsAN6nVu

The resolution is incredible. Below are rooftops, some new and shiny, while others are rusted. Marx, Stoker and Suri (2015) uses this as a proxy for income to discover the effects of co-ethnic elections. https://t.co/U1GuDOvc8z

These examples are largely taken from Dave Donaldson and Adam Storeygard’s 2016 survey article — there have been many more examples since, including Dev Patel’s “Floods”, which we will cover tomorrow.

https://t.co/Z2RZiICjvz

papers.entry_text:
Dev Patel, “Floods” (2025)

https://t.co/OSE7ZeWWMu

detailed_thread.text: "Floods", by @dev_a_patel, is one of the more astounding Big Data econ papers I have ever seen. Machine learning can be used on satellite data to essentially do magic. How damaging are floods? How much do they affect human capital attainment? A thread on the paper 1/ https://t.co/7pEBxxfqW9

The problem Dev Patel faces is that floods are extremely hard to measure. Finding out which places flooded and which didn't would require considerable work in on-the-ground data collection, which many countries don't do.
River stations are sparse and wholly inadequate. These are all the stations in the Khulna division, a region with 2.6 million people. https://t.co/XBn8M6GOYk
Inferring flooding from newspaper reports deprives us of a constant definition of flooding, and again lacks sufficient precision to identify which towns are more affected than others.

The answer is, of course, satellite data. Patel trains an ML model to recognize water, and monitors the daily inundation over 20 years for each of Bangladesh's 5,158 "unions" (local districts of perhaps 15 square kilometers). He checks other datasets, and finds they line up.

I'd like to take a moment to admire the dataset. Here, for example, is where surface water is permanent and where it is seasonal in Bangladesh. He made a rather pretty website explaining his methodology -- it's good for a few minutes gawking.
https://t.co/vZtMrsNzgw https://t.co/pUBPdgXKXl

Now with the dataset in hand, we can answer bigger questions. Floods substantially reduce income and economic activity in an area, however you measure it, and these effects last as long as he is able to measure them (7 years).
He has data on the footprint of every building in Bangladesh (bruh stop flexing on us), and finds that floods substantially reduce the area covered in buildings.

There is heterogeneity in the effects, however. Places which had had floods in the past had smaller effect sizes, suggesting that people can partially adjust to flooding. Turning now to administrative data on 19 million Bangladeshis (how do you keep getting this data??)

He shows that floods cause structural change. People move out and stop working in agriculture. A survey he conducted suggests that farmers who expect more floods expect their children to obtain more education and work out of agriculture.

This structural reallocation is plausibly affected by infrastructure, so he throws in this too. Places which were connected by the British for plausibly exogenous reasons had more migration, and so likely better personal outcomes.

It's a genuinely astonishing paper. At every step, he somehow exceeds any possible expectation you could have. I really think that this could be divided up into three papers, each of which would belong in the QJE. It is work that I wish were emulated.
https://t.co/eHbT41Gs9W

papers.entry_text:
Mankiw-Romer-Weil, Klenow and Rodriguez-Clare, and various other papers on human capital
https://t.co/WIczcYty42

detailed_thread.text: Having learned about the Solow model, how well does it actually explain the differences in income between countries? How large is the contribution of physical capital, in contrast to physical capital? Let's learn about Mankiw-Romer-Weil, and its critics. https://t.co/RU99UxJTOF https://t.co/4WMbQEmhul
To review though, the Solow model has output Y be a function of capital (K) and labor (L), with L multiplied by the level of technology A. These both are raised to the exponents alpha and 1-alpha.

(These summing to one has the handy property of making the exponents equal the share of income that each factor receives). L is exogenously determined, and K is a function of savings (also exogenous). Thus, changes in A increase wages.

MRW start by regressing measures of physical capital on GDP per capita. R^2 is the measure of how much of the variation in the regression is explained by the variables -- almost 60% is explained by it. https://t.co/MEOz9AIzNh

They can do one better though. MRW augment the production function by pulling human capital accumulation out of A, and treating it just like physical capital. Now it can explain almost 80% of the variation! https://t.co/HVFvuSkNFL

There's just one problem. The exponents are the share of income which go to each factor. They are much too large. Klenow and Rodriguez-Clare argue that policies which encourage efficiency are also correlated with capital accumulation, giving us a misleading conclusion. https://t.co/SX6bGtdPgx

Their approach is to fix the coefficients in the production function first, and only then see how much the regressions can explain. This is the opposite order that MRW take. Obviously, capital explains much less now -- "ideas" are back to being of first order importance.

The theoretical view of human capital explaining growth is much more ambivalent now. If you argue that workers are not substitutable for each other at all, then you can get back to arbitrarily high contributions of human capital. (And also explain immigration raising wages!) https://t.co/b2G2vx3ejg
Of course, this is maybe too strong. In theory, human capital can explain anywhere between 0 and 100 percent of income differences between countries. https://t.co/ewRuaoRPU0

We can get some evidence, however, from studies of immigrants. When they move, their wages rise, but they were just as skilled as when they left. Thus, it must be some x-factor -- A -- that is responsible.

Clemens (2013) is the best identified evidence -- the H-1B visa is randomly assigned, so immigration is uncorrelated with characteristics. Inside one single firm, people who moved massively increased their wages. https://t.co/G6CEO1QdcG

Hendricks and Schoellman (2018), building on papers they had written independently, argue that human capital accounts for 60% of immigrant wages, and 40% from the country. Workers are imperfectly substitutable as well. https://t.co/0osseU73my

If you are interested in this and more, be sure to subscribe to my blog! I publish three times a week, on topics such as this.
https://t.co/2UO76Ehh7B https://t.co/ST1z0D2Oyf

papers.entry_text:
Gentzkow and Shapiro, “What Drives Media Slant?” (2010)

https://t.co/CTlVOxklA9

detailed_thread.text: Why are newspapers biased? Are the owners pushing their ideology onto consumers, or simply providing what is demanded? Gentzkow and Shapiro (2010) is one of the most magical papers ever written — let’s see how they do it. 1/ https://t.co/HdsM8AJDew
What are the ingredients we need? First, we need to define “slant”, and we need to define it in a way that gives us actual quantities. (A subjective rating would make our regressions meaningless).

The solution they hit upon is to take the words which are used more by one party or another, and compare this to newspaper content. (Note that they exclude the opinion pages — they are only using the main content). https://t.co/BfSf3rzglX

With our slant measure in hand, we can now assign each of them a score showing how conservative or liberal they are, and then see how this affects consumer demand. They compare circulation in different zip codes, and find that it obviously corresponds to political preferences.

Given the observed consumer demand for slant, they can then ask what the profit-maximizing slant would be for a newspaper. Since this corresponds to what newspapers offer, they conclude that it is demand which drives slant. https://t.co/gH8VZ5DhnW

They check this belief by seeing how newspapers owned by the same groups alter their slant as they face different demands. There is no statistically significant correlation between newspapers owned by the same owners, in terms of slant.

They check against newspapers cause this belief by treating religiosity as an instrument. (That is, they say that newspapers don’t affect religiosity, but religiosity independently affects political orientation).
To be clear, I don’t believe this is a valid instrument, and I’m not convinced they do either, but it is in there.

This approach is called “structural” modeling. You are looking for some underlying fact about the world — in this case, how responsive demand is to slant — and then taking that fact and applying it elsewhere. It’s really cool.

papers.entry_text:
De Loecker, Eeckhout, Unger “The Rise of Market Power” (2020)

https://t.co/d1OALDVw2y

detailed_thread.text: I recently read De Loecker et al (2020), and came away extremely impressed. I will not cover the whole paper now — that will come later — but I wanted to cover how they estimate markups. But first, let’s show why all the other ways to estimate markups fail. 1/ https://t.co/PW1bPgmsEJ

First, you can simply try and subtract marginal cost from price. This is indeed how a markup is defined, but there is simply no way of actually ever knowing what the proper marginal cost is. You might, by careful study, obtain figures for one company — good luck with the economy!
Obtaining this from easily available data requires heroic assumptions — no fixed costs, constant returns to scale everywhere, perfect substitution, the whole lot. https://t.co/jwjqw9tcZk

The other common approach is to work from the demand side first, ala Berry-Levinsohn-Pakes. Going to digress briefly by turning to Aviv Nevo’s “Practitioner’s Guide” here. Under no circumstances rely upon what I write here; read the paper to fully understand this.
BLP break down consumer demand into observed and unobserved characteristics of the product, and the characteristics of the consumer. https://t.co/Q76bJm2N3X
You can then solve for market share. https://t.co/uf8079lm62

You will need to make distributional assumptions, or try and measure them as best you can. You also need to specify how firms are competing with each other. This is difficult to do, which is why such studies confine themselves to one industry. https://t.co/kyCcnDxWDd
In any event, our authors dismiss it. The data simply doesn’t exist, even if you did want to specify the mode of competition across the entire economy. So what is there to do? https://t.co/uOz7OsA80s

This was the part I thought was really cool. We are going to say that, in each period, firms are minimizing their costs against a constant production function. Variable inputs, like labor, can change, but capital needs time to accumulate. https://t.co/N1fqf0kOe5
Since you are doing a maximization under constraints, take the Lagrangian of the production function.
It turns out that the Lagrange multiplier is the marginal cost itself. https://t.co/kKt3iXVgDl

So, given a constant production function (which they will estimate at the industry level) all we need is the elasticity with respect to the cost of the variable input, and the share going to that input, all of which can be inferred from public data! https://t.co/6tjajf3vvF

Now, I’m not entirely convinced for a few reasons. Labor seems an awful lot like capital here, insofar as it takes time to accumulate. We cannot credibly separate out human capital here. Moreover, the stability of the production function is a bit heroic over decades.

Nevertheless, it is really awesome to be able to get *any* knowledge on market power and markups. You can read the whole paper here.
https://t.co/yVDxiqlYe5

papers.entry_text:
Bond, Hashemi, Kaplan, Zoch “Some Unpleasant Markup Arithmetic” (2021)

https://t.co/j43keiDmh3

detailed_thread.text: We have seen how one can estimate markups using data on outputs and inputs. But what if we only have revenue? Can we use this to recover markups? Buckle up for some unpleasant arithmetic. 1/ https://t.co/XnxWR4XEXo https://t.co/cj9pEeq8eU

To review, the DLEU approach is to estimate a production function for each industry, then take the ratio of the elasticity of output to the elasticity of its share of expenditures as the measure of markups.

However, they don’t have outputs directly. Instead, they use revenue as a proxy for output, and make the assumption that firms cannot affect the price of their own products. This isn’t satisfying, though — part of why we care about markups is market power!
If we break this assumption, how much can be salvaged? See how the revenue is jointly determined by the slope of the demand curve and its output elasticity. https://t.co/ZLov4SCoZD
If we have output, everything works — firms minimize cost within their technology, and we spit out the markup. Note how it’s the inverse of the demand elasticity. https://t.co/DX5oAutr2h

When we only have revenue, we end up dividing the demand elasticity by itself. It’s equal to one. It is informative if and only if the real markup is 0, or no markup whatsoever. https://t.co/vGsiYR1tOD

Other parts of the paper attack some other assumptions. For example, if the variable input isn’t quite variable, markups will include that and the cost of changing. Unobserved costs bias markups upwards. https://t.co/9BwgJrzFRo

It’s a really fantastic article. I’m happy to have finally understood it, years after I started. 
https://t.co/KCPo3jaZpP
Hi @pzoch5 this one’s for you

papers.entry_text:
Some papers on tax salience and incidence

https://t.co/tkZ5vL2MOz

detailed_thread.text: Who bears a tax? Standard economic theory assumes that who physically pays a tax has no effect on who actually has their consumption reduced. Yet, salience does have a meaningful effect on consumer behavior. Let’s look at some examples: 1/ https://t.co/GlBZD9KM7X

I will detour first by explaining why who pays the tax should be irrelevant. Suppose there is a buyer and a retailer, and we are imposing a sales tax. Suppose we place it on the retailer when they purchase the item. They will partially pass this tax on by raising the price.

How much depends upon the elasticity of demand. If demand is perfectly inelastic — that is, quantity purchased is not affected by price — the entire tax will be reflected in a price increase. If perfectly elastic, prices won’t change at all.

Now let’s suppose we place the tax on the buyer instead. This is just the same as if the retailer had raised the price, so if inelastic, the retailer keeps it the same price, and reduces if elastic. These two cases are like if a sales tax is included in the price, or added later.
There should be no difference. Yet, Chetty, Looney, and Kroft found large differences in purchasing behavior — it reduced purchases by 8%, despite the total price not changing a whit!

Or take the case of tolls. E-Z Pass made paying tolls less of a hassle to think about. It is not so much that consumption increased, which could be due to fewer traffic jams — it’s that driving became less responsive to price. https://t.co/efEjRIxPmB
A fun little implication of this is that toll-setting became less corrupt. Local governments in New Jersey would lower tolls around election time, just to juice the incumbents numbers.

I wrote about these and more in one of my early blog posts. Read it all here!

https://t.co/ZmlvJvzp9p

papers.entry_text:
Luis Martinez, “How Much Should We Trust the Dictator’s GDP Estimates?” (2024)

https://t.co/lyyO6Fae0z

detailed_thread.text: The connection between democracy and growth is surprisingly weak. The case is plausible, but the evidence largely isn’t there. What if I told you, though, that it’s all based on lies? Autocracies lie about growth much more than democracies, and adjusted for this, freedom is good. https://t.co/Rxy8NBwnws

This is the key chart. Luminosity at night is a way of measuring economic growth objectively. For any given rise in night lights, autocracies report more GDP growth. The figures are probably being fudged. https://t.co/egLA2ipYK6

Adjusting for this reduces annual growth rates in the worst countries by 15 to 30 percent. Note that these are growth rates, not levels, so this compounds into very large differences. https://t.co/C1A4LcEYGX

The full paper is here:

https://t.co/r8uR779StL https://t.co/yfESkGb9gb

papers.entry_text:
Moser, Parsa, and San, “Immigration and Innovation” (2025)

https://t.co/XU0gbHCVL8

detailed_thread.text: What happened to science and innovation when we restricted immigration in the past? The US anti-immigration quotas prevented at least a thousand major scientists from immigrating. Their loss not only deprived us of innovation, but made native scientists less productive too. https://t.co/g95KQTu8FO
They do this by seeing what fields Eastern and Southern European immigrants patented in. When the quotas came, patenting rates tanked. https://t.co/i80K2KzXge

Countries, like Canada, which did not implement quotas saw no such change. It is this, along with the effect on natives, which assures me that this is a sign of a real reduction in patenting, not merely displacement. https://t.co/qMjFtH9fRq

And of course, being able to work with more talented scientists makes every scientist more productive. This reminds me of a recent Marta Prato paper, in which she found that an inventor moving made even the people they left behind more productive. https://t.co/tWBcNlUxoi

I covered Prato’s work here, and you can read the other papers too:

https://t.co/vVeAhsZMET
https://t.co/eL4Iuawo7L
https://t.co/G7xlKzii0T

papers.entry_text:
Lee and Schaelling, “Content Relatability and Test Score Disparities” (2025)

https://t.co/3KswXiSKxP

detailed_thread.text: How biased are reading tests against Blacks and Hispanics? To what extent are disparities the result of unfair test questions? Bias does exist, but it explains very little — only 4 percent of the gap. 1/ https://t.co/0t4yOQx2zd

Their method is to take the corpus of tests and assign each to a topic, then compare to how often people report doing that activity in the American Time-Use Survey. The different levels of exposure does pass the smell test. https://t.co/huu7yi0zw8

If students were less experienced with a topic, they performed worse. If the tests were on topic they were equally exposed to, the students would be 4% closer. This is obviously very small though, and the cause of Black-white disparities is not this. https://t.co/ccUdIpVXew

However, because Texas is a very big state, the results do imply that 11,000 Black and 15,000 Hispanic students (between 2013 and 2019) were miscategorized as a result. Full paper here:
https://t.co/ADtRFsctdN
The paper is currently in R&R at the AER — @cremieuxrecueil you may find this interesting
From Steven Lee and @MattSchaelling

papers.entry_text:
Maya Eden, “The Cross-Sectional Implication of the Social Discount Rate” (2023)

https://t.co/E0ZPWm23MW

detailed_thread.text: This is an incredibly subtle, yet provocative paper. If you think we should treat future humans the same as present humans, then it implies that we should have massive transfers from old to young, even if wasteful! Let me show you why 1/ https://t.co/UJ99EwpkUA

I should be clear about the terms. The interest rate is simply what the rate we need to be paid in order to defer consumption. If interest rates are 6%, then I am indifferent between $100 and $106 in a year. (We will abstract away from risk-aversion here)
The social discount rate is how much the government should value future vs present humans. If 2%, then we should be indifferent between $100 of benefit now and $102 later.

The logic of the paper is realizing that a transfer over time can be thought of as a transfer between ages. We would obviously be indifferent to transferring a dollar between two people of the same age, because nothing is lost or gained.

However, that person would have been happier if they had received the dollar earlier in life, because they personally value consumption at the interest rate, not the social discount rate. Thus, they would have been happier receiving less than a dollar.

Now suppose that someone is just born. It would be, by that standard, worth it to transfer to them a smaller sum than what was taken from the older. Note that this is because of the difference between interest and social discount rate, and will flip is sdr is higher.
So what’s the upshot? If you support action on climate change, you must also support abolishing Medicare. You cannot support both.

Full paper here!
https://t.co/XsTUkvQIkx

papers.entry_text:
Raul Sanchez de la Sierra, “On the Origins of the State” (2021)

https://t.co/fsG7nBWIQR

detailed_thread.text: Why does the state emerge? Is it beneficial? Why are some states stronger than other states? It is rare that we can see a state emerge, but Raul Sanchez de la Sierra has 18 years of data collection efforts to answer the questions. 1/ https://t.co/wRIx1Fem2s

The “stationary bandit” view of the state derives from Mancur Olson. In the state of anarchy, some people are bandits who profit by stealing from others. This theft prevents people from increasing output, though. It is profitable for the bandit to settle down and impose taxes.
By guarding their population from other bandits, it is possible for both bandits and peasantry to be better off off.

This theory makes several predictions. First, since forming the apparatus of a state entails paying fixed costs, we should have more states when there is a positive income shock. Second, the form the state takes should be influenced by what is easiest to tax.

The Democratic Republic of Congo provides the ideal place to test this. Public order broke down, especially in the east. There are still many competing militias, of which the Nduma Defense of Congo-Renouvelé is a representative example. https://t.co/1oWEBkQAUN

There are two primary sources of economic activity — coltan and gold. While both of these are sources of mineral wealth, they imply very different things for the organization of the state. Coltan, which is hard to conceal, necessitates taxing production.

Armed militias find it cheapest to tax by requiring the purchase of licenses by miners. Gold, which is easy to conceal, necessitates taxing the consumption of the people. This implies very different things for state capacity — to legitimately impose consumption taxes
The militias have to provide public goods, like law and order. Not so for production taxes. There, the state extracts surplus, but without providing anything in return.

Prices for coltan surged with the introduction of the Playstation II (and flopped when it failed); gold surged after the Great Recession. In both cases, state formation greatly increased, but only in the areas endowed with those minerals. https://t.co/4lqI3j8lwA

The good news is, it seems that state formation is beneficial to locals. They have a positive opinion of the militias, economic indicators like weddings and immigrants are positive, and local militias are vastly less violent than external forces.
It is the external forces who, lacking a tie to the people, engage in arbitrary killings and theft. https://t.co/6YZjsen2mz

I am still agog at the fact that this paper involved 18 years of data collection, spanning hundreds of villages, in the midst of civil war. It is really somewhat magical 
https://t.co/zv22FB3fiz

papers.entry_text:
Bulow and Klemperer, “Auctions Versus Negotiations” (1996)
https://t.co/YJKdfAG6Ml

detailed_thread.text: Suppose we want to sell an item. Is it better to be able to extract as much rent as we can, or to have more people competing for it? It turns out that it is always strictly better to have another bidder, than to have full market power. 1/ https://t.co/DkOpDAnIfB

The standard auction here is a second price auction. Everyone submits a bid, and the highest bidder wins and pays the second highest price. This makes it so it is only optimal to bid your true valuation of the item.
We suppose that all bidders are identical and have a value drawn [0, 1]. Thus, with two bidders, the expected value is (N-1)/(n+1), or 1/3rd.

The optimal mechanism for a monopolist to extract surplus is to give a take-it-or-leave-it offer at the middle of the expected distribution. With one bidder, this is .5, which will be taken half the time for an expected value of .25.

(The expected value is x(1-x), or -x^2 + x. Taking the derivative, we have -2x + 1, which is the slope of marginal revenue. It is maximized when it hits zero, so x=.5.) When we have multiple bidders, the mechanism takes the form of the owner submitting a “bid” at .5.

This is a reserve price, which they will not sell below. 

It is apparent that, in the case of N and N+1 bidders, when N is one, N+1 generates more revenue. This will hold for all values of N.

Why? Since in the case of N bidders, the seller is submitting a bid too, it’s as if they have N bidders, except that some of the time the seller “wins” and no sale takes place. If the seller could replace themselves with a real bidder, they would.
This is a very hopeful paper. Instead of trying to extract all the revenue they can in a socially destructive way, we’re all better off for simply increasing the size of the market.

Bulow and Klemperer, “Auctions versus Negotiations” (1996)

https://t.co/K9LeG6HZgT

papers.entry_text:
Hall and Jones, “The Value of Life and the Rise in Health Spending” (2007) 
https://t.co/IdGrEZUFnu

detailed_thread.text: We spend more and more of our income on healthcare, which has risen from 5% of GDP in 1950 to 17.6% today. But is this really a bad thing? Rather than a symptom of an inefficient system, it follows simply from living longer not having declining marginal utility! 1/ https://t.co/cFHFPLv1cx

Assume we care both about how long we live, and how much we consume in those years. Packing more and more consumption into a year has declining returns, but being around another year has no such decline. https://t.co/qDRe6PL0iQ
Many people think that the high medical expenses of the US is due to waste, but this just isn’t true. We just consume way more than other countries — we do more procedures, more tests, more drugs.
And this is a perfectly efficient response to reasonable statistical values of a human life! https://t.co/RE7ipc5Inu

papers.entry_text:
Bell, Chetty, Jaravel, Petkova, Van Reenen, “Who Becomes an Inventor in America” (2019)

https://t.co/ORPkEqOUgu

detailed_thread.text: Who becomes an inventor? Three main factors dictate patenting rates: income, intelligence, and exposure to innovation. People are greatly influenced by the environment in which they are brought up, and that really matters. Let’s take a comprehensive look! 1/ https://t.co/dRpaOqBLuN

The authors are able to answer these questions because they have access to data no one else has — they have complete patent records, which are then tied to tax records, which are tied to early life math scores (New York City only).
First, parental income strongly predicts eventual patenting rates. The social value of patents, as measured by citations, is also strongly skewed as well. https://t.co/d2a9olT9gP

This is in part due to differences in intelligence, but not entirely. Adjusting for math scores removes a substantial part, but not close to all, of the gap between high and low-income individuals. https://t.co/XKcjk87FDq

The gap in intelligence increases over time, consistent with how the measured heritability of intelligence increases over time. This could be due to decreased noise, or due to gene-environment interactions, or due to richer kids learning more. https://t.co/fz5f0OD7q3

The third influence, though, is childhood exposure to innovation. If your parents have a patent in a narrowly defined class of technology — say, amplifiers as opposed to antennas — you are vastly more likely to patent in that class. https://t.co/pVPM9f7iwZ

Not only that, but the area you live in has a large effect on the narrowly defined technology class. This effect is gender specific — girls are affected only by women inventors, not males, and vice versa. https://t.co/922WbgkBCY

Obviously, moves aren’t random. The empirical strategy is that, when parents have multiple kids, the length of exposure to the new environment is determined by the age when they moved. Thus, you can compare only within the family. Moving has *massive* effects on outcomes. https://t.co/LzTUThAYkZ

My takeaway is that culture really matters for innovation. People pursue what is high status. Rhetorically elevating the importance of inventing and working in science actually matters a lot. People invent in the specific tech classes because that’s what’s seen as expected.

Bell, Chetty, Jaravel, Petkova, Van Reenen “Who Becomes an Inventor in America?” (2019)
https://t.co/UG4lROp37V

papers.entry_text:
David Yanagizawa-Drott, “Propaganda and Conflict” (2014)
https://t.co/n4vtu0Jx3Y

detailed_thread.text: On April 7th, 1994, the plane carrying the Presidents of Rwanda and Burundi was shot down. 100 days later, half a million were dead. Why? How did the killings happen so fast, all around the country? This paper argues that radio propaganda was critical. https://t.co/aEXHZ60Cwr

First, though, some historical background. Rwanda and Burundi are hilly, densely populated countries, divided between Tutsis and Hutus. Rwanda was ruled by the majority Hutus, while Burundi was ruled by the Tutsi until 1993. The groups strongly distrusted each other.

In 1972, the Tutsi dominated Burundian government killed hundreds of thousands of Hutu. In 1990, the RPF (a Tutsi organization) attacked Rwanda from Uganda. Three years of war followed, concluded by a peace treaty. Many believed that there would be a power-sharing agreement.

When the President was assassinated, Hutu extremists sprung into action, and broadcast calls to kill all the Tutsi that they could find. Critical to this was the Radio-Television Libre des Mille Colines, which aired propaganda about Tutsi atrocities and the need for self-defense.

To see how important it was, David Yanazigawa-Drott exploits the reason why Rwanda is nicknamed the “Land of a Thousand Hills”. Radio can’t go through mountains, so idiosyncratic quirks of geography will leave similar places with and without signal. https://t.co/4c0oca8j92
One transmitter was placed on Mount Muha, the second tallest peak, and the other in Kigali, the capital. Some places far away could get a signal — others could not. https://t.co/4Wcqh5hTu5

The obvious concern is that it could be something correlated with radio access in general, not just the genocidal rhetoric of RTLM. His robustness check of choice is to see if the other radio station, Radio Rwanda, had an effect. It did not. https://t.co/aBBBdlDBu7
It increased violence by convincing local elites that if they killed their neighbors, they wouldn’t be prosecuted. Now is the time to kill them and steal their possessions. https://t.co/hJxiy4dcQS
The killers were clear that what motivated them was piling up stacks of sheet metal, to put on one’s own roof. https://t.co/OVQSZUoQtC

Since the killings were started by local toughs who could go from place to place, organizing others, there were spillovers to violence. Have more places near by with signal made violence more likely in yours. https://t.co/0z1hJfkCZG

What it suggests, most tragically, is that the US could have stopped it. The killings happened in the belief that they could get away with it, not really for ideological reasons. If people thought justice would come, there would have been far fewer deaths.

In Haiti today, we are permitting a country to descend into civil war because we don’t want the optics of whites shooting at blacks. We should consider intervening more into the tragedies of our times. We don’t have to be bystanders again.

David Yanagizawa-Drott, “Propaganda and Conflict” (2014)
https://t.co/fjrYzRGKwK

papers.entry_text:
Hsieh and Klenow, “Misallocation and Manufacturing TFP in China and India” (2009)

https://t.co/3S6wREhp5H

detailed_thread.text: Why are poor countries so unproductive? One possibility is that resources are misallocated within an industry. Productive firms are kept from growing to their optimal size. Hsieh and Klenow (2009) is the framework in which we think about this. 1/ https://t.co/TQkznxFOzI

Assume an industry faces a Cobb-Douglas production function (which is to say, the share of income capital and labor get is invariant to size). Firms face “wedges” on their cost of inputs, which take the form of multiplying by (1+r) in the production function.

If r equals 0, then the marginal product of capital and labor should be equal. On the margin, adding one unit of either increases output by the same amount. If they weren’t, a firm could profit by changing how much of its inputs it uses. Misallocation is the dispersion of TFPR.

Their estimation method can only compare from country to country — they can only say things about reallocating to the level of the US, not getting rid of all misallocation. See section 4 for more details.

They argue that firms are essentially too small in India and China. Holding resources to an industry constant, output could be greatly increased by reallocating resources from plant to plant. https://t.co/KXZhkb6vdT

There are two big difficulties, which I have or will cover in detail later. First, we have to assume the same production function within an industry. Second, pure measurement error will overstate misallocation.
https://t.co/ZADDYzOPUr

They are not entirely insensitive to measurement error, as they check what happens when you lop off the tails of productivity. Nevertheless, it probably isn’t enough. Still, this paper has been profoundly influential in terms of methods, and you should know about it.

Hsieh and Klenow, “Misallocation and Manufacturing TFP in China and India” (2009)

https://t.co/9QN6RqVprZ

papers.entry_text:
Koh et al, “Labor Share Decline and Intellectual Property Products Capital” (2020)

https://t.co/3DhU8WBNJz

detailed_thread.text: Double-counting investment is not a trivial concern. The decline in labor’s share of income can be entirely explained by changes in how we treat intellectual property. Accounting changes can lead to wildly different policy conclusions, and we have got to get it right. https://t.co/xENwwUAj0H https://t.co/QxKMVwf46R

In 1999 and 2013, the Bureau of Economic Analysis (the BEA) revised its treatment of intellectual property, in particular software. No longer would it be treated as an intermediate input, but as a good into itself.

This was done not through a discontinuous jump, but by adjusting prior periods using the same methods. We can see that this was not neutral, though — the share of intellectual property in investment has been steadily increasing over time. https://t.co/1MU5neTINa
The BEA attributes payment for investment as a return on capital, thus reducing labor’s share of income. Adjusting for this entirely removes the downward trend. https://t.co/shUsKaMgvq

Nor is that all. Some software is obviously consumption, and not an intermediate good. Taking account of that is plausibly balanced out, though, by considering that many workers receive equity. A software engineer with stock options is plainly labor income. https://t.co/eD25IBzY5i

We have spent considerable time and effort explaining the decline in the labor share of income, and it’s been the root of some calls for redistribution. We just have to be careful that we think we know isn’t all a mirage.

papers.entry_text:
Bell, Chetty, Jaravel, Petkova, Van Reenen, “Do Tax Cuts Produce More Einsteins?” (2019)

https://t.co/Itth2qkcUL

detailed_thread.text: The upshot of the importance of exposure is that tax credits may not be as effective as we like in spurring innovation. Cutting taxes will spur more economic activity, but not necessarily innovation. https://t.co/bxcLtJMPKA https://t.co/t18WCheL8U
People choose their career first, and then choose to work more or less. Tax cuts won’t reach those who never chose to become inventors to begin with. https://t.co/2lucBYLZi6
The argument for tax cuts must be a more indirect one — the rewards to different careers change our culture, and it is that which is the most powerful cause of innovation.

Bell, Chetty, Jaravel, Petkova, Van Reenen, “Do Tax Cuts Produce More Einsteins?” (2019)

https://t.co/jTJZykQDu5

papers.entry_text:
Akcigit, Ates, Impullitti, “Innovation and Trade Policy in a Globalized World” (2021)

https://t.co/jRWBANZHPJ

detailed_thread.text: I think that there is something special about competition. Beyond just moving price to marginal cost, I believe it also leads to firms being simply better run and finding new ideas. This paper provides a model by which this matters. https://t.co/129rhwVmzp

They build on an Aghion-Howitt model of creative destruction. There are two firms, one in each country. They face a higher cost to export than to produce domestically. Whoever produces the better product, taking into account price, wins the entire market.

Thus, firms spend more on innovation when they are just below the threshold of winning the foreign market, or when their competitor is about to take their market. Falling trade costs mean that they are always on the edge of being overtaken, and so invest more in R&D.

In this world, the long-run optimal import tariff for a country is always 0. Because being overtaken in a foreign market is always bad for the domestic economy, R&D subsidies are justified, but these tradeoff against trade openness.
Akcigit, Ates, Impullitti, “Innovation and Trade Policy in a Globalized World” (2021)

papers.entry_text:
Peter Temin, “Price Behavior in Ancient Babylon” (2001)
https://t.co/pbcnM1lwyl

detailed_thread.text: Peter Temin, who passed away two days ago, wrote the only paper I have ever seen using a 2500 year old time series. The priests of Babylon recorded astronomical events along with the price of six goods. One can tie the clay tablets back to create a genuine price series! 1/ https://t.co/WFqxyG5ecX

He argues that they were indeed market prices, as they follow a random walk and the relative valuation varied. Further, political instability after the death of Alexander led to widespread inflation. https://t.co/YNWr8LLKte

I covered another of Temin’s papers here. He was a good economist. His life was a good one.

https://t.co/c0q75jBPkO

Peter Temin, “Price Behavior in Ancient Babylon” (2001)

https://t.co/k8c7SIMR4y

papers.entry_text:
Peter Temin, “Two Views of the British Industrial Revolution” (1997)
https://t.co/c0q75jBPkO
