detailed_thread.text: Peter Temin, who recently passed away, authored a number of great papers. “Two Views of the British Industrial Revolution” is one of them. It provides a crystal clear example of how theory and a formal model dispels confusion. https://t.co/wrQBbL9SeG https://t.co/1VtjmzLIer

The two views are the old view, which sees the Industrial Revolution as part of a general rise in productivity, and the new view, which thought only cotton and iron were truly rising in productivity. Temin’s insight is to show that these imply different things for trade. https://t.co/vofanePT5G

He writes down a model of Ricardian trade with many goods, as in Dornbusch-Fisher-Samuelson. In it, countries produce a continuum of goods numbered 1 through N, which allows you to treat them like a supply and demand curve, as below. https://t.co/uPuxP7x93S

A general rise in productivity is equivalent to a shift in A, and Britain exports goods it had previously never exported. A rise in some sectors is a shift in B. Essentially, we can satisfy the demand for traded goods using goods from fewer sectors.

Temin shows that Britain exported all kinds of things, and in fact surprisingly little of their exports were the things supposed to have risen in productivity so much. Clearly, the Industrial Revolution was a general rise in productivity. https://t.co/kAaLGK8U7q

Peter Temin, “Two Views of the British Industrial Revolution” (1997)

https://t.co/BwKbPMxoo8

papers.entry_text:
Foster, Haltiwanger, Syverson, “Reallocation, Firm Turnover, and Efficiency” (2008)
https://t.co/HdlbGUeGdY

detailed_thread.text: Reallocation — the closing of unproductive plants, the opening of more productive ones — is an important channel for growth. There’s just one problem with most of our measures: we measure revenue, not productivity. This confounds market power with efficiency! 1/ https://t.co/zXYnJdWUvZ

Foster, Haltiwanger, and Syverson have detailed data on homogeneous products, such as concrete, plywood, coffee beans and gasoline, including both quantities and prices. Typically you would have to assume the average price for an industry holds for every firm.

Once you do this, you find that younger businesses are actually more productive than they seem. They simply charge lower prices, and thus are more likely to exit than equally productive firms. https://t.co/Jf44d9Qg4f

It is unclear whether or not this is efficient. A reason for inefficiency would be if we’re in a Klette-Kortum type world. As time goes on, incumbent firms marginally innovate, and can charge higher markups. New firms don’t yet have time to build up their advantages.

However, it could also firms are reducing transactions costs, and have webs of supplier relationships. Even in data as rich as this, we can’t observe those costs. I think the latter is more likely — sources are unanimous on these being goods where value added is not possible.

We are left unsure over what this implies for policy. What is clear, however, is that using revenue measures to estimate changes in markups over time will be extremely vulnerable to unmeasured “relationship capital”. https://t.co/8zm7P50BRu

Foster, Haltiwanger, Syverson, “Reallocation, Firm Turnover, and Efficiency” (2008)
https://t.co/nPyyaGIPlR

papers.entry_text:
Colas and Sachs, “Indirect Fiscal Benefits of Low-Skilled Immigration” (2021)

https://t.co/tabT2j5tuT

detailed_thread.text: People claim that immigration will depress the wages of low-skill workers. People also claim that immigrants are net fiscal negatives. What nobody seems to have noticed is that these are contradictory! 1/ https://t.co/nabI73vWIq

You don’t need a complicated model or difficult to identify assumptions either. Suppose there are two types of workers, high and low skill. Both are complementary to each other. High skill workers face a higher marginal tax rate than low-skilled workers. https://t.co/y18WQDRLZa

As you get more of one, the marginal product declines (just the same as how marginal product of capital or labor would decline in a standard production function). This is completely offset by the gain of the other group. Thus, immigration pushes more income to higher taxes.

The fiscal impact is sizeable, and flips immigrants to being net positives under many calculations. Further, as we add more realistic features to the model, these tend to increase, not decrease, the fiscal benefits of immigrants. https://t.co/d64FTQJcF7
So when you see charts like these, take caution. They are only including the direct effects, and not taking into account general equilibrium changes. https://t.co/104Njzq4TC

Colas and Sachs, “Indirect Fiscal Benefits of Low-Skilled Immigration” (2021)

https://t.co/H2caqxSrgN

papers.entry_text:
Roger Myerson, “Optimal Auction Design” (1981)
https://t.co/d0tRhZo6YQ

detailed_thread.text: How should a seller structure an auction to maximize their surplus? Myerson (1981) is the seminal paper in mechanism design, and answers this and much more. 1/ https://t.co/lQ4zY5sjAX

If the seller knew exactly what everyone was willing to pay, they would simply sell to the person with the highest valuation at that price. Let’s suppose they don’t know — instead, all they have is a distribution of possible values. https://t.co/YtMtcSFer5

We assume that these valuations are independent of others, and that everyone is aware of the distributions. For simplicity, the seller places a value on the item of zero, but this could be any arbitrary value. https://t.co/JNyjLq7Av8

Let’s constrain our space of mechanisms by imposing three constraints, the important ones being individual rationality (no-one can expect to lose money) and incentive compatibility (we only consider mechanisms in which people reveal their true valuation). https://t.co/OW1cmlSahP

The first important result: we do not lose anything by only considering incentive compatible mechanisms. This works because you can always calculate how people can strategize, so there exists an equivalent incentive compatible mechanism. https://t.co/BnkDhnJI69
Result number two: if a mechanism gets the same item to the same people with the same probability, the revenue is equivalent. https://t.co/PODe1tASKr

Remember first price and second price auctions from earlier tweet threads? Turns out, these are identical with independent valuations. People hedge their bids just enough to exactly equal the expected revenue of a second price auction.

So, with all this in mind, the optimal auction takes the form of a Vickrey (second-price) auction, with a reserve price at where the derivative of marginal revenue equals 0, so long as bidders are identical. https://t.co/fpYRkdXPSg

If they are not, then the seller sequentially offers the good at the reserve price of each buyer’s distribution until the good is bought. Suppose someone has a value between 20 and 30, and another between 0 and 10. The seller offers it first at 25, and then at 5.
This means the good is sometimes sold to someone who will always value it less. The optimal mechanism is systematically inefficient for society. https://t.co/0d0klz7tQF

Roger Myerson, “Optimal Auction Design” (1981)
https://t.co/YPKpdDYcnh

papers.entry_text:
William Vickrey, “Counterspeculation, Auctions, and Competitive Sealed Tenders” (1961)

https://t.co/J36cWtlCQb

detailed_thread.text: How shall we allocate resources? In order to optimally allocate items amongst people, we need some way of getting a truthful report of people’s preferences. Vickrey (1961) shows this to be simple: give the item to the highest bidder, at the price of the second highest bid! 1/ https://t.co/QjxN2CTefW

Vickrey approaches this almost seemingly by accident, in discussing an old proposal of Abba Lerner. Suppose there is an imperfectly competitive market. The government can, by tax and subsidy, equate marginal cost and benefit. https://t.co/xu5dBOs28A

However, the government won’t know how expensive anything really is. If they ask people, they will misreport in ways that benefit them. The government must make the reports “incentive-compatible”, or having no way to gain from lying. https://t.co/IyhLvth3lg

An English auction is one where the price rises until only one person is left, and can be contrasted with a Dutch auction, where the price falls until one person steps forward to buy the good. The former is incentive-compatible, while the latter is not. https://t.co/sx37iB4rVp

In the Dutch auction, you are incentivized to underreport your valuation. You gain by buying lower than you are actually willing to buy for. In the English, ascending auction, however, you do not have to raise your bid after someone drops out. You go until price exceeds value.

We can do this by way of bids, too. The Dutch auction is isomorphic to a first-price auction, where high bidder pays their bidded price. The English auction is isomorphic to a second-price auction, where the highest bidder pays the second highest bid.

We will discuss the proof of this in another thread, as it came 20 years later, but the second-price auction has the same revenue, but requires only one round and no knowledge of opponents’ strategies.

Why is it incentive compatible? Suppose you bid high. It either: does not change anything; or leads you to buy it when it wasn’t actually worthwhile to you. Suppose you bid low. Again, it either changes nothing, or you don’t get it when it would have been worthwhile.
Your bid only affects the price someone else pays, or whether you win at all.

A third price auction doesn’t work because you are now incentivized to bid a bit higher than usual. Also, if a second-price auction is Pareto-optimal, you can hardly improve upon it. https://t.co/liRPLoVirF
Vickrey also anticipates Myerson-Satterthwaite (1983) here. A mechanism that makes all mutually beneficial trades happen does not have a balanced budget. https://t.co/gHcdtxpWhk

William Vickrey, “Counterspeculation, Auctions, and Competitive Sealed Tenders” (1961)

https://t.co/2nmbdsM7QY

papers.entry_text:
Ronald Coase, “Durability and Monopoly” (1972)

https://t.co/5s6LJdmKlT

detailed_thread.text: Suppose a monopolist produce a perfectly durable good. What price will it be sold for? Ronald Coase shows, in a short little paper which (unusually) cites no sources, it will be sold for the competitive price! The monopolist is in competition with his future self. 1/ https://t.co/w0OvYpMD2l

Suppose there is some good, like all the land in US, which has a marginal cost of zero. The monopolist will not sell all of it — rather, they restrict the quantity to M in order to sell at price A, as below. https://t.co/ifw561ypFX
Having done this, though, the monopolist would notice that he has units left over. Why not sell some more, at a lower price now? And so down he goes until eventually all the land is sold.

The trouble is that everyone knows this. If the monopolist sells at a high price in the first round, everyone will wait to buy in the next round. Unless the monopolist can commit himself, he will be unable to extract any profit at all!
This holds even if the good is produced. A higher marginal cost does not disturb the logic, but simply changes the quantities that will be sold, in the end. https://t.co/XFGLUe5k6N
There has been later work on this. (I borrow this from Kevin Bryan’s “A Fine Theorem”.) https://t.co/2hbMF0V0ZN

Note that there are two possibilities, depending on the discount rate of the seller and the buyers. If the seller is more patient than the buyers, the result reverses, and the seller extracts all possible value.

Ronald Coase, “Durability and Monopoly” (1972)

https://t.co/26Vyx1Qqgx

papers.entry_text:
Alan Deardorff, “Welfare Effects of Global Patent Protection” (1992)

https://t.co/ZM1L18dM8m

detailed_thread.text: Should we exempt developing countries from patent laws? With Trump occasionally fulminating about making the rest of the world pay our prices, it’s worth remembering what that would mean — lower consumer welfare around the globe! https://t.co/leCr7Tvoss
The reasoning is fairly simple. Suppose there are two countries, which for convenience we shall say are identical and have linear demand for inventions. A has patent protection, while B does not.

Extending patent protection increases the supply of inventions which A gets. However, these inventions are the ones which are the most marginally useful. Thus, B loses by even more. They have to give patent protection to all drugs, is the key. https://t.co/5EMpUMbcVC
Practically speaking, poor nations evading patent protections likely increases global welfare, especially since their demand is so small compared to ours.

Alan Deardorff, “The Welfare Effects of Global Patent Protection” (1992)

https://t.co/c0y7ngzTmF

papers.entry_text:
Ichino and Moretti, “Biological Gender Differences, Absenteeism, and the Earnings Gap” (2009)

https://t.co/PJxdpSoc4y

detailed_thread.text: Menstruation sucks. It sucks so much it’s plausibly responsible for 14% of the gender wage gap. Women below 45 are much more likely to be absent around 28 days after their last absence than men are, and this leads to differences in promotion and productivity. 1/ https://t.co/5ulCshm6s8
Some background first. Women miss a lot more days of work than men do, even among the childless. Why? https://t.co/WhUpEXJng1

We can isolate the menstrual cycle from this. The gap in absences spikes 28 days after the last one, and we can also see that men are much more likely to have multi-day absences. (Suggesting severe illness as a cause). https://t.co/isPSCkLis7

Of course, we can do better. Here we compare all pairs of absences, so that menstruation-illness-menstruation is not picked up misleadingly, and we break down by age. Women are wayyy more likely to have an absence around 28 days after a prior one. https://t.co/YfN9jlOWBw
There’s a couple mechanisms of how this affects wages. More absences make it harder to learn about underlying conscientiousness, and it also makes workers directly productive. https://t.co/bzOgmB5u85

Ichino and Moretti construct a simple model to match the facts, and argue that menstruation is responsible for 14% of the gender gap, with the direct productivity effect explaining a third, and signaling explaining the rest. https://t.co/No3tzeiwtI
One wonders how much of the effect of birth control on women’s participation in the work force was actually preventing menstruation.

Ichino and Moretti, “Biological Gender Differences, Absenteeism, and the Earnings Gap” (2009)

https://t.co/5sckApLUW5

papers.entry_text:
Roger Myerson, “Incentive Compatibility and the Bargaining Problem” (1979)

https://t.co/S7xg5SPTII

detailed_thread.text: Myerson (1979) is a paper which made me jump out of bed and pace around in excitement when I first read it. Its conclusion is so unexpected, yet works. Imagine that there are two players bargaining over a bridge. If they can’t use force, how close to the optimum can we get? 1/ https://t.co/5COl7EZvny

Simplify the possibilities greatly. The cost is $100. Player A always values at $90. Player B values it at $90 90% of the time, and $30 10% of the time. Obviously, the bridge should always be built, but can this be done voluntarily? https://t.co/cqPIrGAqIS

Crucially, the players know their own type, and cannot commit to a rule to split the cost so that on average the gain is equal. This rules out splitting it $53-47, as 10% of the time Player B would reject the deal.

Myerson’s insight is that every constraint on the problem — that it be individually rational, incentive compatible, and Pareto efficient — can be translated into a system of linear equations, which are then solved to spit out the optimal mechanism. https://t.co/MfmF9q445o
I’ll give you a moment to work out what you think the optimal solution is.
Take another second. Consider it.
Maybe even consider it longer.

Well, so here’s the solution. We ask player B what their value is. If they say $90, we split it 50.5 to 49.5. If they say $30, player A pays the entire cost — but the project is only built 43.9% of the time. Without force, it is impossible to do better. https://t.co/ImPHcCeaeF

Roger Myerson, “Incentive Compatibility and the Bargaining Problem” (1979) 

https://t.co/LVczpBvBGp

papers.entry_text:
Corno, Hildebrandt, Voena, “Age of Marriage, Weather Shocks, and the Direction of Marriage Payments” (2020)
https://t.co/VuNDOO5z6Y

detailed_thread.text: Marriage, in much of the world, is an economic decision. It is arranged between families, and accompanied by substantial financial transfers. The direction of the transfers has big implications when negative shocks come — dowries delay marriage, and bride price brings it forward. https://t.co/M3ZrdGXeGN

The underlying logic is this — while both sets of families are affected by negative shocks, the family of the son will continue to benefit from having them around longer. Thus, the bride’s family is more sensitive to shocks. https://t.co/RutrSYJnxm

Dowry is ubiquitous in India, while bride-price is more common in Africa. Why? The received wisdom, starting from Becker (1991), is that marriage prices clear the market. Boserup (1970) argues that women are better suited for hoe agriculture than plow agriculture.

Even today, places with plow agriculture (which places a high premium on pure strength) have more patriarchal gender norms. In this world, dowries exist to encourage someone to take on a wife. Where women are valuable, families pay more for them. https://t.co/zIjPWUdA26

In the different places, droughts have different effects. Bad weather increases child marriage in Africa, but not in India. This translates into fewer teen pregnancies, and presumably, better life outcomes. https://t.co/OvGNlNIgrG

Historically, age of marriage was the only birth control really available. You simply have less time to have kids. The European Marriage Pattern was plausibly responsible for takeoff into industrialization, by allowing for the accumulation of skills over time.
For details on modeling this, see Galor and Weil (2000). https://t.co/yXCKUfDKy2

Dowries vs bride-price, at least in the modern age, is not entirely due to current economic conditions. The south of India adopted dowries only recently, due to largely to the cultural influence of Hinduism. Culture matters a lot! https://t.co/MDgPkXRCMN

Corno, Hildebrandt, Voena, “Age of Marriage, Weather Shocks, and the Direction of Marriage Payments” (2020)
https://t.co/FxtWSb8Cxg

papers.entry_text:
Sam Altmann, “Choice by Design” (2025)

https://t.co/d7gLVhDJTG

detailed_thread.text: Why do I support capitalism? It is easiest to show by example. Feeding America is a network of food banks. In 2005, they switched from central planning to a price system. Sam Altmann (not that one) finds that this is equivalent to increasing donations by 32.7%. 1/ https://t.co/IbQDG4Batb https://t.co/bEv8oHXZc9

The previous system worked like this: all the food banks would be in a queue. Whenever Feeding America got a donation of a truckload of food, they would go to the first bank in line. They would then have 4-6 hours to accept or decline the shipment.

Whether they accept or decline, they would then be shuffled back depending on whether their total poundage target had been met (which was based on local poverty). There are some serious problems. Food is of heterogenous quality, and different banks need different items.
Places in Idaho don’t need any more potatoes, but that is what they might be offered. Food would also spoil as they figured out who would end up taking it.

So, in 2005, they switched to an auction system. There were two auctions a day, and all truckloads would be “sold” with a fake currency. Now, everyone knows everything that’s available in a day. Food banks can plan, and get the goods they actually need.

The way Sam Altmann estimates the welfare effects is a bit knotty; but to try and express it intuitively, he can see how bids vary based on what food banks have won in the past. Thus, he can extract the cost of storage, and the values they place on combinations of goods.
The gains under the new system are the equivalent of 105 more tons of food a day. https://t.co/3T73merOmN

Wastage is down, transportation costs are down, storage cost per ton is down. 88% of food banks are better off, and the ones which aren’t are in wealthy areas and previously took everything others didn’t. https://t.co/FvOJfy4JXu
This is why capitalism matters. Prices are how we allocate goods in an incentive compatible way. A benevolent social planner choosing a mechanism would simply choose this!

Sam Altmann, “Choice by Design” (2025)

https://t.co/iJNkmv50V4

And you can read about this, and much more, on my blog. 

https://t.co/iIc54NGjNw

papers.entry_text:
Alesina and Tabellini, “A Positive Theory of Fiscal Deficits and Government Debt” (1990)
https://t.co/6WXEaXGByK

detailed_thread.text: A problem with government debt is that it binds the hands of future generations. If parties disagree on how the money should be spent, they can leave a debt to future governments to force them to behave more in line with their preferences. The more polarization, the more debt. 1/ https://t.co/EK6dOyaT5P

Take as a starting point a benevolent social planner, who has a chance of 1 to be re-elected, and spends according to a weighted average of preferences. They leave no debt, because they want to smooth consumption over time. (They spend the same in each period). https://t.co/W2mxYFjxlB
What happens if parties can change? Then the size of the debt is an increasing function of the likelihood the incumbent loses. https://t.co/1q2mE7jlSk
This results in a positive debt over time. The more they disagree, the bigger the debt. https://t.co/WCW66CRd8d

An implication of this is that “thermostatic” voting will make government behavior worse. If every party knows they will be kicked out no matter what, they will rush to implement their policies, even if it’s sub-optimal.
It also goes a long way toward explaining the destructive, deficit expanding behavior of both parties in America today.

Alesina and Tabellini, “A Positive Theory of Fiscal Deficits and Government Debt” (1990)

https://t.co/wr0JgFmlVA

papers.entry_text:
Hsieh, Hurst, Jones, Klenow, “The Allocation of Talent and U.S. Economic Growth” (2019)
https://t.co/cRebXCYAoI

detailed_thread.text: America is far less discriminatory against women and Blacks now than it was in the past. If people are now more able to pursue their comparative advantage, how much richer are we as a result? HHJK estimate that around a third of US growth is due declining discrimination! 1/ https://t.co/EWHJifjHxp

What I find really interesting about it is what happens when we decompose by cause. People could not go into a high skilled profession because they are discriminated against there, but they could also not go because they were unable to accumulate the skills.
You can differentiate the two by comparing the gap for all people in a given point in time, versus within a given cohort of people. https://t.co/0z5Gu8ydO0
Barriers to human capital accumulation explains much more than labor market discrimination does. https://t.co/Vd2vnwSO7a

A subtle point this paper makes is that wages are affected by selection. Suppose, due to discrimination, only the most talented workers go do a job. In this world, less discrimination can coincide with a bigger wage gap in the job, because now lower skill workers can enter. https://t.co/LGVBPZDp8A

Before the race and IQ folks jump on it, we do not need an assumption that the talent pools people draw from are identical. Rather, all we need is that the difference in innate talent is constant over time.

Hsieh, Hurst, Jones, Klenow, “The Allocation of Talent and U.S. Economic Growth” (2019)

https://t.co/NaX5joJiDC

papers.entry_text:
Francis Galton, “The Most Suitable Proportion Between the Value of First and Second Prizes” (1902)
https://t.co/BgBLvLGyvS

detailed_thread.text: Suppose we have a sum for two prizes. The success of competitors for this prize is drawn from a normal distribution. It seems natural that the ratio of the prizes will match the ratio of now much they exceed the third place person by — Galton shows that this ratio is 3:1! 1/ https://t.co/3EOgAQgo9c

The form of the problem somewhat rules out questions of how to motivate effort, but it does seem intuitively fair that a prize should match the degree to which an achievement exceeds the others. https://t.co/5SEcD4dPUG

Galton’s explanation is a bit knotty, but basically, given a normal distribution, each person will divide up the *area* under the curve equally. So, with ten people, we divide the curve into ten regions of equal area, and place each person at the midpoint. https://t.co/SqxQGFdug3

The real N in any contest is somewhat unknown, of course, as in many cases people don’t enter when they know they’ll be beat. So, what happens when we try out many values of N? https://t.co/ZpVyNyu4UZ

To Galton’s surprise, the ratio converges rapidly to three to one. The best competitor will be three times better than the second place competitor, than the second was to the third. https://t.co/yZe6w6Ef0z

Galton then goes out, and shows that this pattern holds up in data from Civil Service exams, although he fears this is only by coincidence — the exam marks not being normally distributed. (It was here I knew — Galton would be an economist today). https://t.co/nbwyYMA5Sg

He gamely confesses that he is no mathematician, and leaves it to Karl Pearson to prove why this works. Pearson has a note taking up the latter half of the paper proving it — I skip it, as it is just laboriously solving integrals. https://t.co/2oN8iyUaMT
Pearson’s comments are worth reading, however. The tails are weird. The best of the best will be really far apart from the rest. https://t.co/m4TSCRM2I0

Francis Galton, “The Most Suitable Proportion Between the Value of First and Second Prizes” (1902)

https://t.co/b5bureljqm
this seems right up your alley @Afinetheorem

papers.entry_text:
Giommoni, Guiso, Michellaci, Morelli, “The Economic Costs of Ambiguous Laws” (2025)
https://t.co/jNmVQbv3gm

detailed_thread.text: How costly are poorly written laws? A team of Italian researchers argue that the drafting of Italy’s laws alone costs them 5% of GDP — or $120 billion a year! 1/ https://t.co/v441biVThZ

Readers will note that the structure of the paper is rather similar to Gentzkow and Shapiro (2010), who studied media bias. In both cases, the first challenge is you need a treatment variable with cardinality.

If you enjoyed this, please consider subscribing to my blog. It’s basically this, but even more expansive. 
https://t.co/ep27yo3Cnc

papers.entry_text:
Zoë Cullen, “Is Pay Transparency Good?” (2024)

https://t.co/o1K8RmJqVl

detailed_thread.text: Pay transparency is supposed to reduced the gender gap in wages, which it does — but only by reducing wages for everyone! This says a lot about why wages for women are lower, too: it’s not discrimination. https://t.co/pSvKku6uIf

The immediately obvious way that transparency affects wages is through revealing discrimination. If an employer is favoring males, then just as productive females can use that knowledge to push for higher wages. https://t.co/ebjK4zXDEo

On the other hand, pay transparency means that an employer cannot offer higher wages to someone without needing to give it to others. Knowing this, the employer cannot offer higher commit to not paying higher wages, increasing their bargaining power. https://t.co/9EPQ0LELb5

If wage gaps are due to differences in productivity, we’d expect the latter to prevail. If it is purely due to taste-based discrimination, the former seems more likely to prevail. (The mental model isn’t quite pinned down here, tbf — if discrimination takes the form…
of paying males more than their marginal product, then pressure for equal treatment would plausibly lead to falling wages as well. We’d need to be very specific about tastes).
Nevertheless, this should caution us against revealing prices. There’s no reason to think that this consistently improves consumer or employee power!

Zoë Cullen, “Is Pay Transparency Good?” (2024)

https://t.co/t1raUUhxKp

papers.entry_text:
Devesh Raval, “Testing the Production Approach to Markup Estimation” (2023)
https://t.co/htfeRORGGI

detailed_thread.text: The “production approach” is a powerful and flexible tool for estimating misallocation in the economy. The only problem with it is that it’s totally wrong all the time. Here, it is demonstrated by comparing results across different inputs. 1/ https://t.co/lFOlZfCkBf

The production approach states that markups are equal to the ratio of the elasticity of output with respect to some variable input, divided by the elasticity of revenue to that input. In other words, if they increase an input by 15%, but only pay it 10% more, the markup = 1.5.

Labor is the most commonly used variable input, but it’s not the only one. We could use energy, materials, or any such thing that can be costlessly adjusted. (If it can’t be costlessly adjusted, our markup measure will include the cost of changing the inout).

The choice of input shouldn’t matter — the ratio of the estimated markups should be close to 1. Yet, it isn’t, and in fact they go the opposite direction over time, when he tests with data from five different countries which have excellent data on *all* firms. https://t.co/1jeHoAtqqz
The distributions of company levels estimates are just wildly different from each other. https://t.co/wzI4at9chv

All of these lines should be the same! There should be no difference between them, if you’re actually measuring anything meaningful. Instead, they move in the exact opposite way. https://t.co/TaNWKsRkio

To Raval, the best explanation is that productivity is not neutral between factors, a key assumption in the literature. Instead, some productivity affects labor more than energy or some other input. In all, a really sharp paper.

Devesh Raval, “Testing the Production Approach to Markup Estimation” (2023)
https://t.co/q7RZnRAmWA

papers.entry_text:
Tishara Garg, “Can Industrial Policy Overcome Coordination Failures?” (2025)

https://t.co/AyTbkQjt8C

detailed_thread.text: This is the job market paper of the year, and the best paper on industrial policy I have ever seen. Industrial policy can affect outcomes either directly by changing an area’s fundamentals, or by coordinating simultaneous investment. How important is each? Let’s find out. 1/ https://t.co/oMQ3zdgJcH

Tishara Garg is studying the effects of industrial parks in India. These are plots of land set aside for industrialization, with substantial government promotion. These are not, to be clear, special economic zones like Shenzhen, but simply local initiatives. https://t.co/yfiJBQ1mTA

The perennial problem facing people studying IP is that reduced form evidence is meaningless. Yes, it increases local employment. That says nothing about whether that simply redirected — or parasitized — activity that would have simply happened elsewhere. https://t.co/Itv9Las7jm

A program of subsidies, with its attendant deadweight loss from taxes, need not improve welfare. However, we can say with more confidence that coordinating economic activity is beneficial to everyone, if there are investments which are worthwhile only if others also invest.

To do this, we need to estimate the possible equilibriums given a set of fundamentals. The first challenge is estimating those fundamentals. What she shows is that, holding the rest of the economy constant, each variable gives us a function. https://t.co/yelrGGuhjU
If this is the case, then we can estimate the fundamentals of every area using a set of instrumental variables. https://t.co/uA78ScrZdu
This is not as complicated as it sounds. We can simply use spillovers from investments in another place to infer the fundamentals of the place we’re studying. https://t.co/uPMor1ZpMi

These spillovers are quite local in nature. Thus, so long as there are two assumptions met (no difference in trends, no subsidization of control areas by treated areas) we can use these as instruments. https://t.co/LQYnA2ApT5

Second, we need to enumerate all possible equilibriums, and (third) assign them into types. Her contribution are methods from algebraic geometry — she can approximate systems of linear equations as the roots of polynomials, which have well-established methods for solving. https://t.co/h2vLl2uZgU

With this in hand, we can finally separate out what is unambiguously good about industrial policies. The formation of industrial parks leads to places changing their equilibriums to higher, better ones. https://t.co/WGGqE5UaFU
About a third of the increase in industrialization in and around the area is due to switching equilibriums, and not due to any direct subsidy. https://t.co/lVJh6XIRqz
Her methods are the main contribution, though. This is a paper you should know of, and be able to consider applying to whatever question you are solving. https://t.co/6T3n50Gotb

Tishara Garg, “Can Industrial Policy Overcome Coordination Failures?” (2025)

https://t.co/6W2juFUKpM
This is a paper you should know of @Noahpinion @mattyglesias @tylercowen @ATabarrok @Afinetheorem @heeney_luke @JustinWolfers @jasonfurman @nominalthoughts

papers.entry_text:
Lowes and Montero, “The Legacy of Colonial Medicine in Central Africa” (2021)

https://t.co/H6P55MnCA7

detailed_thread.text: Distrust of medical authorities is common in Africa. One possible reason is the legacy of colonialism. Places which saw more public health campaigns under the French are today more likely to not cooperate with public health initiatives. 1/ https://t.co/GVW5EX89lq

The French controlled a substantial swathe of Sub-Saharan Africa. Our study focuses on Cameroon, which is the country with the dark red spot in the middle. Colonial authorities repeatedly embarked on public health campaigns to control the spread of sleeping sickness. https://t.co/VjMCa4JRdE
Sleeping sickness is a serious disease. It is a good thing to control. Nevertheless, the actions of the French were not that effective — and being in a village treated by them just plain sucked.
The primary medicine for treating sleeping sickness did so only by killing the parasites just a little bit faster than it killed you. https://t.co/rDNfRO4xZY
Even if you didn’t have the disease, everyone got prophylactic treatment. This, however, did not have much preventative effect, and was extremely unpleasant to take. https://t.co/BNX3URgy87
The all-around shabby treatment passed into folk memory, with songs being written about it. https://t.co/d16C9ksZSh

It is not sufficient simply to note that treatment and distrust are correlated. We need some way of generating exogenous variation in the intensity of the treatment. Lowes and Montero use soil suitability for cassava, as opposed to millet. https://t.co/LkLf2DriEP

It is possible that the crops affect outcomes through some other channel, so they compare British and French Cameroon. They find soil suitability predicts distrust only in French Cameroon, and that France was the only one to conduct these campaigns. https://t.co/WEp8ZBNMHj

These are extremely large differences — 15 years of visitation doubles the rate of refusal to take a blood test. Assessing the success of World Bank programs, it is equivalent to moving the average project from “moderately satisfactory” to “moderately unsatisfactory”. https://t.co/NresXGTQLl

Lowes and Montero, “The Legacy of Colonial Medicine in Central Africa” (2021)

https://t.co/uiVUz14Vi4

papers.entry_text:
Glosten and Milgrom, “Bid, Ask, and Transaction Prices” (1985)

https://t.co/IslSjycQGY

detailed_thread.text: Why is insider trading bad? It’s simple enough — too many people trading on information leads to no market existing at all. Along the way, we can explain why there is a gap between bid-ask prices. 1/ https://t.co/WySsjjLk1h

Suppose there are two types of traders, insiders and outsiders. Outsiders don’t know much about the value of a stock. They just want to trade for liquidity reasons. Insiders, meanwhile, possess private information on what the value of a stock really is.

If a market maker offered to buy and sell at the same price, then every time an insider came along they would lose money. The insider would only trade if they knew more than you do. So, the market maker leaves a gap in price — a bid-ask spread.

The trouble is that a wider bid-ask spread makes the market less attractive to outsiders. When they pull their money, all that’s left are insiders, and the market maker knows that they will only trade when they can take the market maker’s money! https://t.co/vkJUHXMI8g
The way around this is to give the market maker enough in monopoly profits to offset the losses from insiders trading. https://t.co/OWb3BW29lr
And it also provides a rationale for outlawing insider trading. Yes, it communicates information to the market — but too much of it prevents markets from existing at all.

Glosten and Milgrom, “Bid, Ask, and Transaction Prices” (1985)

https://t.co/sWjPeTFe6p

papers.entry_text:
Walker, Shankar, Miguel, Egger, Killeen, “Can Cash Transfers Save Lives?” (2025)
https://t.co/TXuZiRRqP4

detailed_thread.text: Cash transfers reduce mortality, and they do so through allowing the purchase of more medical care. It is reassuring to find that the common sense story is completely accurate. Thread for some cool graphs. 1/ https://t.co/HgAfmodYAe

If you’ll recall, a very similar research team found that cash transfers have large positive spillovers onto other people in the same village. They count this as part of the treatment. https://t.co/KRfdb0W0bn
They specified their subgroup in the pre-analysis plan. https://t.co/PSW5vtSzy5

The main results are just barely insignificant. However, one should consider that the treated group was de facto means tested, and the control group includes higher income households. Comparing poor to poor, the mortality difference is quite large. https://t.co/ylldlC5E2m

These effects did not last. It is important to remember that. Marginal propensity to consume was extremely high, and it got eaten up fast. However, if the transfer occurred during the birth month, the child was wayyyyy more likely to survive. https://t.co/zuDLck8nPI
This is due to mothers choosing a hospital delivery. https://t.co/PRh9CFe37W
And not only did it save the baby — it also saved the mother. https://t.co/tklJkarMiy

Cash transfers work, insofar as they raise consumption. They will not permanently change income, but they will make life better. If you believe in redistribution, oughtn’t this be far more important than redistribution within a country?

papers.entry_text:
Brown, Montero, Schmidt-Padilla, Sviatschi, “Market Structure and Extortion” (2019)

https://t.co/yntzZYUIU9

detailed_thread.text: Gang truces reduce homicides — but at the same time, by reducing competition they increase the amount paid in bribes. You cannot buy off gangs. You must defeat them. 1/ https://t.co/VtkDzHBnLt

The data is from a trucking company, whose drivers are stopped in order to pay bribes. They record the amount of the bribe, when and where, and this can be compared to what they were carrying at the time. https://t.co/3k18EXzqO1

In 2016, the principal gang leaders announced a truce. They would divide up the country into districts, and stop fighting each other. This did greatly reduce the number of homicides, but only in the areas previously under competition. https://t.co/1zv0gcTD7b
Not having to defend their turf meant that bribes could go way up. The richer the area, the greater the increase. https://t.co/e1VhcU55w0

Brown, Montero, Schmidt-Padilla, Sviatschi, “Market Structure and Extortion” (2019)

https://t.co/tU1WuYrTxy

papers.entry_text:
Doraszelski and Jaumandreu, “Reexamining the DLW Method for Estimating Markups” (2021)

https://t.co/tP0sTt38Tn

detailed_thread.text: The De Loecker-Warzynski approach to markup estimation — later used as the basis for all claims that corporate power is increasing — is not robust to firms facing different demands. This is constantly violated, thus their estimation procedure is totally uninformative. 1/ https://t.co/oCWhgcPOQS

DLW are building on Olley-Pakes, who break the production function into fixed and variable inputs, and productivity which is observed by the firm but not by the researcher. We assume productivity follows a random walk, and fixed inputs come from past productivity. https://t.co/jeGURqg3K8

The firm will minimize costs, which means that the marginal value of a dollar spent on an input must be as high as it spent anywhere else. Since we can observe fixed inputs, we can recover productivity, and thus the production function. (A PF is how inputs combine to make output)
However, if firms face different demands, we cannot recover productivity unless we first control for the markup. The whole exercise is circular. https://t.co/KZropJZtlf

I’ve covered how, when we get super accurate information, firms plainly face wildly different demands even within narrowly defined industries.

https://t.co/HdlbGUe8oq

Doraszelski and Jaumandreu, “Reexamining the DLW Method for Estimating Markups” (2021)

https://t.co/gFC7ZRu83R

papers.entry_text:
Ermest Liu, “Industrial Policies in Production Networks” (2019)

https://t.co/b4Wfozdr1u

detailed_thread.text: A very hopeful paper for industrial policy. In a distorted economy, there’s no guarantee that correcting a distortion in any one sector raises total output. Liu is able to show that, in a network and under reasonable restrictions, targeting upstream sectors is always good. 1/ https://t.co/IbEwLAjjNE

So, a problem facing policymakers is that correcting a market failure by too much is a problem, and so too can be correcting one sector without correcting another. A market failure in one sector can be correcting a failure in another.

To give a simple example, having cheap fossil fuels leads to higher emissions, a negative externality on the world. That fossil fuels are made substantially more expensive by OPEC actually serves to correct this, and leads to better outcomes than the world without OPEC.

A lot of production occurs in networks. Inputs have multiple steps toward becoming a final product, and so distortions in different steps will not have the same weight. If they are hierarchical, then we want to correct the first steps, which need not even be the most distorted.
Imagine the following good. (The common factor might be labor). The distortion will be largest in 3, and smallest in 1. Yet, a policymaker will want to correct 1 first. https://t.co/5vvNFBDM51
He then goes on to show that this roughly describes how China and South Korea actually went about industrial policy. The intuition that heavy industry first is what matters is indeed the correct one.

Ernest Liu, “Industrial Policy in Production Networks” (2019)
https://t.co/zXVDoih5gO

papers.entry_text:
Sekhri and Storeygard, “Dowry Deaths” (2013)

https://t.co/jncsN4prOE

detailed_thread.text: Gendered violence, up to and including murder, is stunningly common in India. Much of it is economic in origin — during times of drought, the husband abuses the wife to coerce the wife’s family to pay more in dowry. Hundreds of women die this way each year. 1/ https://t.co/YgYMmxMv0j
I’m serious about the rate of death over dowries. Yes, India is a big country and lots of things will happen. It’s still really high! https://t.co/PHnrnoSOeV
Dowry deaths are 7.8% more likely when rainfall declines by one standard deviation.

What sways me to believe their story, and that it’s economic in origin, is that this pattern isn’t there in districts with many Muslims. Muslims in India have different customs (although confusingly, Muslims in Pakistan and Bangladesh almost all give dowries). https://t.co/ljjuwWkRKH

Sekhri and Storeygard, “Dowry Deaths” (2013)

https://t.co/dLDbqc53FC

papers.entry_text:
Olley and Pakes, “The Dynamics of Productivity in the Telecommunications Industry” (1996) 

https://t.co/gISwiiJjg6

detailed_thread.text: Olley and Pakes (1996) is the foundational paper in supply-side industrial organization. It introduces the (with modifications) now standard procedure for estimating production functions, as well as a handy way to think of growth vs reallocation. 1/ https://t.co/mpAdCJYPay

A production function is simply an equation describing how combining various inputs produces output. For example, it might be a function of capital times labor. If Cobb-Douglas, they’re both raised to exponents denoting the share of income which goes to each.

The problem that econometricians face is that the productivity facing individual firms is known to them, but not to us. This causes two things. First, the least productive firms will leave the sample. Dropping these observations will bias the resulting estimates down https://t.co/q67DnSmYNn

Second, the inputs the firm chooses are simultaneously determined. More productive firms will use more inputs, leading us to think that adding more capital will raise output by more than it actually would. https://t.co/O5Mkpxiuex

To solve this, Olley and Pakes write down a production function where output is a function of labor, investments, and an unobserved productivity draw. Productivity is assumed to be related only to productivity the period before, and the firm invests more with higher productivity.

Investment is fixed by the productivity draw of the period before. Thus, we can express firm productivity as a function of the prior investment, and now control for productivity as a function of observables.
This lets them solve for what an optimizing firm should do, and add back in the firms which went out of business. https://t.co/gfydu4yLIm

The period they happen to be studying was the period in which AT&T lost its monopoly on telecommunications equipment. Previously, in order to hook up to the AT&T phone lines, you had to be licensed by them. Removing this lead to a great increase in productivity.

Olley and Pakes also give us a handy way to think about changes in productivity. They decompose it into the average productivity of all firms in their market, and the covariance of firm productivity and firm size. https://t.co/nfYVbxK4cz

Productivity growth can take place through raising the technological level, or through taking business from the least efficient firms and giving it to more efficient ones. They found that it was the latter that prevailed. This theme would be taken up by later trade work.

Olley and Pakes, “Dynamics of Productivity in the Telecommunications Industry” (1996)

https://t.co/qIvso1Vbag

papers.entry_text:
Hughes and Majerovitz, “Measuring Misallocation with Experiments” (2025)
https://t.co/wyiSy4ph1a

detailed_thread.text: Measuring misallocation is difficult because we can’t tell if firms face different production functions or different costs when they choose their inputs. To get around this, economists have devised clever — but unbelievable — methods. What if we just randomized inputs? 1/ https://t.co/f7IaMhu672

The literature on misallocation truly starts with Hsieh and Klenow (2009). There, they assume each firm in an industry shares a Cobb-Douglas production function. Misallocation is measured as a tax or subsidy to labor or capital.

You have to assume a shared production function in order to estimate it. Otherwise, you’d have only one observation. What Hughes and Majerovitz use to get around this is the random assignment of capital to microenterprises in Sri Lanka. Now we don’t need a production function.
Instead, we can use the dispersion in how firm output changes. In an efficient economy, all firms — regardless of production function — would change by the same amount.
Specifically, they impose a constant elasticity of substitution condition on demand. https://t.co/IL19FLfJcf

This is a fairly big deal, to be clear. If you don’t do this, the Bond et al critique (that firms affect revenue through quantity produced, thus making us unable to measure the markup) really bites. (I think). https://t.co/nRGhIbn22W

Returning to our story, we can show that losses from misallocation are quadratically increasing with the size of the distortion. Losses from misallocation are also increasing in the number of things which can be reallocated. https://t.co/AvlBiwYIep

If you want to scale up to the economy as a whole, you can either take the weight of the sector in expenditures as a whole (standard), or you can (with more data) include reallocation from sector to sector. https://t.co/9lyJsaggio

The paper this is closest to is Carillo et al, who find wildly different results for misallocation. (Why? Our authors are focusing on intermediates in a network, rather than final goods in construction). https://t.co/WICx4d9b2j

So, results wise, I’m not sure whether I should believe them. Something unconsidered (I think) is whether or not micro-entrepreneurs face greater variance in productivity over time. https://t.co/lLH8p7nwWl

Still, I’m reminded of Marschak and Andrews (1944). The fundamental problem is that the industrial organization economist doesn’t control anything. Why not? Why not experiment? https://t.co/n5RmRpaIQ5

Hughes and Majerovitz, “Measuring Misallocation with Experiments” (2025)

https://t.co/uDr3mKr91f

papers.entry_text:
Bachas, Gadenne, Jensen, “Informality, Consumption Taxes, and Redistribution” (2023)

https://t.co/x9KeF1WmNg

detailed_thread.text: Conventional wisdom is that consumption taxes are regressive, but this is not the case in the developing world. Why? Because poor people just don’t pay taxes! 1/ https://t.co/SvFY8XLj8r
The share of expenditures made in the informal — read, tax-evading — economy is sharply falling in income. Thus, the rich end up predominantly paying a uniform tax rate. https://t.co/2n4XqOx1aq
An implication is that exempting food on the basis of redistribution does not actually redistribute. https://t.co/PSXpfWUIUn
They construct their data from a pile of household expenditure survey — I include these as being of interest to researchers. https://t.co/2qyyM66qk0
The paper is a cool example of how theory must meet practice. We cannot assume even simple things, like “people pay the taxes imposed”, without checking that it’s actually happening.

Bachas, Gadenne, Jensen, “Informality, Consumption Taxes, and Redistribution” (2023)

https://t.co/UU8EwLAEvf

papers.entry_text:
Levinsohn and Petrin, “Estimating Production Functions Using Inputs to Control for Unobservables” (2003)
https://t.co/zGJ59Wp4av

detailed_thread.text: We discussed Olley-Pakes (1996), the foundation of modern production function estimation, yesterday. Levinsohn and Petrin (2003) is an improvement over their methods as it allows us to have more useful observations. 1/ https://t.co/gxVwxxsKlL https://t.co/uA5hJS2xBB

OP estimate unobserved productivity from investment. This allows us to properly estimate the effect of more capital and labor — otherwise, the effect would be biased upward by more productive firms hiring more. https://t.co/hYAu8xsRt8

Levinsohn and Petrin note, however, that many firms don’t make investments in every period. The infrequency causes lumpiness in the response of output. All firms do, however, use materials and power. Can we use these intermediate inputs instead? Under conditions, we can. https://t.co/QOwzUJVrob

They implement some tests to check if it works. More productive firms must use more materials or capital. If there are multiple flexible inputs, they should agree with each other. And productivity has to follow a random walk. https://t.co/2Wd1mP2lod
Ultimately, the researcher should choose the methods which suit the data set. Levinsohn and Petrin simply expand the set of choices available. https://t.co/wYe7ELaEdF

Levinsohn and Petrin, “Estimating Production Functions Using Inputs to Control for Unobservables” (2003)

https://t.co/hQi1hFa6bq
@instrumenthull

papers.entry_text:
Boxell and Conway, “Journalist Ideology and the Production of News” (2022)

https://t.co/6NitFDSBXI

detailed_thread.text: Journalists have opinions over what news should be covered, and how. It turns out that they’re responsible for 16% of observed differences in slant between newspaper outlets. Along the way, Boxell and Conway make some very elegant methodological advances. https://t.co/srMbOPd6Lt

Prior work, like Gentzkow and Shapiro (2010) had to measure slant by taking the specific words used by Democratic and Republican politicians. This is obviously not ideal, so now we can train an AI model on the articles tweeted by Republican and Democratic politicians. https://t.co/4gCTEe1lug

The other methodological advance is in accounting for selection. Obviously, a paper which wants to change its slant might hire more ideological journalists. We can’t just show that hired journalists don’t entirely change their slant to match their new outlet.
However, what we can do is compare them to new hires and leavers. All we need is for them to be equally selected, which is a much more believable assumption. https://t.co/oDnCj0XtNk

Boxell and Conway, “Journalist Ideology and the Production of News” (2022)

https://t.co/yK1Jns2BKv

papers.entry_text:
Moldovanu and Sela, “The Optimal Allocation of Prizes in Contests” (2001)
https://t.co/6Siy7IZYEx

detailed_thread.text: Contests sometimes award one prize, and sometimes award several. Why? Which is optimal? Moldavanu and Sela show that it depends on how costly effort is for contestants. If it grows sufficiently costly fast enough — that is, it’s convex — it is optimal to offer multiple prizes. 1/ https://t.co/VIGTHkGM1f

The setting is that multiple risk-neutral agents are in a contest to produce a thing. Producing this requires effort, which is costly. Everyone knows how costly effort is, but agents have private information on how able they are. https://t.co/XQqOTnESY6

We can note that the problem is isomorphic to that of an all-pay auction, which is one where people pay their bids, win or lose. In that world, whether you choose to bid is related to how likely you think you are to win. Low ability people drop out, high ability stays in.

If costs are linear or falling, increasing the first prize always induces as much or more effort as a second prize. This is because the second prize induces effort among lower ability people, but reduces it among higher ability people. https://t.co/zazrFxrDGG

If, however, marginal cost is increasing and its second derivative is positive, then a second prize induces more effort among lower ability people than it reduces among the high. https://t.co/OixVo7lpUt

This holds even if the low ability people are screened out via a fee. The number and sizes of prizes is also conveniently related to the curvature of effort, and can be calculated. https://t.co/utlY7i5y8y

What does this mean practically, for you? If you have a task to be done which is always unpleasant, offer multiple prizes. If you have a task which, once you get into it, will be fun, offer one prize.

Moldovanu and Sela, “The Optimal Allocation of Prizes in Contests” (2001)

https://t.co/8AHXYH7BVl

papers.entry_text:
Ronald Coase, “The Nature of the Firm” (1937)

https://t.co/gJA4uj4rB1

detailed_thread.text: Economic activity is organized into discrete units called firms. They’re so common that we might even stop to think why they even exist. After all, couldn’t economic activity be coordinated by prices? Why do firms exist all? 1/ https://t.co/UYsDaekeug

It is perfectly conceivable to have firms organized as markets. Textile mills, for instance, used to not have employees, but rather they rented out the right to use the equipment every day. https://t.co/5tN5aEElV3

Coase’s answer is transaction costs. People are unable to perfectly specify everything in a contract, and so to avoid costly renegotiation, we engage in open-ended contracts -- in other words, we make a firm. https://t.co/hhTqYaS2jJ
At the same time, we must take care that it not imply that the firm should expand without limit. There must be a constraining cost on its expansion. https://t.co/jyTjQswPuQ

We should think of market mechanisms as a way of eliciting information. Thus, if the cost of managing increases as the firm gets larger, there is a point where it is better to undertake transactions on the open market. https://t.co/QjIe8WJJCB
Thus, Coase can give us some broad strokes predictions of when firms will be small and large. https://t.co/AtNL2YMcuc
Ronald Coase was the first to consider the theory of the firm. In the next few days, we will cover how other economists have taken it.

Ronald Coase, “The Nature of the Firm” (1937)

https://t.co/GoHL3PNWmY

papers.entry_text:
Nicholas Ryan, “Contract Enforcement and Productive Efficiency” (2020)

https://t.co/YmmuL1Ecxr

detailed_thread.text: The government sticking to an optimal course of policy, even when it is time-inconsistent, is a big deal not just for monetary policy. Nicholas Ryan shows that when contracts aren’t enforced, firms misrepresent their bids, knowing they’ll just renegotiate them later. 1/ https://t.co/5wNwREQkbE

This is in spite of the fact that bidders are able to entirely account for the possibility that coal prices will go up in their bids. Those firms which were more connected choose to take on the risk of price increases, knowing they’ll be bailed out. https://t.co/2gGV1zr6vu

The measure of how politically favored the firm is comes from a rather embarrassing chapter of Indian history. The state has a monopoly on the production of coal. Naturally, they must have some system of distributing it. Did they sell it in an efficient auction?
Of course not. They just, gave it away. They gave away TWO HUNDRED AND FOURTEEN BILLION DOLLARS WORTH OF COAL. (AHHHHHHHHHHHH) https://t.co/pVuf9ETqu3

But anyway, now we have a proxy for how favored firms are by the government. (I’m not done screaming — AHHHHHHHHH). You can use this to construct a structural model and recover the bidders preferences.

I skip over the details of how to get to the main result — India totally committing to enforcing bids precisely as they are written would reduce the cost of generating a unit of electricity by 10%. https://t.co/vq2iDLy5ed

Nicholas Ryan, “Contract Enforcement and Productive Efficiency” (2020)
 
https://t.co/9tvgqy6ebx

papers.entry_text:
Katherine Wagner, “Adaptation and Adverse Selection in Markets for Natural Disaster Insurance”, (2022)

https://t.co/R0xwjz1zLj

detailed_thread.text: Flood insurance is more than actuarily fair — it is actually giving away money to homeowners. Yet, 40% of high-risk consumers don’t purchase flood insurance. Why? And what does it mean for optimal policy? 1/ https://t.co/XbWF6vfBTx

It is possible that they have information the insurance company lacks, and low-risk consumers are opting out. This is called adverse selection, and has a simple test — if average cost rises when prices rise, the market is adversely selected.
You cannot simply use any price change, of course — you don’t know why the price changed. What you can use are exogenous shifts, such as those mandated by Congress. https://t.co/YYjobkzg04

Costs do rise, but it’s entirely accounted for by the characteristics the flood insurance company can observe. Consumers do not possess private information — they’re just making bad choices. So what can explain the effects? https://t.co/TIvXrgce3i

She tries out, and rejects, some alternate explanations. Ex post bailouts simply aren’t large enough; most homeowners own too much to foreclose; moral hazard doesn’t fit; the hassle is bigger when getting it the first time than when it lapses. https://t.co/IZsadBqjoa
What does fit is that people are just plain wrong about how likely flooding is. https://t.co/L1BgUr80Yr

This means that making flood insurance actuarily fair would actually *decrease* welfare, not increase it. In addition, a mandate to purchase insurance would raise welfare by billions of dollars. https://t.co/Y00RwJBlxc

Katherine Wagner, “Adaptation and Adverse Selection in Markets for Natural Disaster Insurance”, (2022)

https://t.co/IRg5qppwqS

papers.entry_text:
Klein, Crawford, Alchian, “Vertical Integration, Appropriable Rents, and the Competitive Contracting Process” (1978)

https://t.co/lNMlsPtuT5

detailed_thread.text: Why do newspaper publishers own their own presses, while book publishers do not? Klein, Crawford and Alchian argue that as an output becomes more specialized, the more likely firms are to vertically integrate in order to stop opportunistic behavior. 1/ https://t.co/6JDas0Y37I

Specifically, the larger the gap between the value of a good between two companies, and the next best value of the good, the more likely it is for firms to engage in opportunistic behavior. https://t.co/q1Hji5PbAp

For example, a firm could claim that commercial conditions have changed, and it is simply impossible for them to provide the good unless paid more. There are clearly instances where this is true, but these renegotiations allow for the extraction of rents.

To go back to the newspaper and the book publisher, the newspaper needs their stuff printed every day, and delivered over night. They cannot be held up at all. On the other hand, a book publisher can get it printed wherever. They gain from being able to use specialized printers.

Klein, Crawford, Alchian, “Vertical Integration, Appropriable Rents, and the Competitive Contracting Process” (1978)

https://t.co/w9egoJVYE9

papers.entry_text:
Milyo and Waldfogel, “The Effect of Price Advertising on Prices” (1999)
https://t.co/uAykLzRpko

detailed_thread.text: In 1996, the Supreme Court ruled Rhode Island’s ban on liquor advertising unconstitutional. Afterwards, the prices of advertised products fell relative to Massachusetts. An underrated early difference-in-differences study. 1/ https://t.co/7NS61p4OTH

The rules were extremely restrictive — you could not only not place ads, you couldn’t even publicly display your prices. The whole thing was designed to obfuscate price comparisons. https://t.co/fDBAo0x1G4
I also found the data collection part really cool. https://t.co/beIT8aouq7

Anyway, the results are rather mixed. Prices only fell on advertised items. An important point they raise is that, in the absence of quantity purchased, one cannot make welfare claims. https://t.co/X9VoJcmDYZ
They proxy for quantity with lottery purchases made at stores, which is clever. This is imperfect, though — consumers could stock up during times of discounts, and make fewer trips overall.

Milyo and Waldfogel, “The Effect of Price Advertising on Prices” (1999)

https://t.co/vy7UAvALOB

papers.entry_text:
Jerry Hausman, “Valuation of New Goods under Perfect and Imperfect Competition” (1996)

https://t.co/pVQOItCpXs

detailed_thread.text: Much of economic growth comes not in the form of falling prices, per se, but from the introduction of new goods. Not including them will lead to us substantially understating welfare, and overstating inflation. But how do find the value of goods which didn’t exist? 1/ https://t.co/8bzDxlHw3l

We can calculate it by figuring out the price of a good which would set demand to 0, and then presume that this shadow price fell when it came into being. To know this, we must estimate the demand curve. https://t.co/J7HYfcdEZu

Hausman has data over a few years in 7 major metropolitan areas. His identification of the demand curve stems from local advertising campaigns, which he assumes shifts demand in that city but not elsewhere. The unobserved parts of demand are filled in by assuming convexity.

But there are a few complications. First, brands of cereal are substitutable for each other. Not including this overstates gains to the consumer. On the other hand, simply assuming a constant elasticity of substitution will overstate the gains badly.

In the case of CES demand, every good increases welfare by the same increment, which is obviously inaccurate. So he segments into different types of cereal, and while allowing for cross section elasticities, ignores brand to section elasticities.

Further, we need to take into account the ways that a brand affects other brands by the same company. It might take business away, but on the other hand, it also means that the customers who remain have more inelastic demand. https://t.co/pmv1v95qOo
Put concretely, when GM introduces Apple Cinnamon Cheerios, the people who are still buying Honey Nut Cheerios are the ones who really like it. Thus, GM can charge a higher price.

Hausman estimates that the benefits to the consumer are larger than the losses from price discrimination. Apple Cinnamon Cheerios increased welfare by $78 million a year, and the consumer price index overstated inflation by at least 20% in ready-to-eat cereals.

Jerry Hausman, “The Valuation of New Goods under Perfect and Imperfect Competition” (1996)

https://t.co/RhZskWqvVU

papers.entry_text:
Chetty, Guren, Manoli, Weber, “Does Indivisible Labor Explain the Difference?” (2013)
https://t.co/IlJI5gbNyR

detailed_thread.text: Large changes in employment over the business cycle imply a labor elasticity far larger than observed in studies of quasi-experiments. What gives? A common explanation is that labor supply is indivisible — but as these authors argue, that doesn’t actually matter! 1/ https://t.co/Tmbe0upFbO

The argument for indivisible labor supply is that it allows the intensive margin — number of hours, conditional on a job — to be small, while allowing the likelihood of having a job to change widely. Temporary shocks can differ from long run shocks.
These elasticities are the Frisch and Hicks elasticities. https://t.co/IJOJQDOLXv
CGMW point out that the two are joined together, and shouldn’t be that different from each other. You can evaluate using the same experiments. https://t.co/3FRZxxU3zd

In particular, the extensive margin response is determined by the density of reservation wages around whatever margin you’re evaluating from an experimental context, so you don’t need to make assumptions about the distribution of reservation wages. https://t.co/Uc6qhQmN87

They have a couple experiments — the coolest one being when Iceland just skipped a year of income taxes — and plug them into an RBC model. The elasticities observed come nowhere close to what is needed. https://t.co/73nUXX9mar

I still think the macro models can be rescued. It’s totally plausible that everything is more elastic in the long run, and can’t ever be super well-identified. However, if you use things other than vibes to identify one elasticity, you need to accept the other.

Also, this is peak early Chetty. Doesn’t necessarily do his own experiment, just shows how data can be used in an unexpected and clever way. I love his early papers, and sometimes wish he were more challenged.

Chetty, Guren, Manoli, Weber, “Does Indivisible Labor Explain the Difference?” (2013)

https://t.co/oyJdpL160D

papers.entry_text:
Kawano, Sacerdote, Skimmyhorn, Stevens, “On the Determinants of Young Adult Outcomes” (2024)
https://t.co/9xDF45vC6J

detailed_thread.text: The impact of location on a child’s outcomes are small but definite. Here, the authors use the random assignment of military families to different bases to get around selection. A 1 SD better neighborhood for 20 years raises SAT score by 40 points. 1/ https://t.co/KkryeFNFFu

Note the scale. These are, on the whole, small effects. However, with a sample that large (over 700,000 people) they’re quite precise. Effects are linear in length of exposure, as too in Chetty-Hendren. https://t.co/5NNJWWFThB

The neighborhood level estimates are bigger than the county level, which raises concerns about selection. However, instrumenting by average characteristics of places chosen doesn’t get rid of the increase, so I’ll believe neighborhoods have a causal impact. https://t.co/EcMiTx5Nxu

Kawano, Sacerdote, Skimmyhorn, Stevens, “On the Determinants of Young Adult Outcomes” (2024)
https://t.co/CZJl4ppVKP

papers.entry_text:
Dingel, Gottlieb, Lozinski, Mourot, “Market Size and Trade in Medical Services” (2024)

https://t.co/4WLyigciCz

detailed_thread.text: Really important paper for those interested in rural healthcare. Medical care improves in quality as potential demand gets larger. The upshot of this is that subsidizing rural healthcare makes us worse off — including rural areas! 1/ https://t.co/28OqTHa59e
They regard people moving from place to place to receive medical treatment as equivalent to importing and exporting. Thus you can apply the gravity equation toolkit to estimate the cost of traveling.

You can then get scale elasticities with instruments. Obviously these are concerning instruments, but they argue that the ways we’d jump to in saying its confounded aren’t actually an issue. https://t.co/DRe6QEDzVB
We’re somewhat agnostic as to why agglomeration economies occur. They also don’t find what it implies about doctors wages. https://t.co/0pRTeZv9XK
You would expect scale economies to be largest for rare procedures, and lo! what have they found. https://t.co/LnnJ6IUd2V
Plugging their results in, subsidizing rural healthcare — or any net importer — won’t cause them to export to others, but will reduce quality everywhere. https://t.co/LuPZjx0v7o

Dingel, Gottlieb, Lozinski, Mourot, “Market Size and Trade in Medical Services” (2024)

https://t.co/FerRWzpQGj

papers.entry_text:
Hwang, Jakobs, Squire, “Health Effects of Cousin Marriage” (2025)

https://t.co/KfRoZc2NU5

detailed_thread.text: We finally have really credible evidence that cousin marriage is bad for your children. While not perfect, I think their identification strategy is believable, and lets us say that marrying your cousin wipes at least two years off of their life. 1/ https://t.co/VllClhQ953

Their empirical strategy is to only compare cousin marriage children to their cousins who were born from out of the family marriages. They check that the marrying cousins aren’t different either by showing that they live just as long as their siblings. https://t.co/AskZihVVYO

Note that this is an underestimate! Infant mortality is hard to observe, because many infant deaths (especially in the 1800s) would go unreported. The real effect was probably 3-4 years of life, in a time when life expectancy was lower too. https://t.co/gNTGoClYKx
This is why banning incest which would lead to children is so important. https://t.co/XeOnJSdIM8

Hwang, Jakobs, Squire, “Health Effects of Cousin Marriage” (2025)

https://t.co/6F3ncf2mkX

papers.entry_text:
F.T. Bruss, “Sum the Odds to One and Stop” (2000)

https://t.co/rlMIIR9E6I

detailed_thread.text: Suppose a die is to be cast N times. We observe each roll sequentially, and must predict which one is the last time we shall see a number — say, 6 — rolled. What is the strategy that maximizes our accuracy? Go to the end, sum the odds to one, and stop. 1/ https://t.co/Nzg5UWILFx
In other words, the odds of seeing a 6 are 1/6. So you count backwards from the end, until you are 6 rolls back, and say that any observed 6 in those rolls will be the last one.

Here is the proof. Put into plain english, you are comparing the likelihood of the last one happening after. When you observe it happen, the probability it occurred is one. When the probabilities add to greater than one, it’s more likely to happen on the other side. https://t.co/n1ruHjkLny

F.T. Bruss, “Sum the Odds to One and Stop” (2000)

https://t.co/KDEAL1Nm9G

papers.entry_text:
Mejia, Prem, Vargas, “The Rise and Persistence of Illegal Crops” (2021)
https://t.co/z3qQnI9gtX

detailed_thread.text: In one of the great policy blunders of all time, the Columbian government announced in 2014 that they would pay illegal coca growers for switching, when negotiations concluded. This led to the number of acres under cultivation *quadrupling* as farmers raced to qualify. https://t.co/8SVjvz2693
Spot 2014 etc https://t.co/XNrocIybIk

It’s a simple difference-in-differences paper, where they compare differences in places with different suitability, and places that expected to get compensation due to be disadvantaged areas. https://t.co/ghZqcI9qdE

Mejia, Prem, Vargas, “The Rise and Persistence of Illegal Crops” (2021)

https://t.co/ZdyeeOwTK8

papers.entry_text:
Einav, Finkelstein, Cullen, “Estimating Welfare in Insurance Markets Using Variation in Prices” (2010)
https://t.co/w9UkLWj5DA

detailed_thread.text: Adverse selection is one of tue ways that an insurance market can break down. People with low risk opt out, causing premiums to rise and more people to opt out. This paper gives us a simple way to measure how bad adverse selection is. 1/ https://t.co/pu1VfK2ziA

If there is adverse selection, when prices rise the first people to leave will be those with the lowest costs. Average costs rise. If there is advantageous selection, then the opposite happens and average costs fall.
You can represent it graphically as such. Much like a monopoly, there is a disconnect between marginal cost and price, because the same price must be charged of everyone. https://t.co/a8LoikGBoX

You can’t use just any change in price, of course. Changes in price can be due to demand. But, if you have some exogenous change in supply and can observe the insurer’s costs, you can estimate the model.

They have quite detailed data from Alcoa, which is essentially a bunch of miniature companies. The department presidents chose different types of plans from corporate for their division as a whole, and thus you’ll have similar employees in different divisions get different plans. https://t.co/2qFeBSbqwU
Adverse selection exists — it’s just the effects are tiny. The world lost out on $9.55 per employee. https://t.co/LKJSkIBHQU

Einav, Finkelstein, Cullen, “Estimating Welfare in Insurance Markets Using Variation in Prices” (2010)

https://t.co/SILqsE1KDj

papers.entry_text:
Hill and Stein, “Race to the Bottom” (2025)
https://t.co/MfF9DARGsl

detailed_thread.text: We credit the first person to arrive at a result over those close behind, which causes people to rush out sloppier work. Hill and Stein is an exciting paper because it is the first to plausibly estimate how costly this is. 1/ https://t.co/oIHeuUBhYf
There are two steps. First, they need to know the quality of a given work, which they arrive at using the Protein Data Bank. https://t.co/7vOXBY0CHV

The field is uniquely well-suited for objective checks on quality. Upon producing a protein, it takes time to verify the structure by X-ray crystallography. During that time, they could be scooped by someone else, and if they do it sloppier, they can get it out sooner. https://t.co/Q5t9YA9QCC
We can then weight the proteins by their social importance, which they infer from the things which predict citations. https://t.co/FcRNl9RWTM
There’s a concern over omitted variable bias, which they address by comparing to research groups which are not competing for credit as much. https://t.co/mmBZIhHLN6

They construct a simple model of two researchers who make investments, and then let the discoveries “mature”. With that, we can evaluate counterfactuals. The social planner is not able to achieve first best, because they cannot dictate investment. However, credit sharing helps.

Hill and Stein, “Race to the Bottom” (2025)
https://t.co/1byYi5GEeJ

papers.entry_text:
Cicala, Lieber, Marone, “Regulating Markups in US Healthcare” (2019)
https://t.co/5Q7GZDJK77

detailed_thread.text: Part of Obamacare limited the profits which insurance companies could get. If the gap between premiums paid and revenue is too big, they have to write their policyholders a check. So what’s it done? Only increased medical costs in America. https://t.co/dbfnBknzaE

Think about this way. The law is saying that if the company doesn’t spend enough, they lose all the money they saved. Why would you spend any time preventing waste and fraud below a threshold? What’s more, since it’s a percentage, spending more actually *increases* profits!
The identification strategy is to compare those companies which were nearer to pre-existing medical loss ratios to those who were further. https://t.co/NPnB5daHy8
In the end, it raised medical expenses by 7%. We really should repeal this! Making it economically advantageous to do thing efficiently really matters. https://t.co/PqvhKAs8pR

Cicala, Lieber, Marone, “Regulating Markups in US Healthcare” (2019)
https://t.co/w5pHEfHI7E

papers.entry_text:
Al-Malk, Maystadt, Zanardi, “The Gravity of Distance” (2024)
https://t.co/fDJ7tJGAbN

detailed_thread.text: In order to calculate the benefits of trade, we need to know how costly it is as a function of distance. This is often correlated with differences in culture, which may not be what we’re looking for. This paper plausibly decomposes the two sources of trade costs. 1/ https://t.co/waKvm4wufQ
Qatar is a small country in the Middle East, which is not on very good terms with its neighbors. https://t.co/3FOGygwGSN
In 2017, most of the surrounding countries cut off diplomatic relations and closed their airspace to Qatar. https://t.co/H1pCjcPUcB
This was a big deal for Qatar. They don’t have anything besides oil. In order to live, they need to import things, and they often do so by air. https://t.co/8GXMOVSV1T
The identification strategy is simply that flights which were going over the areas blockaded became more expensive relative to flights which weren’t. https://t.co/wRFbgbvcUD
What’s striking is that the elasticity of distance is lower here than in other works. This goes to show that part of the cost of trade is cultural. https://t.co/vQ1CYpprkZ

Al-Malk, Maystadt, Zanardi, “The Gravity of Distance” (2024)

https://t.co/BfL7IBxHqV

papers.entry_text:
Collier and Ellis, “A Demand Curve for Disaster Recovery Loans” (2024)
https://t.co/5qnRaqxRFM

detailed_thread.text: Disaster recovery loans generate $2900 per borrower in value, even after accounting for distortion from taxation. This completely changes my views on the program. Read more to learn how they estimated this! 1/ https://t.co/lrb3SynUlh

The Federal Disaster Loan program is part of the Small Business Administration, and gives out loans to people who have suffered from certain disasters, like hurricanes or floods. The program has the explicit intention of relieving credit constraints.
To that end, they offer below market rates to higher risk consumers. https://t.co/IDtssQg7g9

To trace out a demand curve, you need something which moves supply, but not demand. The FDL changes its rates every quarter, which means that disasters just before, and just after, get offered different rates. https://t.co/dqmhr8DqRb

Normally demand estimation is very difficult, and your “instruments” which shock supply but not demand (or vice versa) aren’t particularly believable. Pulling up Tim Bresnahan’s hierarchy, though, this program is clearly first on the list. https://t.co/rNwqe55R5b
Each of these changes are small, but you can reasonably connect them together to fill out a demand curve for the whole market. https://t.co/1V5sBkf3hy
They double-check their results with a different estimation strategy — there’s a discontinuity in who gets offered what rate at a credit score of 700. https://t.co/KwNx3wKTQT

Putting it all together, we can estimate social surplus by comparing the area under the curve, and the cost of the program inclusive of any reasonable estimate of distortion from taxation. It comes out as still quite positive. https://t.co/HSs6ihby7h
This paper changed my views. I am extremely hostile to the Small Business Administration — I see them as largely subsidizing unproductive small businesses — but this part is good. It can stay.
