Collier and Ellis, “A Demand Curve for Disaster Recovery Loans” (2024)

https://t.co/lAEe4XiufV
@cellis212 is a friend of the blog, and has some cool papers

papers.entry_text:
Bloom and Van Reenen, “Measuring and Explaining Management Practices” (2007)

https://t.co/9R10XkASHh

detailed_thread.text: The quality of management was, for years, a black box to economists. Everyone believed it had to matter. Nobody had the data to study it seriously until Bloom and Van Reenen (2007). 1/ https://t.co/6YYCJtEqIz

The only way to elicit information on management is to ask people. Their RAs interviewed 732 firms in 45 minute long phone interviews, asking about basic good management practices, like recording inventories and promotions for good performance. https://t.co/5goOGV1WlL

Questions were very openended, and were essentially double blind. Firms did not entirely know what they were being asked about and why, and interviewers didn’t know how well the firm was doing. https://t.co/DSoww26iFb

With scores in hand (normalized to have mean of 0 and variance of 1), we can estimate the effect on production, using several (including Olley-Pakes 1996) methods. (They get financial and production statements from other sources). https://t.co/AgYe52KCqW

Their main findings are that management really does matter, and that the reasons why poor management continues are lack of product market competition, and passing down firms through the family. https://t.co/dXrUYep5Hn
This is enough for Britain and France, both of whom have many family-owned firms passed down by inheritance, to have far more firms in the “left tail” of terrible management. https://t.co/ADaPYkr29c

This is one such firm. It’s a BBC show that takes a recently retired head of a major chemical corporation, and drops him into a failing company. I have never seen a group of people more antipathic to the notion of change. I wanted to bash my head in.
https://t.co/imWIR1f5WV
It may even be responsible for much of Britain’s stagnation. Certainly doesn’t help, then, that firms are apparently exempted from estate tax too. https://t.co/PkQS4D16AI

Bloom and Van Reenen, “Measuring and Explaining Management Practices” (2007)
https://t.co/51VK6VodAT

papers.entry_text:
Autor, Pathak, Pallais, “Marginal Effects of Merit Aid for Low Income Students” (2021)

https://t.co/NAj7eDeVU5

detailed_thread.text: Awarding a scholarship to a low income student increases their likelihood of completing the degree from 64% to 72%. However, I am skeptical that doing so is cost-effective, in spite of the authors’ claims. 1/ https://t.co/Wvpfcv4QXE

Here’s the key bit. We are assuming they are like the average student, that the effect on their wages is purely through human capital accumulation, and that education is a transfer. I take issue with all three. https://t.co/uiXg0bXnlq
First, they don’t control for SAT scores or similar. It is extremely plausible that the benefits might be smaller for below average students. I would have liked to see this discussed.

Second, we assume the gain to the student is purely due to them being more productive. If it is because they have a degree that allows them to get a stream of rents instead of another, though, we needn’t expect any gains to society.
Third, education is a cost. The people who would have dropped avoided paying for it. You can’t say that paying for education is therefore a transfer like cash.
So, we should expect the headline figure to be lower. Will ask about this. https://t.co/JF7Cxl2OrT

Autor, Pathak, Pallais, “Marginal Effects of Merit Aid for Low Income Students” (2021)

https://t.co/qfwPTdEX5n

papers.entry_text:
Chevalier and Goolsbee, “Are Durable Goods Consumers Forward Looking?” (2009)

https://t.co/UDDzcpZUor

detailed_thread.text: Is it possible for manufacturers to raise their profits by making their products become useless faster? It depends on whether consumers consider future reselling opportunities. https://t.co/K4iiKp8jkr

Back in 1997-2001, the only real option for buying a textbook was the school bookstore. You could buy it for the semester, and then sell it back at a lower price. Sometimes, though, a new version would come out, and the bookstore wouldn’t accept it.

We can write down a demand system where likelihood of buying is due to the observed characteristics of the book, plus price times the likelihood it dies times discount rate, plus unobserved characteristics and error. We want to estimate delta, the discount rate, times mu. https://t.co/k3xUVuOgx3
We can pin down mu with the hazard rate of a book being revised. We can’t see, though, if students are discounting the future, or if they are simply unaware. https://t.co/XdOzNlAFDG
It’s multiplicative, so hard to separate out. https://t.co/p3tqPdQ4MR

To deal with the unobserved characteristics, they argue that non-profit publishers hold prices down, and are an exogenous shock. I do not, to be clear, entirely believe this; but I think it’s probably most true, and in any event it’s the best we got. https://t.co/S76rupON1Z
We can definitely reject myopia. Consumers care about what prices will be in the future, and you can’t very well increase your profits by making old copies obsolete. https://t.co/1Wad2oISp2
They test for an informal market biasing their results. They consider this unlikely. https://t.co/B7sYSuxUwm
The implication is that planned obsolescence for large purchases is unlikely to be a real thing. Rather, obsolescence might be due to technological tradeoffs, but not market manipulation.

Chevalier and Goolsbee, “Are Durable Goods Consumers Forward Looking?” 2009

https://t.co/N0iT6cE5Lo

Since 2019, MacKenzie Scott has given away 16 billion dollars to non-profits. These gifts come after Scott researches the non-profit in secret — the foundation first hears about it over the phone.

papers.entry_text:
Jones, Logan, Rosé, Cook, “Competition and Consumer Discrimination” (2025)

https://t.co/2lG7ZH2Qdi

detailed_thread.text: A paper which turns the conventional narrative of discrimination on its head — not something done by businesses, but positively demanded by consumers. Competition actually worsens discrimination in this model. https://t.co/KwYf1xhj36

This team of authors had digitized the Green Book on travel accommodations to measure discrimination against Blacks from 1940-1960. Now they add in considerable information on prices actually charged, which is an altogether remarkable data collection initiative.

Their empirical approach is using instruments to test for the effect of more minorities on reported discrimination. Obviously, they find that more Blacks means less discrimination against them. https://t.co/LY53VT9Ogv
Their new contribution is showing that this occurred among the least competitive parts of the economy. Only when Whites were unable to switch away did businesses serve everyone equally.

Jones, Logan, Rosé, Cook, “Competition and Consumer Discrimination” (2025)

papers.entry_text:
Fisher and McGowan, “On the Misuse of Accounting Rates of Return” (1983)

https://t.co/rKJIajUzNH

detailed_thread.text: Determining profits is actually really hard, and accounting profits do not map onto economic profits. 1/ https://t.co/jUOOdSh2XZ https://t.co/Nghlpkmrqs
There is some value which we would exchange for the future flow from an asset. That’s the economic rate of profit. It is this we should tax, not profits minus costs. https://t.co/hwDCux1D70

Practically speaking, firms which are growing more have lower profits in accounting statements than slower growing firms. Taxing accounting rates of return distorts firms away from the optimal amount of investment. https://t.co/ZxFBrJalBG
A numerical example. All of these are set such that the economic rate of return is 15%, yet apparent profitability is way different. https://t.co/F6qZXjNv55
Also, be so fr, depreciation is unknown. We don’t know it. Depreciation is what the tax code says you’re allowed to depreciate by.

Fisher and McGowan, “On the Misuse of Accounting Profits” (1983)

papers.entry_text:
Eric Zwick, “The Cost of Corporate Tax Complexity” (2017)
https://t.co/j2Zw9FfTKn

detailed_thread.text: If we are to tax corporate gains, we must allow them to deduct corporate losses, else we would penalize firms for variance. The problem is that only 37% of firms take a refund when eligible. We need to greatly simplify the tax code. 1/ https://t.co/glrCDWuG6l

To be clear, the number taking advantage of any sort of deduction is fairly high, it’s just that firms have two options. Either you take the deduction against past income and claim an immediate refund, or you carry forward against future income. The former has a higher NPV.
This matters for fiscal stimulus which comes through tax deductions. We should expect a lower MPC out of it.

https://t.co/Cbjn3ArWRK

The estimation strategy is synthetic control. Basically you search for a weighting of comparison cities that most closely matches the trends before, and then see how it diverged after the treatment event happened. https://t.co/7bCmHywLjH
Sometimes a picture is worth a thousand words though. Speeds jumped after implementation. https://t.co/MiPAEd4d0X
In particular, entrance portals saw a big improvement in speed. https://t.co/63O5wcwu0l

papers.entry_text:
Dreyfuss and Hoong, “Calibrated Coarsening” (2025)
https://t.co/UGVoTg9ctO

detailed_thread.text: Elegant job market paper from @RuruHoong . Suppose humans are biased against AI, but still provide some information. Can the way in which AI presents their recommendations matter? Yes! 1/ https://t.co/ETmU5snxg7

If the human doesn’t correctly include the information the AI gives, we can change their decision by giving less. Instead of giving a score, we can divide into a “yes” or “no”. (Read Bayesian Persuasion!)

If partitioned into above and below 50%, a yes is equivalent to 75% and a no to 25%. This means that strong yeses are underweighted, but because human-AI signals are correlated, those were getting approved anyway!

What I thought elegant was the graphical exposition. Suppose AI and human ratings are correlated, with those pairs given by the colors. Biased humans will overweight their own signal. https://t.co/YLnH5old5S
In the extreme case, the line is vertical. Likewise, if AI is the only thing providing info, the line would be horizontal. https://t.co/uIW8SHFFkK
The area for improvement is the gap between the lines. https://t.co/q1uzUb1VBJ
The people whose decisions would actually change are shown by the curves. https://t.co/RDFbEmD9P5
Thus, the gains from coarsening are the region highlighted in blue. Yes, the gold area gets worse, but those are very rare due to the correlated signals. https://t.co/qsboDorAY0
But where to put the threshold? They then prove that it’s at the place where the AI alone would approve or deny.
They put it into practice with an experiment, and coarsening raises welfare in practice. https://t.co/s4XtBHwH7Z
I am concerned, obviously, that they are not measuring a primitive. The degree of bias is likely to change. However, showing where the optimal partition is is very interesting.

Dreyfuss and Hoong, “Calibrated Coarsening” (2025)

https://t.co/slOGOjw1rZ

Kreps and Scheinkman, “Quantity Precommitment and Bertrand Competition Yield Cournot Outcomes” (1983)

https://t.co/g8WaffMB54

detailed_thread.text: Market outcomes under oligopoly are often dependent on the way in which firms compete. Do they choose quantities first, and then price? Or price first? Kreps and Scheinkman show that any quantity choice, even small choices, yield the former outcomes. 1/ https://t.co/HR0UtxCjMx

This can be as simple as the firm building capacity to produce stuff later. So long as there are limits, they will solve it as if they are producing quantities first. Hence markups need not disappear with multiple, symmetric firms. https://t.co/I1KofD61II

Kreps and Scheinkman, “Quantity Precommitment and Bertrand Competition Yield Cournot Outcomes” (1983)
https://t.co/je0ubwN4hs

papers.entry_text:
Caplin and Nalebuff, “On 64% Majority Rule” (1988)

https://t.co/faIBGwDmW5

detailed_thread.text: Voting systems commonly require super-majorities. Why? They favor the status quo, which means it’s possible to avoid voting cycles, where no position is able to stick. What Caplin and Nalebuff do is show minimal conditions which means that a 64% rule is *always stable*! 1/ https://t.co/m8TAUnSlZF

We’ll start by saying what we mean by voting cycles. Consider the simple preference relations below. You will never have a stable majority, and if candidates are eliminated, then the outcome depends entirely on the order of elections. https://t.co/AbZPLdnXKn
Simple majority votes will always have the possibility of cycling, but not super majority votes, if we place some restrictions on preferences. https://t.co/zMuRN8B9Ux

This can be vanishing, to be clear. Consider the question “who should we expropriate?” with n voters. Each proposal could be defeated by another expropriating a different person, with the min max being n/(n+1).

Gonna quickly define everything. We think of people’s beliefs about issues as occurring in a very literal space, with n dimensions. Thus we can treat opinions as having actual magnitudes. https://t.co/sQQKk6oYYv

We need to impose some structure. First, everyone has a most preferred point, and utility is monotonically decreasing as we get away from that point. This rules out multi-peaked preferences. https://t.co/LMPhmX2MjM

Equally important, voters’ preferences when aggregated are also single-peaked. If either of these are broken, then it is possible for there to be multiple local maximums. This is essentially a monotonicity condition. https://t.co/8qBAOq6RLU
Because of this, we have a strong result for political positions converging. It’s median voter theorem, but for n dimensions, guaranteed by the assumption of concavity. https://t.co/WsdtxSpmqZ

This changes the problem into cutting a cake. (Which, incidentally, I had heard of “you cut, I pick”, but never “you cut, I name a point it goes through”. These work out the same!) https://t.co/c0WAILdWrQ
Suppose you have two dimensions. Then it’s equivalent to cutting through a triangle, and the only stable majority is five ninths. https://t.co/T4lgWQXvhB
They grab a lemma from mathematics, which in the next theorem https://t.co/9LIyzAlOTX
Allows them to transform things to the equation shown below. https://t.co/dPQIeuBwrV
Simplifying, it converges to a limit of 1/e, or about 64%. https://t.co/ez6tVRUQoQ
The assumptions are quite strong. Can we relax them? Not really, to be honest. We have to have single peaked preferences. https://t.co/MwoyHNDbsT
With finite samples, you can have bad luck, but it converges to 64%. We don’t need to have a complete set of alternatives either. https://t.co/2lmx0uVR10

Caplin and Nalebuff, “On 64% Majority-Voting” (1988)

https://t.co/1u2P7efRzW

papers.entry_text:
Jerry Hausman, “Individual Discount Rates” (1979)
https://t.co/EPXE92OVhW

detailed_thread.text: Suppose we want to know how much consumers discount future consumption. A simple way to do this is to observe purchases of consumer durables, which often trade off a high price now for lower energy costs later. 1/ https://t.co/LrW7gL4bKL

Let’s first consider what we’re trying to do. We want to find some discount rate which will rationalize consumer behavior, given observed depreciation of air conditioners and less well observed taste for air conditioners.
We’re assuming exponential discounting of the future, where it decreases by some constant percentage over time. https://t.co/xutlmZ5WWk

The cost of operating the AC is assumed to be a linear function of temperature, and we say it only kicks in at 65 degrees. We now need to estimate how much households want to utilize the AC. https://t.co/H0NVgAdO1H
We trade off between utility from AC and from outside goods. These should be equal. https://t.co/MEEI9h9w29
Since they substitute for each other, operating cost has to equal marginal utility. https://t.co/3BL83loAQF
Thus we can get it from energy usage, which we do observe. https://t.co/d5zgFhyUNL

Observed energy usage is a function of capacity over efficiency, times utilization. Hausman cites evidence that capacity is given by the room size, and since we are not modeling choice of housing, we will treat capacity as exogenous. https://t.co/AljE1m3Snt
To simplify the problem still further, he assumes consumers pick high, medium, or low types of air conditioning. https://t.co/ndrn982jDi
We assume a normal distribution for each parameter, and that they are independent of each other. https://t.co/g0wSRlAupc
And here is our likelihood function to maximize. https://t.co/bEdiTRJOHY

He treat air conditioners as bundles of attributes, and estimates how changing one attribute would change the price. You do this by assuming independence of the attributes, and having a multi-dimensional regression. https://t.co/DgDWvGMQj6
No data on the breakdown rate of air conditioners existed, so he estimated it himself from consumer survey day.
We can get utilization from actual data, including house characteristics and the weather. https://t.co/G92jLkmTLq
Nothing is left but the discount rate. Almost 25% a year, which is extremely high, and indicates that consumers are perhaps myopic about future costs. https://t.co/JhFuCSBp0y

Jerry Hausman, “Individual Discount Rates” (1979)
https://t.co/GGLTslbCtJ

papers.entry_text:
Samuel Kapon, “Dynamic Amnesty Programs” (2022)
https://t.co/H8dhg3eBqp

detailed_thread.text: Why do we give amnesties for crimes? It turns out that the optimal policy for crime prevention is to alternate between periods of amnesty, and harsh punishment. Come see why! 1/ https://t.co/lE2mHCAob2

We assume that there is a flow of criminals who all share a rate of detection, and receive some utility from crime which is observed by them. Some are high return types, and some are low return types. This will effect who will self-report, conditional on an expected punishment.

There are two extremes of policy. One is the same policy all the time, which allows high crime types to get away with it while it’s high, and turn themselves in when low. The other is amnesty once, which obviously can’t happen again for future criminals. https://t.co/GeNIzjZ2Va
Optimal policy involves a cycle. Low return types self-report immediately, while high return types report right before it jumps back up again. https://t.co/Nfkd4jobcr
The frequency of these cycles is determined by detection rate, maximum penalty, and how quickly returns change. https://t.co/gLC6P707Yw

This rationalizes historical anti-desertion amnesties — and more importantly, shows that offering a continual amnesty is probably not optimal unless detection rate is really low. One is naturally curious about its application to cartels (of the antitrust kind!) https://t.co/K0IT8pn1Cz

Biggest problem with the model is entry into being a criminal. Choosing to enter/not enter is sort of the one-time amnesty as before. However, I think describing people as entering as criminals is reasonable. https://t.co/39W3TNlkBV

Samuel Kapon, “Dynamic Amnesty Programs” (2022)

https://t.co/jISjcZ8cxe

papers.entry_text:
Miller and Weinberg, “Understanding the Price Effects of MillerCoors” (2017)

https://t.co/3b6hqdABVx

detailed_thread.text: In 2008, the FTC allowed Miller and Coors to merge. This was a mistake. Not only did their prices spike immediately after, the price of Budweiser beers also surged due to tacit collusion. 1/ https://t.co/4FulcZud6z
Miller and Weinberg (names I find funny, in context), estimate that it raised prices 7%, despite costs for the firm falling. Welfare fell substantially. https://t.co/M17u5QH6YB

In merger analysis, there are two (sort of three) competing forces. First, a merger may make the firm more efficient. In the case of beer, Coors is an exception in making their beer in Colorado and shipping from there. Local production would cut transportation costs.

On the other hand, a horizontal merger will increase a firm’s market power. It can also, by reducing the number of firms, make it easier to collude (although collusion depends on symmetricality too, so it need not always make collusion easier).

To estimate which prevails, you need to estimate demand, and how consumers substitute. In this case, they use distance from a brewery as an instrument for price, along the lines of a gravity equation in trade.

As we can see, these goods are substitutes for each other, and consumers limit their substitution to the other types of beer. They don’t buy the “outside good”, in other words. This shows a 1% increase in price reduces demand for a beer by about 4%. https://t.co/orjOYoVjhR

With that in hand, we can show how the merger would affect costs, and what prices should have been. They can reject any other explanation but a change in the conduct of firms (conduct has a technical meaning for the way in which firms compete — prices or quantities first?)
They reject it being explained by some correlated shock because the other types of beer didn’t show this same pattern.
We can show with confidence that Miller Lite got made more expensive. In particular, beer got 80 cents cheaper to make, but increased in price by a dollar. https://t.co/1AfuX1U8Xy
And worse still, beers unaffected by the merger were also able to raise their prices — markups everywhere! https://t.co/THA5vah5VO

Miller and Weinberg, “Understanding the Price Effects of MillerCoors” (2017)
 
https://t.co/qwDRK8X66n

papers.entry_text:
Sherwin Rosen, "Potato Paradoxes" (1999)
https://t.co/6H96xpPs4N

detailed_thread.text: Do Giffen goods exist? Are there products whose demand increases when prices rise? Potatoes are a commonly cited example, but Rosen shows this isn't so. Instead, since potatoes are a capital good too, confusion over future productivity explains the apparent rise in demand. 1/ https://t.co/pEr67U76iB

Why might it be the case that a good will sell more when its price rises? There is the possibility of Veblen goods, which are desired for their exclusivity, but these are obvious and uninteresting. In any case, no one desires potatoes in 1840s Ireland as a marker of status.

When a price changes, there are two effects. The higher price causes a consumer to substitute to other, cheaper goods. At the same time, the rising price changes the effective income of the consumer. It is possible for the income effect to outweigh the substitution effect.

Potatoes are grown from potatoes. Output is thus a pure function of saving. Equilibrium is reached when the marginal profit of saving a potato is equal to the marginal profit of eating it now. https://t.co/ZQNCvO0E7O

The dual nature of potatoes, as consumption and savings, means that it exhibits totally different responses to transient and permanent shocks. A transient negative shock to productivity implies saving more now, while a permanent shock implies eating more now.
In the context of the potato famine, a fall in productivity could lead farmers to liquidate their savings, thus lowering the price in the short run. https://t.co/QvyEQjfGgP

Since the farmers were confused about it, and believed it to be a transient shock, they planted far too many potatoes in 1846. This goes to show the importance of functioning futures markets for agriculture. https://t.co/cb5DP4SOso
And we can also rationalize the apparent rise in consumption. Farmers thought they had to save much more, in response to a fall in supply, to meet demand the next year.

Some asides. Why weren't Irish farmers diversified? Well, they were -- against a transient shock. You turn potatoes into pigs. Pigs take a while to breed, so they insure against year to year fluctuations. https://t.co/lqtMJZqLpn

Sherwin Rosen, "Potato Paradoxes" (1999)
https://t.co/6fZXWpP9v4

papers.entry_text:
Tim Bresnahan, “The Oligopoly Solution Concept is Identified” (1982)

https://t.co/3W2jOzNXZw

detailed_thread.text: In industrial organization, the data we have to work with is extremely limited. We can't directly see cost, so we have only price and quantity data. How do we tell apart collusion from a shock? https://t.co/EyAgnuMeyk

The first thing to note is that P and Q are dependent upon the way in which firms compete with each other. They could engage in Nash-Bertrand pricing, which is setting prices first, then quantities; they could price like a monopoly; they could choose quantities first.

All of these yield very different outcomes. With homogenous goods, the first is the competitive outcome. The monopoly outcome is the maximum markup we could get, which we'll scale to 1. Cournot, which is quantities first, gives a markup of 1/n.

We can represent supply and demand in equations, and the mode of conduct is a simple multiplier of the demand equation. Astute readers will note that this works for the same reasons as the Hall (1988) method of markup estimation. So how to identify lambda? https://t.co/Y6gljgXqcv
A simple instrument for demand -- that is, shocks in Y -- will not help us here. Both full collusion and perfect competition will look identical. https://t.co/viBWDip2zG

However, if the slope of the demand curve changes as the result of a shock, they are now observationally distinct. If competitive, then there will be no effect -- the same quantity will be outputted. If there's collusion, then more output will be produced. https://t.co/mtxNnn6vtZ

The pure demand shifter Y and the curve changer Z can be thought of as income and substitute goods. It makes sense intuitively -- if firms have been charging markups, and a similar product enters, that has a very big impact on how they price their product!

Tim Bresnahan, "The Oligopoly Solution Concept is Identified" (1982)
https://t.co/jkJZ6wJu37

papers.entry_text:
George Stigler, “The Cost of Subsistence” (1945)
https://t.co/mj11Yqd65I

detailed_thread.text: In 1945, George Stigler turned his attention to the question of a minimum diet. What is the absolute cheapest one could live off of for year? The answer: 370 lbs flour, 57 cans of evaporated milk, 111 lbs cabbage, 23 lbs spinach, and 285 lbs of dried navy beans. 1/ https://t.co/aOZ5mGCtJh
What makes it interesting was that there was, at that time, no methods of linear programming available. George Dantzig had not yet invented the simplex method.

Stigler starts by dropping all commodities which are strictly inferior to another, and then drops all that are markedly inferior per dollar on all but a very few things. Then, he tries out the promising combinations. https://t.co/D27v2CJWOV
Incidentally, he found a Giffen good. The price of flour increased, but this actually means that someone minimizing costs would consume even more. https://t.co/ZuQuwzOfR9
He finishes up by assailing nutritional scientists as being unwilling to cut to the bone, and quite possibly being corrupt. https://t.co/pbnbXbdiBx

George Stigler, “The Cost of Subsistence” (1945)

https://t.co/jcpORQJUcE

papers.entry_text:
Correia, Luck, Verner “Failing Banks” (2025)

https://t.co/zMK6GNCPxn

detailed_thread.text: Why do banks fail? There are two basic stories: bank runs, and insolvency, which call for different policy responses. The authors find that bank runs are common in theory, but rare in practice. This substantially undermines the case for deposit insurance. 1/ https://t.co/qzCS1BkeMm

Theory first. The bank gets deposits and lends them out to be paid back later. The bank promises to allow you to withdraw at will, but lacks the cash to pay everyone. If people believe it will fail, it will fail as everyone rushes to be first to withdraw their savings.

The key reference here is Diamond and Dybvig. Bank runs can be stopped by either insurance on deposits, or credibly being able to borrow. (Insurance is really just borrowing, except you pay in advance). The business is sound, it just needs to get paid off later.

On the other hand, a bank could fail because it just plain made bad decisions. It gave out loans to people who could not repay. Lending to the bank does no good — it pumps a bit of water out of a sinking ship, but there’s a gaping torpedo hull anyway. So which is more important?
They have data on 37,000 banks, of whom 5,000 failed. https://t.co/jnMB1KkDJH
These are not healthy banks which failed. You could have predicted it from their balance sheets. https://t.co/p6HtSRY5CH

The bank run and insolvency views aren’t totally incompatible, obviously. It could be that bad news sets off the bank run. Deposit outflows also became much less common after the FDIC. https://t.co/UsIoX7LYuH
But they have different normative implications. An insolvent bank failing does not destroy any value. In fact, subsidizing it to keep it afloat would.

Correia, Luck, Verner “Failing Banks” (2025)

https://t.co/BZ3ah9kObp

papers.entry_text:
Gelber, Isen, Kessler, “The Effect of Youth Employment” (2016)

https://t.co/LDRCxcPT6P

detailed_thread.text: Income shocks don’t really reduce criminality, but employment does. The important thing for crime reduction is not that people are less poor, but that they are *busy*. 1/ https://t.co/I7rDwo4Xue https://t.co/sHT6IizC7K

The Summer Youth Employment Program puts youth in entry level jobs for 7 weeks over the summer. It is widely applied for. They randomize, which is important because it controls for all factors, observed and unobserved, which may differ between the groups. https://t.co/T4EOx8Lf1o
This substantially reduces the risk of incarceration and of death from any cause. https://t.co/sJv2mlUp7e

Earnings increase in that year but decrease after, which is consistent with them getting job experience which is kind of a dead end. There no effect on college participation, however. https://t.co/SS0y7TfPR9

Putting some numbers on it implies that the benefits likely exceed the cost — especially since the cost of paying them is not equivalent to the actual deadweight loss of raising that revenue. https://t.co/ON26eDCFdz

Gelber, Isen, Kepler “The Effects of Youth Employment” (2016)

https://t.co/4u9IFdH3A6

papers.entry_text:
Green and Porter, “Noncooperative Collusion Under  Imperfect Price Information” (1984)

https://t.co/N8GCsXPSvp

detailed_thread.text: Suppose a cartel sees their prices fall. This is consistent both a fall in demand, and the breakdown of the collusive agreement, which call for the opposite response. How do firms tell which is which? Is it possible to sustain collusion with imperfect monitoring? Yes! 1/ https://t.co/g18QMY3MYV

Under the collusive agreement, firms (let’s say there are two, to keep things simple) estimate the monopoly pricing level of output, and divide it between the two. Each firm can profit by producing more than their share, but they can then be punished by the other.
Punishment looks like shifting to competitive, Cournot behavior. The markup becomes 1/n of the monopoly level, so 1/2.

The optimal strategy for each firm is for when their demand falls below a certain point — a trigger price — to always behave competitively. This rules out cheating, but the firms still have to commit to doing this. https://t.co/cCTWxXkVFj
It’s subgame perfect because everyone expects demand to follow a random walk, and it will return to a higher price in the future.

The takeaway is that, yes, collusion is possible without coordination given a sufficiently low discount rate. You cannot expect either the possibility of entry, or opportunistic cheating, to prevent it.

Green and Porter, “Noncooperative Collusion Under Imperfect Price Information” (1984)

https://t.co/8cneeJDC2i

papers.entry_text:
Bils and Klenow, “Quantifying Quality Growth” (2001)

https://t.co/rzJGHMSmS4

detailed_thread.text: When measuring inflation, we have to adjust for the quality of goods. This is really hard, though — how do we plausibly say how much more utility a vacuum gives as a function of suckage? Bils and Klenow have a clever solution. 1/ https://t.co/Pd6sk2CjiN
They exploit the fact that rich and poor households spend different amounts on the same kinds of goods. They interpret this as buying higher quality goods. https://t.co/rDMy0VhPqW

As incomes rise, the higher quality goods rise faster than lower quality goods. They interpret this as unmeasured increases in quality, which the BLS does not entirely adjust for. https://t.co/plUEfATO6H

The reason we adjust for quality, incidentally, is that a price index is ideally measuring the amount it takes to buy a constant level of utility. Thus, better goods provide more utility for the same price.
By their measures, inflation was overstated from 1980 to 1996 by 2.2% per year. The implications are enormous — in particular, we’re wildly overspending on social welfare programs.

Bils and Klenow, “Quantifying Quality Growth” (2001)

https://t.co/1iITdX1sTk

papers.entry_text:
La Porta and Lopez-de-Silanes, “The Benefits from Privatization” (1999)
https://t.co/vSL9rEgSf0

detailed_thread.text: Privatization raises profitability, but does this come at the expense of workers? Privatization leads to layoffs of unproductive workers, but pay raises for those that remain. Most of the gains in profits are due to increasing productivity. 1/ https://t.co/Uk9qDQ0v2b
They have the universe of privatized firms in Mexico from the 80s. Their method for finding causation is to match to a set of similar companies. https://t.co/T57lOkF0cn
When privatized, they quickly converged to the level of already private firms, slashing costs per unit along the way. https://t.co/3Q0HReJW1Z

The important thing is that only part can be explained by firing unproductive workers. It also cannot be explained by harder bargaining, as pay rose. Instead, better managerial incentives made the firm better. https://t.co/pmBdiKtdeh

La Porta and Lopes-de-Silanes, “The Benefits of Privatization” (1999)

https://t.co/bGZ9G2mqrF

papers.entry_text:
Dellavigna and Kaplan, “The Fox News Effect” (2007)
https://t.co/ZrF0eNXZHd

detailed_thread.text: What the media shows really does matter. The introduction of Fox News substantially aided Republicans, and could plausibly have swung the 2000 election. When Trump interferes with who’s on television — yes, that really does matter. 1/ https://t.co/4vjrRDyGKL

The concern, of course, is that the entry of Fox News is endogenous. Simply comparing before and after would not suffice, as there may be time varying trends. It might also be the case that Fox News targeted either more persuadable, or places they expected to change more.

The first would hurt any attempt to find the general equilibrium consequences — the “low-hanging fruit” would already have been plucked. The latter would totally bias our results, and possibly give us the wrong sign.

They use an instrumental variable technique. Fox News was rolled out lumpily, as different cable companies adopted in different years. If they can argue that this is uncorrelated with the outcome, then you can use it as a source of exogenous variation. https://t.co/k1P4lILLzm
In particular, prior trends do not predict the availability of Fox News. https://t.co/aJxeJL3KuF
The explanation they believe best fits the facts is that Fox News persuaded people to vote Republican, largely through increasing turnout. https://t.co/R3II9GkySX
This is on the order of 200,000 voters, more than enough to swing the 2000 presidential election. https://t.co/9cdlVO8wvU

Interestingly, their results are biased down by the only way to show a change in beliefs being voting. If everyone already voted Republican, becoming more extreme means no apparent change. If it’s persuasion, in the long run you get more conservative candidates. https://t.co/GMEg1HlVtR

Dellavigna and Kaplan, “The Fox News Effect” (2007)

https://t.co/OjvZj2dqOo

papers.entry_text:
Taylor and Druckenmiller, “Wetlands, Flooding, and the Clean Water Act” (2022)
https://t.co/CaE3qEibpY

detailed_thread.text: Wetlands play a substantial role in reducing flooding, by essentially sopping up water that would have flowed elsewhere. This makes them an example of a positive externality. Allowing development on them in 2020 was a costly transfer from the public to landowners. https://t.co/8pOvfTj1XA

To make this question tractable, they narrow the scope of damages greatly. They are only including claims to the National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP). They have on hand the corpus of claims, but this means they will not include uninsured damages or loss of recreation.
The main empirical approach is to use gravity. Water flows downhill. Only upstream wetlands mitigate flooding. https://t.co/LQnlqBtrAS
Bearing in mind that this is a lower bound, the flooding prevention benefits swiftly outweigh the benefits from using the land for other purposes. https://t.co/qzBz4sS3pe

Taylor and Druckenmiller, “Wetlands, Flooding, and the Clean Water Act” (2022)

https://t.co/3gLf92og4Y

papers.entry_text:
Vincent Rollet, “Zoning and the Dynamics of Urban Redevelopment” (2025)

https://t.co/dqgAaEYo0u

detailed_thread.text: Fantastic job market paper from @vincent_rollet. Highlights key problem for upzoning — what can be built is shaped by the past. You need to demolish the small before you can build the large. This revises down the gains from upzoning, which accrue mainly to movers. 1/ https://t.co/L0RMhE28B4
First things first, he has *extremely* impressive datawork. If you doing research on New York, you may find this of use. https://t.co/EsG389wvox

We can also establish that, yes, upzoning increases the amount that is built. We will figure out who benefits, and how much, later, but there can be no doubt that allowing people to build increases building. https://t.co/WCDhenEXkk

It also reallocates from use to use. NYC is undergoing a great switch in the location of land use, with residents moving to the core and commercial properties relocating to the outskirts. https://t.co/xUu0ZmGyzn

Rollet then models the decisions of developers in the spirit of Rust (1987). Developers have a depreciating building, and are deciding when to tear it down. We don’t observe their private information, but can infer it from behavior. That’s the supply side.

Demand side is pinned down by a quantitative spatial model. Unfortunately this is not an area I really understand well. You basically write down a model of commuting time etc, and see what best matches the data. https://t.co/XUzARDYT8K
This is a job market paper, so he is kind of throwing absolutely everything at the problem to show that he can do it.

But now we have results! Relaxing zoning substantially increases welfare, but by much less than you would get if you didn’t include transition costs. These benefits are spread across the whole country. https://t.co/wc26K1mEw9
If you’re concerned about distribution, this disproportionately benefits the poor. https://t.co/pCgmSWrZPQ
This tracks with prior literature on regional convergence — the poor were priced out of moving to better jobs by housing restrictions. https://t.co/ziWiJ29yGg
On the subject of adjustment costs, it’s entirely possible for burning down a city to raise welfare. https://t.co/8li8guQKPq
You might find, such as after the Equitable Life Insurance building burned down, that the land was worth more without the building than with. https://t.co/1oOC7BlmDq

Vincent Rollet, “Zoning and the Dynamics of Urban Redevelopment” (2025)

https://t.co/fpQfirHjxn

papers.entry_text:
Hunt Allcott, “Site Selection Bias in Program Evaluation” (2016)
https://t.co/ScGlwGEaFD

detailed_thread.text: In order for experiments to tell us things about the world, they have to generalize to other settings. Unfortunately, the places in which we choose to test things differ from the average. How big of a deal is this? https://t.co/sh7mRANmsF

Allcott studies this in the context of Opower, which mails out letters with energy conservation tips. It is trivial to randomize who gets the letter, and thus to evaluate the impact. https://t.co/FT8OafzZeD
What makes it useful for Allcott is that the experiment was conducted in 111 different places. Strikingly, the first ten places saw much bigger results than the subsequent tests.
The first sites tested were all more environmentally conscious, and so you’d expect bigger results from them. https://t.co/OX8SzXLEq9

This is a pretty big deal. Development interventions tend to be tested in relatively stable areas; drugs are tested in bigger hospitals; educational interventions are tested in schools that have bought it. If this is the case, external validity is less than you hope.

Hunt Allcott, “Site Selection Bias” (2016)
https://t.co/yS3B4Q77Fm

papers.entry_text:
Chris M. Wilson, “Ordered Search and Equilibrium Obfuscation” (2010)
https://t.co/FzgktvxOuh

detailed_thread.text: Firms are able to charge higher prices if search costs are higher for everyone. If comparison shopping is hard, then firms face less effective competition. Surprisingly, though, this holds even if one firm unilaterally makes it harder to buy from themselves! 1/ https://t.co/7A8sKYIKSu

Wilson’s result is that when a firm raises the cost to search from it, it induces its competitor to raise prices, which in turn allows it to raise prices. This increases the profits of both firms, and decreases consumer surplus.

The model gets price dispersion from some consumers paying no cost to search, and others paying positive costs. Note that if all consumers had the same search costs, increasing one’s search cost would lose all business. https://t.co/t3LfcKl7pa

Now, though, the positive costers go only to your competitor, who raises prices. You can now raise prices to within epsilon of your competitor, and get all the low cost types. Thus you move closer to the monopoly price.

Chris M. Wilson, “Ordered Search and Equilibrium Obfuscation” (2010)

https://t.co/uV0R2cv0Zb

papers.entry_text:
Romer and Romer, “The Macroeconomic Effects of Tax Changes” (2010)
https://t.co/fVkPQNdDsX

detailed_thread.text: A perennial difficulty in measuring the effect of big tax changes is that they are not exogenous. If a tax cut is caused by a belief that growth will slump, for instance, then any regression will be biased. How do we get just the exogenous tax changes? 1/ https://t.co/EVsPHRQMRX
It’s as simple as going through the source documents, and seeing why people did. There’s generally remarkable consensus on why exactly we’re doing a tax change. https://t.co/YJmPKqw06X
We can then normalize as “changes in percent nominal GDP”. https://t.co/iOE6hGwVOG
And find the effects of tax changes on GDP, which are considerably larger than the estimates which do not do this. This is more in line with what theory suggests. https://t.co/mhs01od0SI
I covered this paper is largely because I think this sort of work is ripe for AI. Previously this sort of work would have required considerable RA work.

Romer and Romer, “The Macroeconomic Effects of Tax Changes” (2010)

https://t.co/NZbkGWtBAw

papers.entry_text:
Maggie Shi, “Medicare Audits” (2023)
https://t.co/8BwT33e1bp

detailed_thread.text: If we care about government efficiency, we should care about overpayments to hospitals for needless care. Audits substantially reduce spending by cutting down on treatment, without any effect on patient health. 1/ https://t.co/dRDNZ6hUiX

Two empirical strategies to account for the endogeneity of audits. First, the country is divided into different regions for audit purposes. Hospitals on one side of the border see different intensities of audits. https://t.co/lO59z0GLPe
She also shows that this is not due to it being a state boundary by running the same exercise on all state boundaries which are not also RAC boundaries, and finds no effect.
Second, days in hospital are counted from midnight to midnight. Thus, rules affecting who can be audited by length of stay have discontinuities before and after midnight. https://t.co/bYFQtRqzPd
The savings are absolutely enormous! If we care about government efficiency, we should spend more money on this. https://t.co/7yJgKvDhbU

Maggie Shi, “Medicare Audits” (2023)
https://t.co/judDdGf5zS

papers.entry_text:
Hastings and Shapiro, “Fungibility and Consumer Choice” (2013)
https://t.co/mQgTd3Zpf2

detailed_thread.text: One of the genuinely important developments from behavioral economics is the idea of “mental accounting” — people designate income for some purpose, and don’t switch from purpose to purpose. This leads to seemingly bizarre behavior, such as purchasing more gas during a recession! https://t.co/u0wncP7pnA
There are multiple grades of gasoline. You don’t actually need to buy higher grades — you’re wasting your money — but people do it anyway. https://t.co/AMNslJ3XHw
When the price of gas rises, people substitute from high to low octane gas, holding expenditures constant.
Income shocks do little to change gasoline consumed, but commodity price shocks do. https://t.co/77q4OZO1Ci

This all matters because it leads to really different implications for, say, food stamps. Neoclassical theory says that it’s just a money transfer. Mental accounting says that it will increase the amount spent on food.
And that is indeed what these same authors found in later work. https://t.co/Bo8OuuXxGG
Adding a reference: this is the first formulation of the idea. Very clear exposition. https://t.co/aTAWm4ngJ5

Hastings and Shapiro, “Fungibility and Consumer Choice” (2013)
https://t.co/YlrFmkw4Mk

papers.entry_text:
Berry and Waldfogel, “Do Mergers Increase Product Variety?” (2001)
https://t.co/fkEShu39rP

detailed_thread.text: It is not clear from theory whether mergers will increase or decrease product variety. Firms will have the incentive to space their products further apart so they don’t compete with each other. On the other hand, they might simply reduce the number of products. Which prevails? 1/ https://t.co/mtOHb4Zolb
There’s also a third option — many varieties, in order to deter entry, but all really similar to each other. https://t.co/ZKyURIe2FX

Berry and Waldfogel use the 1996 Telecommunications Act, which removed many of the restrictions on firm concentration which had hitherto existed. In particular, different markets had different rules, creating plausibly exogenous variation. https://t.co/EiMYiU9Q3X
First stage checks out — there was a lot of consolidation, related to your ability to consolidate. https://t.co/DbMRVCm4ec
After consolidation, product variety rises. https://t.co/oAcYm1xkuN
They then inquire if this is due to slight differences in stations, to prevent entry. They find this is largely the case. https://t.co/H0R3TSI6fA

Given that there was excessive entry before, the Act likely raised welfare, though they cannot say this conclusively. One certainly should be cautious before saying anything about mergers and variety. https://t.co/kNDdBGXiyz

Berry and Waldfogel, “Do Mergers Increase Product Variety?” (2001)

https://t.co/5beaGVHG4e

papers.entry_text:
Madestam, Shoag, Veuger, Yanigazawa-Drott, “Do Political Protests Matter?” (2011)
https://t.co/kuFXNmmCZZ

detailed_thread.text: Protests are both a cause of changes in political attitudes, and a sign of changes. How do we disentangle the two? We can use rainfall as an instrumental variable — if rainfall on a day, conditional on likelihood of rain, is random and affects attendance, we can get the effect. https://t.co/1fDe88w6dJ
In 2009, the Tea Party movement had a series of protests around Tax Day on the 15th of April. Obviously places with greater attendance will have more people who care about the issue.

What we can do is exploit the possibility for rain. Fewer people showed up to outdoor rallies in bad weather. Rainfall and opinions could be correlated by region, so you also control for likelihood of rainfall. https://t.co/abH2waivLT
They get quite substantial results. Republican vote share increased, and representatives voted more conservatively. https://t.co/xlbIIKZETQ

Now, you might be asking, “aren’t rainfall instruments invalid”? Well, not as a general rule. The ones which generally are invalid are in the development economics context, where we’re using it as an instrument for agricultural productivity or what not.
Using rain/did not rain on one day or another should be valid, however. Tax Day, April 15th, was exogenously chosen.

papers.entry_text:
Heidi Williams, “Intellectual Property Rights and Innovation” (2013)
https://t.co/lawmvyzhhE

detailed_thread.text: Patents are a necessary part of inducing innovation, but they come at a cost. Heidi Williams found that a gene being patented, instead of being put in the public domain, reduced follow-on innovation using that gene by 20 to 30 percent! 1/ https://t.co/iKlvsDBm0M

The drive to sequence the human genome was something of a race. Private firms could take out a patent, if they found it first, but when the Human Genome Project found it the gene would be put in the public domain. https://t.co/U67RBuTDeF

We start with a simple cross-sectional regression. Genes which were found by Celera in 2001 are much less cited in future publications and patents than ones found by the HGP. This is an enormous difference. https://t.co/QKPDPVHaKD
One can be concerned that the genes are selected. However, that would tend to bias the results toward no effect, if Celera were seeking more valuable genes.

It wasn’t really the fees, per se. The companies which would use this are large and can afford it. Rather, the transaction costs are due to lack of shared information about future prospects, what other research is being done, and ex post appropriation. https://t.co/XYaeU0N19K

This really shows why academic research is valuable. Suppose that a good is produced using an innovation, which is in turn produced using another innovation, and so on. If each one is a monopolist, the distortion is greater than if a good is produced using multiple innovations.
Patents are more damaging, the more “upstream” in a network they are, and just like in optimal industrial policy, we should subsidize the upstream sectors and put them in the public domain.

Heidi Williams, “Intellectual Property Rights and Innovation” (2016)

https://t.co/pG3e15COr9

papers.entry_text:
Gentzkow, Petek, Shapiro, Sinkinson, “Do Newspapers Serve the State?” (2014)
https://t.co/zS4In2vnfz

detailed_thread.text: The free press was freer than I expected. With the exception of the end of Reconstruction in the American South, changes in party control had little effect on the success of partisan newspaper. https://t.co/0fhLiA9kkC
Methods wise, it’s a bog standard close elections regression discontinuity. We assume that places which voted 51:49 do not systematically differ from places voting 49:51. https://t.co/9wUqtPKbYJ
What is cool is the dataset, which you can actually find online. https://t.co/Z0iraWs2as

Gentzkow, Petek, Shapiro, Sinkinson “Do Newspapers Serve the State?” (2014)

https://t.co/PZhMQhIYpV

papers.entry_text:
Currier, Glaeser, Kreindler, “Who Bears the Cost of Road Roughness?” (2024)
https://t.co/GFr5PTei5k

detailed_thread.text: This paper is one of the most astonishing feats of sustained data wizardry I have ever seen. Using data from Uber, they are able to estimate the roughness of every road in America and precisely estimate the value people place on it, and so much more. 1/ https://t.co/naLGBp8f0V

First, what makes this all possible — acceleration data. Uber wants to know if people are suddenly braking. Their measurements along a horizontal axis incidentally allows them to measure up and down motion. https://t.co/IYQYso3Zu4

If you liked this thread, you can get more cogent exegesis of contemporary economics on my blog. 

https://t.co/JwvVPLytYh

papers.entry_text:
Aghion and Howitt, “A Model of Growth Through Creative Destruction” (1992)

https://t.co/ATYjbdVNal

detailed_thread.text: While Romer was first in making technological growth part of our models, I think Aghion and Howitt explicitly considering creative destruction has been more influential. Firms discover new technologies to be a temporary monopolist, only to be replaced themselves. 1/ https://t.co/5W5giAoDke

They have essentially a two-period model, although the length of each period is just the time between an invention and is so random. Firms invest in discovering new ideas, and then receive a flow of rents for as long as the next period lasts.

This has some implications. The amount we invest now is dependent on how much we expect other people to invest later. If we expect lots of research in the future, we don’t research now. Also, the more expensive labor is, the less they will research. https://t.co/jpxqk6vHiW
These will make for an equilibrium level of technological progress, and we will have steady, balanced growth. However, a dark possibility lurks.
It is possible for growth to stop. If everyone anticipates research effort to quickly replace an innovation, nobody finds that innovation to begin with. https://t.co/SVcWyvwqQv
Better research productivity need not improve outcomes either. https://t.co/WLSExzVnV1
In practice, things are fine — the positive spillovers from technology outweigh the negative spillovers from stealing business. https://t.co/SwBWEIwso6
Why is it better than prior endogenous growth? Those deliver a strong prediction that monopoly is always better for innovation, which doesn’t necessarily hold up. https://t.co/5BTAWijjle
I’ve never been, I admit, entirely convinced by the curve fit. Nevertheless, the relationship has to be much more linear if AK models describe the world. https://t.co/BGts0Mp6uE
Other stuff builds on it — take, for instance, Klette and Kortum, who allow firms to have multiple product lines, and to increase their markups by quality improvements. https://t.co/Ov4XVxAdxJ
This suggests population growth, by slowing creative destruction, makes markups worse, and perhaps explains the increase in markups over time. https://t.co/6Sy4now9IX
Aghion and Howitt do not win it for this alone. But, it is a substantial part of it, and a worthy contribution to the theory of economic growth.

papers.entry_text:
Ahlfeldt, Redding, Sturm, Wulf “The Economics of Density” (2015)

https://t.co/q7sTy77yQ8

detailed_thread.text: This is one of the most extraordinary papers ever written. Economists have long wondered how much the growth of cities is due to spillovers. The authors use the fall of the Berlin Wall to show that while spillovers are substantial, they’re very local.  Let’s understand how 1/ https://t.co/WM6m9fT874
First, the question. There are two basic reasons why people might be where they are. It could be that the location is really good for a city, or it could be that there are already people there.
Prior work focused on the distribution of people across cities, and tended to find that locational fundamentals were dominant. https://t.co/bx0rqo781C

The authors want to consider where and how people place themselves in the city, though, not just what city they live. They also want a model which can precisely estimate the impacts of infrastructure improvements, the opening of a factory, and everything in between.

They get this from Eaton-Kortum (2002), who have a general framework for international trade. What one can do is simply treat every city block as if it were a country, and apply the same modeling tricks. https://t.co/8UH3mWMzMW

At the core of the model is the idiosyncratic draw of utilities. In the EK model, each industry in a country draws its productivity from a Frechet distribution. (I’m going to skip why it’s convenient — see “Putting Ricardo to Work” for more). https://t.co/A1kP7pkyTe

This greatly simplifies estimation. We don’t need to separately model each industry or worker, just a continuum. There are two parameters — the absolute productivity, which determines wage, and the dispersion of productivity, which determines gains from trade.

These map onto absolute and comparative advantage respectively. The wider the variance in industry productivities, the greater the scope for trade. Since these are workers, they also care about where they work, but it’s the same idea.

People place themselves in the city in order to maximize their utility. They neither collapse into a point, nor spread infinitely, because they will sort themselves so that wage is exactly to transport costs, and no improvement is possible.

If one has the distributions of idiosyncratic utilities, then you can solve for the equilibrium given only the exogenously given characteristics of the city — transport links, proximity to water, and so on. https://t.co/Rs2hROsNKZ
A difficulty are the parameters governing agglomeration, which are endogenous to the model. An obvious concern is that there are multiple possible equilibriums.
One of the central results of their paper is showing that they can, in fact, find a unique equilibrium, although they need every parameter to do it. https://t.co/CBtvlQSkdy

But enough of theory! We need to estimate it somehow. What they use is the fall of the Berlin Wall, which was equivalent to transportation costs going from infinite to finite. If this was an exogenous shock, then it is sufficient to identify the whole model.
You can see the effect in the map of land values. After it fell, the value of land returned to the central business district, which could now trade with its counterpart. https://t.co/2iP2a0Hn9n
Incidentally, this is how they computed the cost of travel. I’m pretty sure that this is an extraordinary amount of computation, and I find it very impressive. https://t.co/PCCQvKatVD
The central result is that spillovers fall to almost nothing after just 500 meters, or 9 minutes of travel time. https://t.co/9UdiB9QvhH
Some might say that this is a howitzer to kill a gnat. But what a howitzer it is!

Ahlfeldt, Redding, Sturm, Wulf, “The Economics of Density” (2015)

https://t.co/vyd3XI5bq3

papers.entry_text:
Bick et al “How Do Hours Worked Vary?”
https://t.co/qsylzrj1bR

detailed_thread.text: Some important facts on hours worked. Poor countries work more, which leads us to understate their poverty relative to the United States. 1/ https://t.co/ChhDfW9iaX

The difference is really quite large — the bottom third of countries by income work 28.5 hours per week, while high income countries work 18.9 hours. 3/4ths of this is employment rates. https://t.co/IQE0Um0SyZ
The pattern is the same across all levels of education, as well as by gender.
You’ll substantially err when trying to find productivity if you don’t account for this. https://t.co/WxfaPF3JEz
Data comes from national surveys. https://t.co/x06oyfdIVI

https://t.co/1fZ7xP1bzZ

detailed_thread.text: I quite enjoyed this essay by Ed Lazear on the spread of economics to all subjects. Really it’s a survey, and introduced me to a lot of Becker’s work in particular. (Nobody reads Becker anymore!) 1/ https://t.co/2v83YzEpLA
What I found most surprising was economics as an influence on actually running businesses. Nowadays, IO methods are ubiquitous at any serious firm.
The other really important development was getting judges to think earnestly and seriously about what is good for the world. https://t.co/ffDIvJD3ca

papers.entry_text:
Ganong, Noel, Patterson, Vavra, Weinberg “Earnings Instability” (2025)
https://t.co/nCs5gnwm7k

detailed_thread.text: Really important paper. We totally miss most earnings volatility for the 60% of Americans who are hourly workers, because we measured it at the yearly, not the monthly level. High-frequency data reveals that the situation faced by low-income workers is worse than we believed. 1/ https://t.co/D7vsNLP10t

The important fact is that this is driven by volatility in hours worked, and not at all by changes in wages. Wages are quite sticky, in fact, which has led researchers to falsely believe that salaried workers have greater volatility. https://t.co/TQ84mY3VzD

Volatility for salaried workers is driven by bonuses, largely in December, which is all upside — they are much less likely to get negative income shocks. By contrast, low wage workers get evenly split shocks. https://t.co/VoGTkrkkv0
Well over 50% of salaried workers see no change in month-to-month pay, while just around 5% of hourly workers see the same. https://t.co/uCaP9pJUbm

This stuff really matters — since consumption changes with shocks, it is not simply smoothed over time. They estimate that this is equivalent to a reduction in income of 4-11%. https://t.co/7JVmW1KqkG

It’s not simply due to idiosyncratic desires of the worker — changes in total firm hours largely explain the month-to-month differences. They estimate fixed effects for firms from job-to-job transitions as well. https://t.co/BzD3EgKOqc
Since the volatility of earnings falls largely upon those least able to bear it, business cycles are likely more important than we thought.

Ganong, Noel, Patterson, Vavra, Weinberg, “Earnings Instability” (2025)

https://t.co/thQtgoZxVb

papers.entry_text:
Adrouzi, Blanco, Drenik, Hurst “A Theory Of How Workers Keep Up With Inflation” (2024)
https://t.co/HtBu0AIpsd

detailed_thread.text: 2021 had all the signs of a hot labor market. Vacancies were high while employment was low. Yet, workers hated it because their real wages were falling — something which should not happen if there really was a hot labor market! 1/ https://t.co/w3NHjM2MVi

Afrouzi, Blanco, Drenik, and Hurst seek to explain these facts with a theory of how workers keep up with inflation. An unexpected change in inflation leads workers to engage in costly search for better jobs. https://t.co/TC7vs7O8qw
In practice this looks like writing down a model, and estimating a pile of parameters. https://t.co/Fz3n6hT3qG
The important takeaway for policymakers is that the Beveridge curve, or the ratio of vacancies to unemployed workers, is not a sufficient statistic for the labor market being good for workers!
Second, Calvo pricing does indeed roughly describe the labor market, which makes inflation pretty bad for workers.

Afrouzi, Blanco, Drenik, Hurst “A Theory of How Workers Keep Pace with Inflation” (2025)
https://t.co/yNVc4DQB49

papers.entry_text:
Koki Okumura, “Ownership Structure and Economics Growth” (2025)
https://t.co/79bi0pt2bE

detailed_thread.text: In America, companies are increasingly owned by large institutional investors. This could discourage competition, but also increase investment into innovation. Kiko Okumura’s excellent job market paper argues the former effect is larger, and innovation will fall. 1/ https://t.co/V9oJMGTlaf
Firms introduce new products partially to steal business from competitors. If R&D is to find new products, then there will be less of it when all firms are commonly owned.

On the other hands, firms don’t capture the spillovers of the ideas they find. Thus common ownership would cause these spillovers to be internalized, and would increase R&D. It is not theoretically clear which prevails.
In the model, consumers have preferences over characteristics, which can be thought of as dimensions in space. https://t.co/8AeGlKRYSt
To get these product characteristics, he uses prior estimates of product similarity from work which finds the cosine similarity of product descriptions. https://t.co/RPwJWjjE6G
He finds technology spillovers from the similarity of patents, and allows for discoveries in one sector to happen randomly. https://t.co/58Q8bfHnth

I’m not entirely sure I understand what he’s doing in solving the model, but, he’s treating this as a dynamic game with a fixed number of firms. It’s dynamic because what firms do affect the payoffs, which affects what firms do, and so on. This is really hard to solve. https://t.co/5qbbuXNyJX
So, from what I can gather, he’s put some restrictions on the effects on payoffs to make them monotonic.
Having solved it, when can get estimates. R&D was below the social optimum to begin with, but common ownership would only make it worse. https://t.co/i0qC68dFPI
This would lower the growth rate substantially. Remember that stuff like this compounds over time! https://t.co/godNGqxl7t

Things are slightly better if common ownership affects product market competition as well, rather than just R&D. Thus we can explain the results where accounting for tech spillovers makes the effects of common ownership better. https://t.co/cWsDEMBY92
He argues the results are robust to alternative weights on ownership — e.g. if small shareholders get screwed. https://t.co/1y3OXygfqy
All in all, an excellent paper. I look forward to seeing it published in the Spring 2033 edition of Econometrica. :)

Koki Okumura, “Ownership Structure and Economic Growth” (2025)

https://t.co/apqQZ4JDGj
Pinging some interested parties for common ownership (this is a JMP, and I’m sure there’s some causal effect of being promoted): @DBaqaee @florianederer @BPellegrino_CBS @conlon_chris

https://t.co/KGAmF9eRuy

detailed_thread.text: A really elegant paper — essentially applying Ramsey tax theory to the minimum wage. If we’re set on redistribution, both the minimum wage and corporate taxes can do it. If capital is more mobile than labor, the “tax” should fall upon the labor input. 1/ https://t.co/YoiPFDpygF
The rest of the paper is estimating it, which is fine and believable. Fundamentally I like this paper because it makes one really clear conceptual point.

papers.entry_text:
Aakash Kalyani, “The Creativity Decline” (2025)

https://t.co/rHZs7uIpvH

detailed_thread.text: Total factor productivity growth is slowing down, but we have more patents than ever. What gives? Aakash Kalyani argues that if you look at the actual text of the patents, they’re getting less creative — a change likely caused by falling population growth. 1/ https://t.co/k3GXLid068

How can you tell creativity? Obviously, we cannot have RAs read all the patents, but we can use simple computational methods. Find all two word combinations, and discard those in common use before 1900. Those are technical bigrams. The less they’ve been used, the more creative. https://t.co/Vpsvz2bkXU
This is plausibly an improvement over using subsequent patent citations, and tracks with observable data on what goes with a patent being important. https://t.co/oP1BRcSArx

Kalyani applies the Olley-Pakes method of calculating total factor productivity (read De Loecker-Eeckhout-Unger for how that works) and finds that firms with more creative patents also have higher productivity growth, while firms with derivative patents fall off. https://t.co/FEBdZwL0QE
This allows us to explain how patents can have surged while we seem to find fewer new ideas. The patents aren’t breakthroughs in the same way. Creativity has fallen. https://t.co/4JAeM9hatd
Here are all three plotted together. https://t.co/fTsYWp5nrK

Kalyani wants an explanation, and thinks he has one in population change. First patents are more creative than later ones. If fewer new people are entering, then perhaps we will get fewer creative new ideas. https://t.co/Di4t4vPaOm

I am a bit skeptical of this bit. However, he argues that we can explain much of the decline in creativity from slowing population growth, which intuitively makes sense — fewer people means fewer ideas. https://t.co/VP8fUtlbR5

Aakaah Kalyani, “The Creativity Decline” (2025)
https://t.co/K7GyLzDyzy

papers.entry_text:
Zhongmin Wang, “Mixed Strategy in Oligopoly Pricing” (2009)
https://t.co/TeOXccvZ9u

detailed_thread.text: Any friction in changing prices is sufficient for even seemingly competitive firms to have above competitive prices. Let’s learn about price cycles in the context of Australia’s gasoline stations. 1/ https://t.co/wsfIyzVnjy

Suppose that there are N firms selling identical products and competing on price. For simplicity, whoever has the lower price captures the entire market. Firms go back and forth changing their prices. At each step, they will undercut each other, as above.

Eventually the price has fallen so much that undercutting would lose money. In that case, why not raise your price? The competitors will raise theirs too, and the cycle of undercutting can begin again. Everyone profits.
In real life, it looks like this. The biggest firm, BP, is consistently the leader, signaling to everyone else that they should raise their prices. https://t.co/NfZKLHGlZ7

This annoyed motorists in Australia, so the government passed a law regulating how frequently prices could change. You’d have to nominate your price for the next day in advance. https://t.co/GjfjQEFsbC
He gets data after from the government website to monitor prices, and data before from credit card companies. https://t.co/poGM6UwvO4

This fundamentally does not upset the method of collusion, though. In fact, it can strengthen it! If prices could be changed instantaneously and costlessly, we would converge to the competitive price. Firms would welcome restrictions on price changing! https://t.co/nYNlPbZRRX
A feature of interest here is that who led the price rise is rotating among the major market participants. This is consistent with them playing a mixed strategy of raising/cutting.

Another feature of interest is that these cycles happen fast. Data which is not recorded frequently will miss this entirely. It is not enough, for many purposes, to record prices once a week or once a month.

Zhongmin Wang, “Mixed Strategy in Oligopoly Pricing” (2009)
https://t.co/4D5jj4mHy1

This is the definitive study on working hours; they use World Bank data which has hitherto never been publicly accessible to cover 97% of the world population. Now let’s look at some graphs! https://t.co/usexP2uZVt

First and foremost — hours worked declines in developed countries. The very poor countries tend to also have lower hours of work, but with higher variance — perhaps reflecting lower quality surveys. https://t.co/VapASx4lPY
Believe it or not, seasonality of work is actually considerably higher in developed countries. You can see the Great European Summer Vacation in the graph. https://t.co/9HuU4V3v43
This is despite developing countries having a far higher portion of workers in agriculture. https://t.co/ylmgynav8u
Women work fewer hours than men. https://t.co/bXNaRo7gYy
This result is driven by the extensive margin. Women are less likely to be employed, but conditional on it, are employed only a bit less than men. https://t.co/cuDLoWJkpL
The pattern of development is stronger when we consider than women are more likely to work in richer countries. https://t.co/RkGPXNwWim
People in developed countries are more likely to have regular hours. https://t.co/ZnUiMC5XMa
This is obviously not causal, but, there is a substantial correlation between taxes on labor income and hours worked. https://t.co/MKgO3qIuTb

Gethin and Saez, “Global Working Hours” (2025)
