papers.entry_text:
Andrei Shleifer, “The Demand Curve for Stocks Slopes Down” (1986)
https://t.co/JdLLqG5Cjz

detailed_thread.text: The demand curves for stocks slope down. This is a pretty big deal. It implies that market capitalizations are basically uninterpretable, and that too little equity will be offered to the public. 1/ https://t.co/MOVblESMek

Shleifer also introduced a new instrument to economics. He needs an exogenous shifter of demand, and gets it from inclusion in the S&P 500, which many index funds try to mimic. Since it’s a surprise, we can use the jump in stock prices the next day.

This instrument will get used a lot in the future for other papers. Pretty standard part of the toolkit you should know about. Anyway, what of the implications? If an individual firm’s demand curve slopes down, then they’ve market power. They can profit by offering less stock.

Market capitalization is calculated by multiplying the total equity in the company by the value of the part out in the market. But since the price is a function of the amount offered, you now cannot just multiply. Market caps are notional.
The benchmark model should be that everyone will converge to the same information, and there should a horizontal demand curve for a stock. This just isn’t so. Disequilibrium is the norm.

Andrei Shleifer, “The Demand Curve for Stocks Slopes Down” (1986)

https://t.co/0nPZKcOATh

papers.entry_text:
Gandhi and Olenski, “Tunneling and Hidden Profits in Health Care” (2024)
https://t.co/qfvpqA1ELP

detailed_thread.text: This is a really cool example of Goodhart’s law in action — when the government relies upon profit figures to decide reimbursement rates, the profit figures become totally unreliable. Nursing homes are way more profitable than they pretend to be. 1/ https://t.co/gDR2JTmPMy
This is genuinely such a big deal. And it’s so simple too. Just sell the assets to a related company, and then lease the space. Now the profits have disappeared from view. https://t.co/cGHJab5jWK
I don’t know of a good solution to this, but, the government should be more skeptical of nursing homes crying uncle.

Gandhi and Olenski, “Tunneling and Hidden Profits in Health Care” (2024)

https://t.co/xceOeqBqSx

papers.entry_text:
Myerson and Satterthwaite, “Efficient Mechanisms for Bilateral Trading” (1983)
https://t.co/k3JkaVlvC4

detailed_thread.text: Myerson-Satterthwaite proved that efficiency through trading will never be reached, even if there are no costs to engaging in a transaction. The way in which goods are allocated initially really matters, and thus, so does auction design. 1/ https://t.co/0RvzeTVzS4

Suppose two parties, A and B, are to trade a good. There are a few desideratum we would like to have — we’d like participating to be individually rational, we’d like to have the person who values the good more have it, we’d like no subsidies, and we’d like incentive compatibility
So, we’d like nobody to get a negative payoff by entering the contest, and we’d like the mechanism to function even when no one is altruistic. Can we get all four?

We cannot. The most intuitive way to see this is that, if we suppose A has the good and a value of 0, and has a valuation drawn [0, 1], the profit maximizing strategy is to offer a take it or leave it offer at .5, for an expected profit of .5.

The only way to get all mutually beneficial trades to happen is to set the price at 0, and guarantee the whole surplus to B. If, however, A also has a value drawn from [0, 1], then the only way to guarantee it gets transferred is to give the whole surplus to A.
Both of these cannot be satisfied. Thus, the only way to get all optimal trades to happen, in the presence of unknown information, is an outside subsidy.
What this means is that we cannot expect trades after the fact to restore allocative efficiency, even if negotiating is incredibly cheap. Inefficiency is baked in to the optimal strategy.

Therefore, the way we initially allocated the good really matters. If the government is giving a license to do something, the format of the auction genuinely matters. Giving it away not only loses revenue, it causes allocative inefficiency directly.

papers.entry_text:
Barahona, Dobbin, Otero “Equilibrium Price Responses to Targeted Student Aid” (2025)
https://t.co/XAs9tTb7re

detailed_thread.text: Whether financial aid for students will simply increase tuition depends on how it is targeted. A blanket subsidy will simply increase the price, but if it changes the elasticity of those on the margin of going, it can actually reduce the price! 1/ https://t.co/yelYmSrq8I

That increasing demand might increase the price is obvious. But what of the latter? If universities can charge only one price, then they may cater only to higher willingness-to-pay folks. If lower WTP people have more elastic demand, then they may drop the price to get them. https://t.co/SpePIuZp2e

The plan of the paper from there is very simple. All you need is the demand elasticity with respect to aid, the degree of price discrimination possible, and how dispersed willingness-to-pay is in the population. https://t.co/pEUlyWK17c
If you have an exogenous shifter of overall prices (the reform) and one exogenous shifter of eligibility (the household income threshold) then the model is identified. https://t.co/NYmLkrOupY
In Brazil, student aid did raise prices. But, better targeting — toward need, not merit — can staunch the rise in prices.

Barahona, Dobbin, Otero “Equilibrium Price Responses to Targeted Student Aid” (2025)

https://t.co/6MBo8Rkydj

papers.entry_text:
Bengt Holmström, “On Incentives and Control in Organizations” (1977)
https://t.co/jvYYZ2NedF

detailed_thread.text: How can a principal control an agent when the agent is biased, and we rule out transfers? Bengt Holmström’s PhD thesis gave us some extremely elegant results on what the principal can do. In brief: the optimal policy is to only cap spending. 1/

Let’s say we’re throwing a party. The Principal (P) engages an Agent (A) to hold it. We normalize the space in which the principal wants decisions taken from 0 to 1, where 1 is the most money they might want spent and 0 the least. This spending depends on the state of the world.

The Agent is able to perfectly elicit this, while the principal is not. However, the Agent has different preferences over the action. They would like more to be spent than P would. This bias is denoted beta, or ß. So, if the optimal spend is .5, A will spend (.5 + ß).

We assume that P has a level of spending they would most prefer, and their utility uniformly declines away from it. In fact, we will say this loss of utility is quadratic, so utility declines as a square of distance from the ideal.

The Principal can only control the Agent through the set of choices which they allow A to take. This leads to the first result: should P only allow A a discrete set of choices, or a continuous range? We can prove that they will always prefer a set with more options than fewer.

Take this simple graph. The upper line is the choices A can take. The bottom line is preferences P have. With a “hole” in the options, you get a stepwise function. Since utility loss is quadratic, this *must* lead to more utility lost than a straight line. https://t.co/iurA7uZsPD

Instead, P should offer a continuous range. But how should they restrict? We can see that restrictions at the bottom are irrelevant — if the state of the world is 0, then A will spend ß. Thus, things between 0 and ß never happen.

On the other end, if the state of the world is 1, then the agent will spend (1+ß). The Principal never wants this, so you can put the cap at 1 without loss. What this gives us is that the optimal sets given are a function of the bias ß.

Specifically, the Principal will offer a choice between (ß, 1-ß) is ß is less than or equal to 1/2, and then give the choice of only 1/2 if ß is greater than 1/2. Intuitively, if A is biased enough, P simply chooses what minimizes their loss without information from A.
It’s a really neat, elegant idea, and shows what happens under idealized conditions. That’s important! It gives us a standard to compare to if we explore deviations from the assumptions.

Bengt Holmström, “On Incentives and Control in Organizations” (1977)
https://t.co/YjXxUkzURJ

papers.entry_text:
Chen and Roth, “Logs with Zeros?” (2025)
https://t.co/B6cnw28LaW

detailed_thread.text: Logarithms are ubiquitous in economics. They allow you to conveniently express percentage changes. However, your data includes a zero, the log representation breaks. It turns out that there is no solution fully robust to the choice of units used! 1/ https://t.co/ujw1U3EZwp

Suppose you’re testing the effect of pay on hours worked. You have random assignment of the program, so you’re not concerned about identification. You find that it increases hours by 2 hours per person on average — no problem, the average treatment effect is easy to represent.

But suppose it instead, from an average of 20 hours a week, raises hours worked by 10%. This is fine if everyone worked 20 hours, but what if some people worked 0 and others 40? How do you represent a percentage increase of zero?
The logarithm represents compounding increases, but famously is undefined at zero. https://t.co/6URwMxKCzK

A common solution is to transform the data by some fixed constant, so log(1+Y), or by the arcsinh(sqrt(1+Y^2)+Y). The problem is that this makes the percentage dependent upon the unit. The constant does not vary as the unit does.
Put practically, if you measure your hours per week, you will get different answers if you measure per year. Any effect can be made arbitrarily large or small with the choice of units.
All logs with zeroes must jettison one of these. https://t.co/05e7yuSggb
The tradeoffs are summarized here. https://t.co/4ToKsnXA29
And here’s why we care. https://t.co/chi0kA5YR6
We jettison the average of individual effects when we only care about total outcomes. This is akin to averaging hours to 20. https://t.co/3gsPcOGahw
If we think some margins matter more than others, we jettison units. https://t.co/ThJCEooxyK
We drop identification when we want to cover both extensive and intensive margins. https://t.co/u9DiH1NNdb

papers.entry_text:
Cailin Slattery, “Bidding for Firms” (2025)
https://t.co/MT2OyAVO53

detailed_thread.text: This paper really annoys me. It does so much right, and yet — it makes modeling choices that fundamentally make it impossible to answer the question it wants to answer. The welfare rise is an illusion. This paper is strong evidence subsidies are bad. 1/ https://t.co/NK0S0Z6iCA
So, what is good: the data collection is really excellent. This is the go-to source on all state subsidy deals, assembled by reading contemporary news articles. https://t.co/ibo7IfK1lE
The approach of the paper is to think of the competition as being akin to an English auction. It is possible to infer from this the valuations that states place on firms locating there.

The valuations for firms are trickier, because they locate both because of locational fundamentals, and because of the subsidy. It is really cool that you can infer the valuations on different characteristics from runner up bids, though I am concerned. https://t.co/ELvfKfUcTX

Specifically, there are a lot of possible characteristics, some of which are sure unobservable, you can’t observe local differences, everything changes over time, and you cut your sample up too many ways. Cross country regression problems all over again.

There is a path to subsidies raising welfare. If there are different private valuations for firms in different areas, then subsidy competition can allocate things better. Yet, he finds that it raises welfare by assuming in the answer.

If the valuations are the same everywhere for a project, then the highest bidder probably received an inaccurate signal. By assuming private values, you make the result inevitable — as he, quite fairly, acknowledges. https://t.co/gzeTWLIjTY
We are also making an enormous assumption that states are accurately maximizing social welfare. It seems obvious that they are not — these are notoriously acts of government largesse.
We are also not taking into account the tax burden placed upon others. Holding budget fixed, subsidies somewhere should raise taxes elsewhere, although of course it is not clear by how much.
His work does shed light on what would happen to the spatial allocation of plants if subsidies were banned. https://t.co/lG0MlFRH5J
Fundamentally I think he is struggling valiantly with a question that cannot be adequately answered. It involves too, its effects are too broad.

I am simply concerned that people will read the abstract, conclude that subsidies raise welfare, and go on their way. The fact that it barely does, when everything is slanted its way, is strong evidence the subsidies are bad.
I think this should be hammered in the abstract. You have a responsibility not to give policymakers the wrong idea.

Cailin Slattery, “Bidding for Firms” (2024)

https://t.co/wLw2rf4cPm

papers.entry_text:
Coglianese, Olsson, Patterson, “Monetary Policy and the Labor Market” (2025)
https://t.co/Or5uzT7aa5

detailed_thread.text: Everyone believes that monetary policy affects the real economy, but finding hard evidence is difficult. Changes in interest rates often come as a reaction to changes in the economy, and so need not cause it. This is one of the few well-identified pieces of evidence for it. 1/ https://t.co/2sUJryYu2I

What Coglianese, Olsson, and Patterson have is a plausibly exogenous change. The Swedish bank tightened monetary policy in 2010 unexpectedly, higher than would be justified under their previous rules. https://t.co/OxtEi3V8EJ

They did this by raising interest rates. If prices are unable to fully adjust, then this means that there will be less borrowing, less growth in money supply, and not enough dollars to employ all of the people who wish to work.
In the aftermath, unemployment increased by 2 percentage points relative to what would be expected. https://t.co/BSB6PYq9kM
As a consequence, investment and output fell. https://t.co/iul36gsKyh

To show that this supports the basic New Keynesian model with nominal price stickiness, they do need to trace out the exact effects. They show that the change was isolated to Sweden, and that the largest changes in employment were in domestic firms, ruling out exchange rates. https://t.co/JKY20XaNjy
Different sectors have different degrees of wage rigidity due to unions, and it was the more rigid sectors which saw greater job losses. https://t.co/jUSchWlI6P
Sometimes it is good to really solidly prove something which is widely believed, but poorly substantiated. File this in your head whenever someone wonders why prices don't just adjust.

Coglianese, Olsson, Patterson, "Monetary Policy and the Labor Market" (2024)
https://t.co/S9ywjd1J0r

papers.entry_text:
Nakamura, Steinsson, Sun, Villar, “The Elusive Costs of Inflation” (2018)
https://t.co/hJxyG8Q9Uj

detailed_thread.text: How should we conduct monetary policy? Remember that, if firms could costlessly adjust, monetary policy would be irrelevant. Therefore, optimal monetary policy depends on the precise way in which firms are unable to adjust. This paper is one of the key pieces of evidence. 1/ https://t.co/Ss2vLuWVBZ

We are going to have to detour a bit to explain the context. As noted, if prices could perfectly adjust, then monetary policy doesn't do anything. To fix this, we add in some form of stickiness. There are a few basic ways to do this.

The simplest way is to say that a fixed-but-random portion of agents in each period can change their prices, as if they're visited by the Calvo Fairy @FairyCalvo. We could also say that agents must pay a fixed cost to change their prices, or a hybrid of the two.

The fixed cost results in what's called (S, s) pricing, where firms only adjust when the deviation between the optimal and actual price is sufficiently far. (It's called state-dependent pricing, because it depends on the state of the world. Calvo pricing is time dependent.)

These two models deliver starkly different predictions about what happens when inflation is higher than expected. Under Calvo pricing, price changes have the same frequency, but become larger. Under (S, s), price changes are the same size, but more frequent.

What Nakamura, Steinsson, Sun, and Villar do is get really detailed data on price changes, and find that high inflation in the 70s and 80s didn't cause an increase in price dispersion. Instead, it just caused more frequent price changes.
I do want to quickly shout-out the data collection efforts. I only wish that this stuff was more publicly available. https://t.co/pV60QaU4V4
What this does is completely up end what optimal monetary policy should be. Rather than targeting inflation, we should instead target an aggregate, like nominal GDP. https://t.co/TImu7HPkCc

The reasoning for this is simple. Deadweight loss is a triangle, so it will increase quadratically as we get further away from the optimal price. If a shock occurs in one sector, if under Calvo pricing, we want to share it between all sectors.
If there are fixed costs, then we don't want to force other sectors to unnecessarily change their prices. Better to have it all in one sector.

Now, the price of goods are not the whole story. The labor market conforms more to the time-dependent pricing story. It's not clear which is the relevant things to care about, and it's a topic for future research. https://t.co/8i0mNS3w9z

Nakamura, Steinsson, Sun, Villar, "The Elusive Costs of Inflation" (2018)
https://t.co/ezNFfS9T8a

papers.entry_text:
De Ridder, Grassi, Morzenti, “The Hitchhiker’s Guide to Markup Estimation” (2025)
https://t.co/DLLtpxgbtO

detailed_thread.text: This is a good paper. I want to explain to you why it matters. To me, it says that certain recent papers are not salvageable on all of their claims, and the case that misallocation has increased over time is extremely unsettled. Long tweet thread coming: https://t.co/tmHaYG6eoe

So what is it that they're trying to do? We are extremely interested in knowing the aggregate markup of the economy, and how it has changed over time. The "markup" is the difference between price and marginal cost, which is the cost of producing an additional unit.

If there is a gap, then it means there exist people who would be willing to pay what it takes to make it, but cannot buy it. This is inefficient! We are not at the frontier of what we could produce, and transfers could improve outcomes.
A now famous paper, while we'll call DLEU for the authors, made the case that aggregate markups substantially increased over the past 40 years. https://t.co/crBp0zgC97

The markup is measured as a multiple of marginal cost, so here it means that the average markup increased from 20% above marginal cost, to 60% of marginal cost. That's pretty darn big! But big claims need strong evidence, and it was immediately criticized a dozen different ways. https://t.co/ifthxEb1dd

Assume that firms are cost-minimizing (as, of course, they are). For any given amount of output, they want to produce it at the least cost. Thus, given some production function, you can take a derivative with a Lagrangian. https://t.co/8iyYTVtFsc

The Lagrangian is itself marginal cost; thus we can substitute it into the formula for the markup. The elasticity of output with respect to an input, divided by the elasticity of revenue paid to that input, is the markup. https://t.co/4m9FxSkEIN

To put as intuitively as possible, if you increase labor 10%, and output increases by 15%, the markup is 1.5. Not producing could only be justified if you had some additional profit from restricting the amount of output.

Now, we need to know the production function. The concern here is that there are productivity shocks observed by the firm, but not by the econometrician. This would lead them to invest more, biasing the relationship between investment and output.

How they do this is, alas, relegated to the appendix, but it is crucial. The NAICS allows us to categorize firms into industries. As noted above, we can't simply regress output on quantities of particular factors. What we can do is use investment and input market conditions.

If the amount invested is always increasing as a function of unobserved productivity, then we can invert the equation, and express productivity as a function of *past* investment. We can then regress factors on output within an industry. https://t.co/yr5puGEhln
Technology obviously changes over time, so an incredibly attractive feature of this is that you can get a different production function for each industry, for each year.

Now, DLEU have a problem. They need to know quantities, but all they have are revenue. The problem with this is that firms affect revenue with their quantity decision, so it becomes self-reflective and meaningless. https://t.co/HNNyihDyrT

Define demand as some slope. That slope determines the elasticity of revenue with respect to an input, as well as the output elasticity of the input. It turns into itself over itself, or 1. It only accurately recovers the markup when the thing we want to study doesn't exist. https://t.co/bYqS99PuMS

So, now, we can finally talk about "A Hitchhiker's Guide to Markup Estimation". Their strategy is essentially check DLEU against the answer key. They have the complete data on French firms, so they know the right answer -- but how much can remain of DLEU? https://t.co/1JGo5xIP7E
Bond et al are correct that we cannot recover the level of markup from the DLEU approach. There is a correlation there, but it is not particularly impressive. https://t.co/AnPfDIQAJy
However, might we still be able to say something about trends? Arguably, yes. What the authors show is that, whatever the level is, you can say that it increased over time.

But, remember from earlier how flexible the DLEU approach was? They could estimate a different production function for each year. You can't do that anymore. If you want to say something about trends, you need to assume constant technology.
My read on this paper is, that in trying to save DLEU, they have buried it. Not all of the paper can last. You must have some claims, but not other claims.

De Ridder, Grassi, Morzenti, "The Hitchhiker's Guide to Markup Estimation" (2025) 
https://t.co/2fjRpNXvoQ

Pinging (potentially) interested parties: @EconTraina @Basile_G @pzoch5 @instrumenthull @ATabarrok @ben_golub @florianederer @ksangani8 @DBaqaee @nominalthoughts @Jabaluck @jasonfurman @maiamindel @BasilHalperin @DinaPomeranz @ChadSyverson

papers.entry_text:
Ben-Porath, Dekel, and Lipman, "Optimal Allocation with Costly Verification"
https://t.co/XO3g1aap19

detailed_thread.text: Suppose that we are trying to allocate a good. We are unable to make transfers, but we are able to verify the reports of agents for a fee. What is that optimal mechanism? How efficient can we be? The answer surprised me, and it will likely surprise you.

Let's set up the problem a bit more. Everyone has the same personal value over the item, but each of them possesses private information about how valuable I, the principal, would find allocating the item to them.

To have a concrete example, I'm trying to decide which scientist should use a piece of research equipment. All of them would like to use it, but their projects hold different value to me. Checking their projects is costly, so I'd ideally just like them to tell me the value.

One base case is just to never pay the cost of verification. In that case we just assign randomly. On the other extreme of cost, we verify everyone, which we'd do if the cost were zero. But, surely we can do better -- and we can.

The first insight to realize is that we don't need to verify anyone who wasn't going to win anyway. We only need to verify the person who reported the highest value. Since misreporting guarantees you don't get it, everyone reports accurately.

We can even save a bit more on the cost of verification, by never verifying if the highest value is below the cost of verifying. It's still incentive compatible to report if above, and it's no loss to randomize below.

The problem seems solved, but we can actually do better. It's possible to save just a little bit more on the cost of verification. What we instead implement is a random "favored agent". First, of the n agents, choose one at random, but keep it secret who it is.

Given your belief about the distribution from which the private values of the project are drawn, have a cut off "v". If the favored agent has a value above that, then you just give it to them without checking.
It's a clever paper! I didn't tweet the abstract in the first tweet, because I didn't want to give away the puzzle, but here it is now. https://t.co/lq9qlNHSrZ

Ben-Porath, Dekel, Lipman, "Optimal Allocation with Costly Verification" (2014)

https://t.co/I0i0cGChn4

papers.entry_text:
Mohamad Adhami, "Quantifying Knowledge Spillovers Using Firm and Product Dynamics" (2025)
https://t.co/snYTU4aRHg

detailed_thread.text: This is incredibly clever paper, which to me shows the importance of mathematical modeling. He uses some very clever tricks to answer an incredibly important question, and you're simply never going to think of these verbally. I shall, nevertheless, try to explain them. 1/ https://t.co/m6Ei2u1W3I

So, the question of interest is the portion of the value of new ideas which a company captures for itself. If ideas can be spread cheaply after the great expense of finding it, then we will spend too little on finding new ideas -- a big problem! But how do we measure the gap?

The standard path up to this point came first from Jaffe (1988), and peaked with Bloom, Schankerman, and Van Reenen (2013). You have patent filings. You can see how much patents which are "close by" affect the value of other companies. https://t.co/qePyAe5O6W

Of course, patents are not ideal. Many ideas are never patented, and many patents are unimportant. The point of this paper is to entirely sidestep counting up patents, and just look at what else should happen when knowledge spillovers are big or small.

Adhami builds off of Klette and Kortum (2004). Firms are defined as a collection of product lines, and firms invest in finding new lines and reducing the cost (and increasing the markup) of their present product line.
Take the case where products don't change quality over time. If there are spillovers (theta) then products enter and exit faster. https://t.co/WvOPdkgavL
With volatility, then some products will exit randomly. Since the markup evolves over time, age allows us to identify this. https://t.co/rh9d4bdaQN

The idea is that the spillovers are affecting cohorts of entrants most of all, and so firms and products cycle out. This is key, but reasonable. If knowledge spillovers affected everyone equally then firms would never go out of business! https://t.co/9IQrZWvZ4T
We don't have product level exits for everything, but we do have stuff for many retail goods. Adhami uses that to validate his claims about the firms, which are just collections of products.

He estimates the parameters that best fit the data, and is left with this. Note that a 16% difference between social and private optimums is conservative. If incumbents benefitted to, spillovers would be even larger. https://t.co/XKXyNCm2Ka

The paper takes strong assumptions, but it is clever. I'm a fan of mustering new methods for big questions. Adhami is on the job market, so go check out his website here. I can attest, having run into him, that he is a perfect gentleman.
https://t.co/1cpayWgmr7

papers.entry_text:
David Atkin, "The Caloric Costs of Culture" (2016)
https://t.co/3ncRA1wYNY

detailed_thread.text: When you move, you're unable to buy the products that you used to buy as easily, even as you still retain a preference for the old goods. That we're willing to pay a premium in America is no surprise -- but this same pattern happens among poor and starving households too! https://t.co/I3nxb6dtCf
He can tackle this because some of the Indian National Sample Surveys asked the requisite questions. https://t.co/cdF21JqrR2
This is a legacy of Mahalanobis, incidentally. For decades, India was poor -- but oh so scrupulously measured. https://t.co/f02Tkcsubm

Back to the paper, the first thing to establish are the facts. Moving is associated with reducing the total number of calories consumed by 1.6%, and this isn't just in the rich households who have room to give. https://t.co/esfCscdMpb

There are lots of reasons why this might be so. The argument for it being cultural preferences is that the size of the calorie tax depends on how closely related the local food is to their home food. https://t.co/kvhR6mEZ2q
These differences last for a really long time, and it's implausible that they simply don't know. https://t.co/OGi7O3iILP
This tracks with evidence from America, where people retain preferences related to their home states for decades after. https://t.co/eyD89mUqw4

This has the amusing implication that declining geographic mobility in the United States might be responsible for a (very, very small) fraction of our excess obesity. If only they moved away from home cooking and comfort food! https://t.co/Xr3VbPdVvc

David Atkin, "The Caloric Costs of Culture" (2016)

https://t.co/1ZRkjzkPRW

papers.entry_text:
Ted Miguel, "Poverty and Witch-Killing" (2003)
https://t.co/NTEMmRShJg

detailed_thread.text: Witch hunts are common in Tanzania. Why? The only thing that solidly explains it are negative income shocks from rainfall, which is consistent with being about competition for resources. https://t.co/VObcbGvlOz
Almost all killings are of elderly women during times of great stress. These estimates are an underestimate, because some women are warned by their children to flee. https://t.co/P3BOQLxi9l
This is socially approved of among the Sukuma people, who see it as a public service. https://t.co/L5GcUVgu7X
The reason it is women, not men, is because men hold the power. https://t.co/d11Xk7E4oo
The best evidence for this being due to income is that other catastrophes, like disease, don’t lead to this. It’s only rainfall.

Ted Miguel, “Poverty and Witch-killings” (2005)

https://t.co/rHF22Ir5nr

papers.entry_text:
Benziarti and Tazhitdinova, “Do Value-Added Taxes Affect International Trade Flows?” (2021)
https://t.co/7BK9mmrZf0

detailed_thread.text: Value-added taxes should be totally neutral with respect to trade, but the assumptions which are required rarely hold. How relevant is this? Are VATs approximately trade neutral? Yes! 1/ https://t.co/qUbDr0GxbJ

Value added taxes are in theory equivalent to sales taxes, but are implemented at each step in proportion to the value added. Firms then claim credits against the VAT on inputs they pay. https://t.co/poTlCe83WH
There are four main assumptions for it being neutral with respect to trade, all of which are likely to be broken. https://t.co/5PrCE3HqSC
However, in practice, changing VAT rates has no effect whatsoever on imports and exports. https://t.co/8C26rOBTMl

Benziarti and Tazhitdinova, “Do Value-Added Taxes Affect International Trade Flows?” (2021)
https://t.co/9DpYpBvZsA

papers.entry_text:
Atkin et al, "Organizational Barriers to Technology Adoption" (2017)
https://t.co/Z4RYVIBc0W

detailed_thread.text: Especially in the developing world, technological advancements are not used for puzzling reasons. When a group of Ivy League economists invented a better way to manufacture soccer balls, they couldn't get anyone to take it. https://t.co/yH9KuFwOEk

Sialkot, Pakistan, is one of the foremost centers of soccer ball production in the world, and it all started with  a chance encounter between a British soldier and a saddlemaker. By 2012, 135 firms employed around 36,000 people to produce 20% of the world's soccer balls. https://t.co/SYc6jhSljZ

Sialkot primarily makes higher quality, hand-stitched balls. One sews together 20 hexagons and 12 pentagons, made of an artificial leather called "rexine" glued to cloth. These materials account for much of the cost of the ball -- 46%, in fact.

The shapes are cut out of sheets by a skilled worker. They are then emblazoned with logos, and stitched around the inflatable bladder which holds the air within. Basically all firms share this production process.

The big area for improvement is in cutting. Hexagons tessellate, and so they can use the whole sheet, but pentagons do not. In cutting them, even the best cutters have to waste a quarter of the sheet. https://t.co/qzoNIb9dND
Compare the leftovers from cutting hexagons on the left, to the waste from cutting leftovers on the right. https://t.co/QRUW466tEW

They paid a consulting firm, but that was a waste. They didn't have any ideas. One of the co-authors, though, was watching Youtube, and with his wife developed a way to cut down waste by 6.25%. https://t.co/sAukAGrpoQ
The previous standard was just paired dies for cutting. https://t.co/3iFNUW9y6f
However, if you offset them, you can pack more hexagons. Turns out that this is actually well-known to mathematicians, and is conjectured to the be the optimal packing. https://t.co/oTQfsDXhaf
So, with the technology in hand, they gave it to firms at random. To other firms, they gave cash, and to others, they gave nothing. Pleased with themselves, they watched the take up.

Which, what takeup? Huh? What's happening?

The firms didn't use their technology. What's going on? Is it not useful? That doesn't really fit the facts -- the firms that did actually use it found it raised their profits.
One of the largest and most professional firms found it to be extremely good, and transitioned all of their production to it. https://t.co/gR5b66jDtP

What seems to be going on is that cutters are paid by piece, and in the adoption period, they slowed down slightly. Workers, resistant to change, then misled their employers about the efficiency, and the dies aren't adopted.

Therefore, they implemented a second experiment, where they gave cash subsidies and training to take up the new die. While the sample size gets small, it seems to have a positive effect on the firms. https://t.co/uJOvC50cuv

I think an underrated obstacle to economic growth is lack of imagination. People are really set in their ways, by default, and they miss out on beneficial opportunities. This need not even be about IQ -- in their experiment, take-up was uncorrelated with intelligence measures.

Atkin et al, "Organizational Barriers to Technology Adoption" (2017)
https://t.co/WMkXSfSSzW

papers.entry_text:
Levin and Smith, "Equilibrium in Auctions with Entry" (1994)
https://t.co/d4PvgKmbfj

detailed_thread.text: Normally, we think that more competition in an auction should raise revenues, but this intuition is flipped on its head when there are entry costs. Now, having fewer possible entrants is good, and sequential mechanisms are better than simultaneous auctions. https://t.co/3ZbYYpPBIk

Suppose there is an auction where symmetric firms pay a cost to enter, and then receive their valuation from a uniform distribution. Each firm therefore randomizes when they enter, and there is an equilibrium fraction of the time each firm enters q.
As a preliminary, we show that the seller's optimal mechanism is also the socially optimal mechanism. https://t.co/eERQa9Fze3

Since firms randomize, this implies that the realized number of firms bidding follows a binomial distribution. Sometimes you get a lot of firms, other times very few. The cool thing is that these outcomes are no symmetric!

As you add bidders drawing from [0, 1], the price paid goes 0, 0, .33, .5, .6, .66, .714, .75, and so on. (The formula is (n-1)/(n+1)). The seller would rather have two auctions with two bidders each, than one with 1 and another with 3. (.5 and 0).
So the optimal number of bidders is one where every firm enters every time. https://t.co/aSxObvRfDm

Levin and Smith, "Equilibrium in Auctions with Entry" (1994)

https://t.co/ZoitsV69Wz

papers.entry_text:
Moallemi and Robinson, "pm-AMM" (2024)
https://t.co/vWQPoLXmq6

detailed_thread.text: I was going to do a writeup on this, but Robinson did it better. The important thing is that assets need not follow a random walk at all. They can have large jumps, and *only* large jumps, which make them unsuitable for prediction markets. This shook me up. https://t.co/qIV7HjfKTc

An automated market maker allows people to buy and sell tokens which have value=1 if correct and value=0 if not. They automatically adjust the price when people buy one of the tokens according to a market scoring rule. The less liquidity, the bigger the change in odds.
To induce people to provide liquidity, they let people people buy a balanced proportion of Yes and No tokens. In return, that buyers gets a fee for every future purchase of a token.
This means they profit, on average, when there's a random walk. The price goes up, it goes down, it does not have any tendency one way or another.
But prediction markets aren't like this. They tend to have large and definite jumps. Consider a soccer game -- when a goal is scored, the win probability of team that scored jumps discontinuously.
As we approach the end of the game, the volatility of the token depends on how close the score is. There isn't constant volatility over time.

The contribution of the paper is to contribute an alternative formula for AMMs for prediction market usage. What it did for me, though, was shake me out of an unexamined certainty that all things everywhere follow a random walk. They don't have to!!

papers.entry_text:
Read and Loewenstein, "Diversification Bias" (1995)
https://t.co/ghjUBqSrcO

detailed_thread.text: A really nifty group of experiments -- Read and Loewenstein found that people would seek variety if given a choice of taking two things at one time, but would choose the same thing each time if given two separate choices! https://t.co/acyrNL2ykU

The coolest of the experiments was a Halloween night, where they saw what trick-or-treaters took when they could take two candy bars at once, versus when they took one candy bar from two houses. https://t.co/jPw8iSWar9
The experimental procedure needed an emergency modification mid-way through. https://t.co/fp6cThGz1p
Everyone who could choose two at the same time chose one of each. Only half did this when they chose sequentially. https://t.co/Q7B9jO71zX
They have a lot of clever ideas for how this explains things in the world. https://t.co/vlGQYEh437
It's a fun study! I like it! Sue me!

Read and Loewenstein, "Diversification Bias" (1995)
https://t.co/gfNJ73OZlg

papers.entry_text:
Buchholz, Shum, Xu, "Rethinking Reference Dependence" (2025)
https://t.co/HSxECSI57c

detailed_thread.text: Contrary to some earlier work, taxi drivers behave like any other worker. They do not engage in "income targeting", where they choose an amount they want to make in a day and work till that is reached, which would imply a negative response of hours worked to income shocks. 1/ https://t.co/ZavgKLIehb

Back in 1997, a group of leading behavioral economists thought they had found that the elasticity of labor supply was negative, which would track best with workers choosing a target. If wages are unexpectedly high, then they will work less. https://t.co/izbjN8mBrx

Rather than the small, hand collected sample of the original paper, they have 27 million trips between July and September, 2012. They're able to show what's going on is simple -- productivity declines over time.

Since taxi cab drivers are getting worse as their shift, and thus their earnings, are increasing, it will look at though they are stopping because of increasing wages for the day. https://t.co/U49QfTrY9X
Second, wages are negatively correlated over time. This is because if take a long trip, you'll get more money, but then you'll have to drive back to the middle of town. https://t.co/778JyGS8Gz
The Bronx is just a terrible place for taxi cab drivers. https://t.co/G8u0ErcgTJ
This is also a really cool test -- they look at people in the exact same place who've worked the same number of hours, but differ in their earnings. There's no effect. https://t.co/rhPLnBdtA1
I'm actually not convinced the dynamic model is actually necessary or adding anything. It confirms the story they're telling, though.

Buchholz, Shum, Xu, "Rethinking Reference Dependence" (2025)
https://t.co/E0iDmmHEak

papers.entry_text:

https://t.co/u8PC650amT

detailed_thread.text: In Norway, you can look up anyone’s tax information, including their salary. Doing this is extremely popular, and people will make leaderboards by income. Around the same time, the relationship between income and happiness got steeper. Might they be connected? https://t.co/jkqKcQKM6B
I do not think this paper by any means proves the case. It’s an illustration of a broad story. We can definitely show, though, that it was *extremely* popular. https://t.co/4ZRBGI2XR5
People looked at tax records more than they looked up the weather! https://t.co/rToIk6uNcH
And of course, people are using it for looking up their friends, or perhaps rivals. https://t.co/KJGAVNWFCG

We would predict that the steepness of the relationship between income and happiness increase in the immediate aftermath, and increase more among those who had more internet access, and it did. https://t.co/imtrimD6Fd
It also didn’t increase in Germany, which did not do the same policies. https://t.co/OeWqHsOEI9

So what does this imply? Given that we are envious, it may not be the case that social media is good for us. By learning about the success of others, we are made less happy. But that is a case that takes more evidence.

papers.entry_text:
Athey, Levin, and Seira, "Comparing Open and Sealed Bid Auctions" (2011)
https://t.co/balttfSpgc

detailed_thread.text: A paper in the empirical auctions literature I quite like is Athey, Levin, and Seira (2011). Auctions are easy to estimate when the model is idealized, but what can we do to deal with all the complications the real world throws at us? I think this paper is masterful. 1/ https://t.co/VjfHAEN3zf
The US Forest Service sells billions in tracts of land. This is done by auction, after announcing the sale and giving prospective buyers a tour. https://t.co/n0egChHvRY
The key bit of institutional background making this possible is the US Forest Service randomizing the manner of auctions, from sealed bid to open outcry. https://t.co/zSHV8gPQXh

Under idealized conditions, this should not change the price paid, but there are lots of reasons this might break. For starters, with entry costs, sealed bid will raise revenue because only the expected number of bidders is known. They can't just bid 0 when they're the only one.

Second, bidders are not identical. Mills own their own equipment, while loggers do not. This gives the mills a different distribution of valuations, and we need to account for this and estimate them separately.

Finally, bidders might be colluding with each other. It's way easier to do this in an open auction, because you can't ever defect. If you choose to bid above what you said you would, the auction would just keep going. How can we test for the presence of collusion?

Here are the preliminaries defining the model. The important thing is that the mills are of the strong bidder type, and not only will the highest draw dominate the weak bidder, but the second highest will too. https://t.co/av8IyQbIiS

Start with the sealed bid auctions. These are first price auctions, so the amount you bid is shaded down. Mills will shade down their bid by even more, implying possible inefficiency. https://t.co/F4vf8P4K4k
The ascending auction is efficient. The person with the highest value will win the auction, because it will continue rising until only that person is left.

Now onto entry. Mills having higher values is neat because it means that all mills must enter, if there are any number of loggers entering. This greatly simplifies estimation for entry by the mills. https://t.co/IIlJ4RNoHD

Section II.F. was extremely clarifying, and well worth pondering. In auction estimation, there are always modeling choices which one will have to make. You will not have the data for everything you want to do. https://t.co/HLhEvMGaGY

What makes me more inclined to believe their work, although not certain, is that they hold out part of their sample, and then see if the model can hold out of sample. Note though that while evidence, it is not decisive.

You could imagine splitting a sample in half, and getting (in expectation) two identical samples. Of course your model would still explain things! We're interested in if you can make big counterfactual claims.
Anyway, sealed bidding leads to far more loggers entering. What can explain this? Collusion! https://t.co/VIY45YIw8j
What randomization does is rule out some possibilities of model misspecification. https://t.co/rb7D6Y7zE8
They make distributional assumptions about heterogeneity, and validate some of the assumptions in their prior work. https://t.co/o63MVVi2fA

Now they can evaluate the differences between the auction formats (or any auction format), since they have entry costs, a distribution of valuations, and the distribution conditional on characteristics. https://t.co/p0dStA94Hk
All in all, sealed bidding is slightly better, although the magnitudes of the gains are somewhat small. https://t.co/vT95D1cRpW

Athey, Levin, and Seira, "Comparing Open and Sealed Bid Auctions" (2011)
https://t.co/PKDbW84Sct

papers.entry_text:
Ostrovsky and Schwarz, "Reserve Prices in Internet Advertising Auctions" (2023)
https://t.co/OWq1USLmN0

detailed_thread.text: Something which I would like to see a lot more of is using a structural model to make counterfactual predictions, and then experimentally validating them. This paper is an important step toward that. 1/ https://t.co/0P4ksv8LH2

Advertising on search results is a multi-billion dollar business. They are generally pay per click, but they will weight your bid by your prior history and apparent quality so that people can't bid terrible ads to block opponents. https://t.co/LfaU2zhEEp

This is done by a second price auction, so the highest bidder pays the next highest price. This means that reporting your true valuation is optimal, and you don't have any incentive to misreport. (We are glossing over strategic considerations). https://t.co/yi2phlD0E8

So what's the optimal reserve price? One would think that, with many bidders, the reserve price quickly becomes irrelevant. However, when auctions involve multiple units, it stays relevant. https://t.co/2iSPqxEVio
See, for instance, this example. https://t.co/xZUW7DZp4Y

It is simply not feasible to do non-parametric assumptions. We must make assumptions about distribution. Here, they assume a lognormal distribution of values, and our goal is pull out the distribution. https://t.co/2ZA1bGVmJg
Estimation was essentially by guess and check, for 460,000 keywords. https://t.co/nQo0jaPkKj
To validate this, they then ran the experiment. Revenue was greatly increased. https://t.co/W4J4Fdh9Pr
This paper took absolutely forever to publish. The experiment was in 2008, and it was done with *Yahoo*, a company which has since become completely irrelevant. https://t.co/2eVD4bdSDO

Ostrovsky and Schwarz, "Reserve Prices in Internet Advertising Auctions" (2023)
https://t.co/ER3l7QG7L8

papers.entry_text:
David Lucking-Reiley, "Field Experiments on the Effects of Reserve Prices in Auctions" (2006)
https://t.co/EjYaZMG2JD

detailed_thread.text: Optimally set reserve prices should increase the revenue to a seller in an auction, but it need not always. This quite clever PhD thesis, on reserve prices on Magic: the Gathering cards, was the first real-life experimental evidence for effective reserve prices. https://t.co/K9MQQLGY95

Magic: the Gathering is a trading card game. I used to play it all the time. I only quit in February 2024, and have been almost two years sober. Players buy cards, generally on the secondary market, in order to construct decks.
When it came out in 1993, this was before eBay. Players bought and sold cards in a newsgroup which became the highest-volume such group on the entire internet. https://t.co/5d8AJobqJY
Enterprising computer programmers soon wrote automatic guides to the prevailing prices on the market place, which Lucking-Reiley would rely upon later. https://t.co/ibtYwTr0fm
To do the experiment, he simply became a trader himself. This whole part is honestly quite charming. https://t.co/Lw71BkkhyA
This was reasonably profitable for him, netting about $400 -- and a considerable advantage over lab experiments where you have to pay people! https://t.co/is5rZrH9ot
I presume he recruited his younger sister into it for help buying and selling. https://t.co/a1Ma4zNsPp
As predicted, reserve prices decreased the number of bidders and the probability of sale. https://t.co/2YBlZkBcwV
This was more than made up for by the increase in revenue. https://t.co/iOpE7bLA4q
Oddly, revenues had several local maximum. What seems to be going on is that people used the reserve price as a signal of quality, which is an interesting anomaly. https://t.co/KXiRGXl6CV

This is a really cool illustration of what an enterprising graduate student can do, although I fear that the expectations are now so high that collecting your own data is no longer feasible. We can't tell apart CV and IPV though, alas.
What one could do is choose to alert different sizes of pools of potential bidders to the auction.

David Lucking-Reiley, "Field Experiments on the Effects of Reserve Prices in Auctions" (2006)
https://t.co/70WSpjQXsV

papers.entry_text:
Yunmi Kong, "Not Knowing the Competition: Evidence and Implications for Auction Design" (2020)
https://t.co/GTSFVAAfUH

detailed_thread.text: A downside of conventional open outcry auctions is that sometimes only one bidder shows up. When that happens, the goods -- in this case, plots of land for oil and gas drilling in New Mexico -- get sold at the minimum price, at a considerable loss for the seller! https://t.co/9UN2HbAuq4

What best explains this is risk-aversion, so long as their are private values rather than common values. It'd be worth discussing what those are, and why they have the opposite implications. https://t.co/tuS29JM1cm

Private values means that bidders draw their value at random from some distribution. In a first price auction, where everyone pays their bid if they win, this incentivizes people to underreport their true valuation, but an honest mechanism exists.

By contrast, common values means that everyone will get the same value from the good, they simply have different signals of its quality. This means that they will always adjust their bids to avoid the "winner's curse" -- if they won by bidding their valuation, it was too high.

Risk aversion means that bidders dislike uncertainty over payoffs. They'll accept a lower accepted value for higher certainty. With common values, risk aversion increases the amount they decrease their bid by, reducing the price paid.

Risk aversion means the opposite, weirdly enough, for private values. People bid closer to their values. They are trading off between a pay off of zero for not winning, and some pay off for winning. They're reducing the size of the payoff to avoid the 0.

This leads Yunmi Kong to conclude that there are no common values at play in the auction, which is surely weird. He justifies this on the grounds that the spread of signals is really small, and thus irrelevant. https://t.co/nmaD2XOEIi
If you would like my thoughts on empirical auction estimation, and the many choices of specification which are possible, be sure to read today's article: https://t.co/4eeeSljDY9

Yunmi Kong, "Not Knowing the Competition: Evidence and Implications for Auction Design" (2020)
https://t.co/RIgdNHd4VA

papers.entry_text:
Felix Chopra, "Media Persuasion and Consumption" (2023)
https://t.co/d7emiWpO6q

detailed_thread.text: Listening to the Dave Ramsey Show actually makes people consume less and save more. Using the rollout of the show to different media markets over time, he shows that exposure to the radio show reduces household expenditures by 5.4%. (!!) 1/ https://t.co/8VJ1wLwcet
If you are not already familiar, the Dave Ramsey Show airs in the afternoon, and is relentlessly focused on personal finances and avoiding debt for consumer purchases. https://t.co/JULPkH7pAS

The central strategy of the paper is that different areas  received the show at different times. If the choice to expand to the area was uncorrelated with the future change in spending habits, then the change in trend after is the true effect of the show.
He has some qualitative evidence for this, as a manager for the show does not report caring about anything other than the pure number of markets they're in. https://t.co/x4KJIiGVS6
He still controls for observables, and tests with machine learning whether there is anything predictive. https://t.co/xkVgXPplPP

With data from Nielsen Homescan, which collects detailed data on consumer purchases, he finds the average household in the area reduces expenditures 1.4%. Since many households don't listen, the effect on the treated is larger. https://t.co/u6PO34ZR3l
This adjustment comes largely through the extensive margin, of purchasing fewer varieties of goods. https://t.co/PEM0gFXYlm
The difference-in-differences chart of the number of items, if you believe it. https://t.co/fdiCesA5b3

Alright, how about another robustness check -- we'll use geographic variation and how it affects signal strength. You may remember this instrument from Yanigazawa-Drott's paper on the Rwandan Genocide. https://t.co/bsUAKZI8no
There's a lab experiment, where five minutes of listening to Dave Ramsey changes people's attitudes toward debt. I gloss over this.

This is a job market paper, so it's a bit overdone insofar as it throws absolutely everything at the question, and just adds more and more. E.g. we want to see that the stuff he talks about is constant, so how about scraping three thousand hours cause ... why not? https://t.co/l5LPwRoJnN

That is not at all a criticism of the paper, but a criticism of the incentives the job market gives people. I think we're spending more time on these papers than is socially useful in order to signal skills and ability.

And honestly the sort of extension we would really want is to figure out the macroeconomic consequences of this, but we don't get it -- it's not his area. Why not collaborate? Well, if you did, it would hardly be a job market paper.

Felix Chopra, "Media Persuasion and Consumption" (2023)
https://t.co/FAKCzpYcqQ

papers.entry_text:
Durante, Pinotti, Tesei "The Political Legacy of Entertainment TV" (2019)
https://t.co/D62HdhprBJ

detailed_thread.text: The basis of Silvio Berlusconi's political power was his TV network Mediaset. Using the staggered roll-out of the network, the authors find that watching television made young people stupider -- and thus, more likely to vote for Berlusconi. 1/ https://t.co/0w4L9G9EW5
The fundamental argument behind the paper is that the places where transmitters were placed, conditional upon controls like population and income, were unrelated to future propensity to vote for him.
There is support for no intentional selection -- by general agreement, Berlusconi didn't intend to enter politics until the political cataclysm that was Mani Pulite. https://t.co/yjqx7IoUAV
They're able to to also compare what the signal strength would have been without geographic obstacles to what it actually was. https://t.co/vmO8KfaHHJ

So how did exposure affect outcomes? And through what channel? The change in voting was driven by the old and the young, not the middle aged. For the old, it was likely due to them continuing to watch the channel when the news programs favored Berlusconi.

I've written before about a similar persuasive effect from Fox News.

https://t.co/tsLFFnXHVw
For the young, though, it's much funnier. Watching Mediaset made you stupid. Stupid people vote for Berlusconi. https://t.co/GewmCBF3di
I'm somewhat agog at this paragraph being published in the AER. https://t.co/33zq7y8p3n

Durante, Pinotti, Tesei "The Political Legacy of Entertainment TV" (2019)
https://t.co/Tkkoo71TN2

https://t.co/uzGtSvMFjY

detailed_thread.text: One of the theories for advertising being good is that it allows people to learn about new things. This is a really cool example of this in action -- people start making more specific searches after they learn about the possible ways a good can exist. 1/ https://t.co/Lu0eTiyc0B

They are collaborating with a platform which pretty clearly seems to be Alibaba to do this. When consumers search, they are given the ability to specify what they mean, with the aid of pictures. https://t.co/G52im4OIRg
There is no immediate effect on the customers exposed to this, but in the long run they begin to complete more orders and buy more. Why? Their searches become more specific over time.
