papers.entry_text:
Bradley Shapiro, "Positive Spillovers and Free Riding in Advertising of Prescription Pharmaceuticals" (2018)
https://t.co/qpeLypAanv

detailed_thread.text: Everybody dislikes advertising, but it's actually possible for there to be not enough of it. Bradley Shapiro is doing some of the leading work in measuring the effects of advertising, and at least in the pharmaceutical industry, people would have been better off with more. 1/ https://t.co/1nSG5G64j5

In the 1990s, the FDA revised its rules regarding the advertisement of prescription medication. It was now possible to put ads on TV, and naturally there was a great boom in ads, particularly for depression medicines.

Obviously, where a company advertises is affected by the likelihood of that place buying. What he exploits is that companies buy ads in bulk for whole markets, and these markets change sharply at somewhat arbitrary borders. https://t.co/hR0a43GuKG

We adjust for the effect of the time in which the ad takes place, and we adjust for the effect of the place, and only then we compare the effect of advertising. He has data on prescriptions written as an outcome variable.
Note that our model is really only taking in the firm side of things. If there is some cost of annoyance to the consumer, we cannot possibly measure that. Instead, we are only measuring purchases.

So there are three possible worlds. First, advertisements steal business from competitors. In that world, there is too much entry of ads, and we would be better off socially with fewer. Second, ads raise only the business of your own company, without affecting others.
Third, and this is what he finds, advertising raises your own business, but also that of your competitors. People who see a Zoloft ad are more likely to get Prozac.
Shapiro's later work suggests that in general, we're in the first world, and there's too much advertising. But it's important to remember that positive spillovers are possible.

Bradley Shapiro, "Positive Spillovers and Free Riding in Advertising of Prescription Pharmaceuticals" (2018)
https://t.co/CuWbHVmjQ6

papers.entry_text:
Bradley Shapiro, "Promoting Wellness or Waste?" (2020)
https://t.co/bVzCy3Zoey

detailed_thread.text: One might naturally be concerned that the prescription of drugs due to advertisements leads to wasteful prescriptions on people who don't really need it. Bradley Shapiro, in followup work, shows that this is  unlikely to be the case. https://t.co/YiTyhB0VIr https://t.co/SEKhZl3vpK

Remember, the identification strategy is the division of the country into media zones. If you're buying ads for Cleveland, you'll get a sharp drop off in ads as you go near Columbus. https://t.co/0HB4BX27oL
If anti-depressants worked, we should see a decrease in days absent from work, which we do indeed see. https://t.co/78RlkHDqX2

These wage benefit alone are 24 times the cost of the antidepressants. It does not appear to be due to less vacation. Nor do there appear to be an increase in adverse side effects. https://t.co/36uUo0GOUs

Bradley Shapiro, "Promoting Wellness or Waste?" (2020)
https://t.co/Dopd9qam57

papers.entry_text:
Shapiro, Hitsch, Taubman, "TV Advertising and Effectiveness" (2021)
https://t.co/KRvizg4ArJ

detailed_thread.text: While advertising for pharmaceuticals may be effective, this does not appear to be the case for most brands. Across hundreds of brands, the return on investment to advertising appears to be negative. 1/ https://t.co/LxOwxV7W7p
The amount of advertising bought it obviously endogenous. What they use to get around this are discontinuities at the border of geographic regions, like this example in Ohio. https://t.co/rSwqpANK6K
Ads are bought well in advance of when they are to be broadcast, making it implausible for them to be reacting to sudden changes. https://t.co/0fRiq3qR7k

We also need to be careful to observe the long run effects as best as we can. They think of viewing advertising as adding a stock of "advertising capital", which depreciates over time as people forget about it. (Specifically, it lasts a year)
To get outcomes, we turn to the Nielsen RMS. They have incredible data on what people purchase, both at households and at the store level. https://t.co/3C68q580Ur

Their main result is that the elasticity of sales with respect to own advertising is considerably smaller than expected. Note that this does not take a position on what is socially optimal, because we do not consider business stealing or positive spillovers at all here. https://t.co/WgnLxBNuVw
Notably, not including controls will make the effects seem larger than they actually are. Ads are targeted toward people who were more likely to buy anyway. https://t.co/h8BWolN2Na
Most brands, if you believe their results, should be advertising less. They are losing money by doing so. https://t.co/tU3jrgLQlB

Why do they advertise so much? It's worth pointing out how much uncertainty there is about the effects of any given campaign. Consumers spend not much on average, but when they do, they spend a lot. There is a high standard deviation, which makes it hard to distinguish true… https://t.co/2JNUm1yfCt

I appreciate a paper which is willing to say, though, that businesses are frankly wrong, and they are losing money due to being wrong. Details on all their robustness checks can be found here:
https://t.co/L31bVIyVvq

Shapiro, Hitsch, Taubman, "TV Advertising and Effectiveness" (2021)
https://t.co/hhfGxYOOaR

papers.entry_text:
Barwick, Chen, Fu, Li "Digital Distractions with Peer Influence" (2025)
https://t.co/UgmcTUAqQa

detailed_thread.text: This is *the best* study we have on the effect of phones on academic performance, and an absolute must read. It's really bad for you! It reduces grades and lowers wages coming out of school, with doubling app usage reducing wages by 1.5%, or $135 a year. 1/ https://t.co/dCXyUkcL8W

It's historically been difficult to measure the effect of videogame usage because it's endogenous. Someone who is already less attentive might play videogames in class -- but who is to say whether it is the videogames causing it, or the inattention which caused both?

To do so, we need exogenous variation, and we have three sources of such. The first are roommates. At this university (which I believe to be Jinan University, but officially it's anonymous), roommates are randomly assigned. If your roommate plays, they share the game with you.

The next is a shift-share instrument. Yuanshen, or Genshin Impact, was an enormous hit in China. The argument the study makes is that people who already played a lot were more likely to pick the game up, and then play more. https://t.co/5wM9gi9EFD

Finally, in 2019, China restricted gaming for minors. While some of the sample are minors, that's not the interesting variation -- rather, what is interesting is their friend group. Some people had more underage friends, which would make gaming less appealing. https://t.co/henQaC8aX7

This is all possible because they have incredible data. They have the complete phone records of over 6,000 students, as well as their contacts, who they play with, their academic records, and their time in and out of class every day.
As expected, app usage substantially reduces one's grades in class. For context, GPA is out of a 100 with a mean of 78 and an s.d. of 6.6. https://t.co/FvYJ1NBKZZ
Further, when leaving college this substantially reduces one's wage, largely through the extensive margin of being more likely to be unemployed. https://t.co/6jtY6WpwOH
People just didn't apply to as many jobs as they would have otherwise. https://t.co/77xz2FDCyL
Following the release of Yuanshen, people just stopped showing up to class on time, if at all. https://t.co/Vc416632b5
No IV here, but we see worse sleep outcomes. https://t.co/AshCV6lSdg
This is an extremely important paper, and I am pleased to see it be published in the Quarterly Journal of Economics, which is the best general-interest journal.

Barwick, Chen, Fu, Li "Digital Distractions with Peer Influence" (2025)
https://t.co/LXdIEiUPD5

papers.entry_text:
Gennaro, Ash, "Televised Debates and Emotionality in Politics" (2022)
https://t.co/Ku4RHEB5JC

detailed_thread.text: C-SPAN let the people see what happens on the floor, but what they saw wasn't the same as before. Politicians became more emotional, and pandered. This came both due to politicians changing their rhetoric, and to more emotional Congressmen entering. 1/ https://t.co/h0pMbCsfbZ
The first test is to compare rhetoric in the House and Senate. The Senate would not get cameras installed until 1986, so we can compare the difference in changes afterwards. https://t.co/PVO9rASkuL

The second approach is to use the channel number in different locations as an instrument. Some districts got C-SPAN at different numbers, which in turn affected which channel they watch. https://t.co/NFOMLwmhBy
Yes, really, channel position does indeed impact how much people watch it. https://t.co/ZNCcaYcqwm
This, in turn, results in Congressmen whose districts watch C-SPAN more choosing more emotional rhetoric.

Might this be justified by causing Representatives to make more effort toward serving their constituents? This doesn't appear to be the case -- Congressmen get more emotional, but don't do more on committees. https://t.co/AdKJ1KCSuE
Having more newspaper coverage encourages accountability without emotion, while C-SPAN gets you emotion without effort.

Gennaro, Ash, "Televised Debates and Emotionality in Politics" (2022)
https://t.co/e0kgVfpeAh

papers.entry_text:
Leander Heldring, “The Origins of Violence in Rwanda” (2021)
https://t.co/7ihskK7uxK

detailed_thread.text: The case of Rwanda illustrates how the state is a double-edged sword — it cuts down on small-scale violence, but increases the risk of calamity. Places with a longer history of the state are less likely to have petty rebellions, but more likely to have genocide. 1/ https://t.co/6k5BItqB5Z

The instrument here is distance from the capital of the old state before colonialism. Naturally, places closer were incorporated first. The capital was moved to Kigali by the Belgians, leaving the old capital a backwater. https://t.co/VHFKhjdRZ5
As you can see, the move was quite substantial. https://t.co/GJSnZV7pSy

Places with a longer history of state control were less likely to rebel, and more likely to participate in genocide. Both associations became stronger when considering radio ownership. https://t.co/7VZSIHHaq7
Now how about mechanisms? People who lived in areas which had had a longer exposure to states were more likely to believe that one should follow the rules. https://t.co/zvLNVioZWg
They were more likely to participate in the state, but less likely to contradict it. https://t.co/gMeIAMiYHC

Leander Heldring, “The Origins of Violence in Rwanda” (2021)
https://t.co/Gl50kdwqJ3

papers.entry_text:
Melnikov, Schmidt-Padilla, Sviatschi, "Gangs, Labor Mobility, and Development" (2025)
https://t.co/7eVzalvaIo

detailed_thread.text: There's a reason Bukele is so celebrated in El Salvador -- the gangs, MS-13 and Barrio-18, were so awful that they reduced the wages of the people who lived in their zone by more than 50%! People were too afraid of being shot to leave their zone for better jobs. 1/ https://t.co/wKJjMhNDrZ

The El Salvadorean gang situation was an accidental creation of the United States -- when we dumped hundreds of gang members hardened by the streets of 90s Los Angeles, El Salvador could not keep up. https://t.co/gNB6bz7vAN

A plausible consequence of the American upbringing is that they kept a very tight control of territory, demarcated by natural boundaries of streets and avenues. These boundaries were quite stable over time (they confirm this with retrospective surveys). https://t.co/Vt0MahqEUZ
These gangs are, to be frank, dirt-poor. They do not have drug money to subsidize their operations. https://t.co/YEhBwfHmhK
Instead, they rely on extortion of their territory, and of surrounding areas. https://t.co/DhLuBiDSB8

The authors use the clearly demarcated territory to run a spatial regression discontinuity design. Can they do this? Can they isolate just the effect of gang control separate from extortion? They argue they can -- nearby places get extorted, but without checkpoints.
First -- the people in gang controlled areas are wayyyyy poorer. Both income, and every indicator of consumption, are way worse. https://t.co/vVxL0n7C7x
Even on the extremely local level, there is a sharp jump at the border. https://t.co/xRq6Jn7O2G
There was no such poverty in the 1992 census, before the arrival of the gangs.

Just across the border, people are not free to leave. It's not that they are immobile -- cell phone ping data indicates that they are just as able to move within the boundary -- but they cannot cross the border. https://t.co/f2QK1tNLe8
The gangs are paranoid. They do not like people coming and going. https://t.co/HIcBNP0RhK

An obvious concern is that it's simply migration -- the partial equilibrium effects cannot be added up to find the general equilibrium effect. However, 77% of people had lived in the same place their whole life. Restricting the sample to them changes nothing. https://t.co/0NCmdASRPF
I hope that people might consider what happens when people are unable to leave abusive governments to work better jobs.

Melnikov, Schmidt-Padilla, Sviatschi, "Gangs, Labor Mobility, and Development" (2025)
https://t.co/aVc01ZwPW3

papers.entry_text:
Carranza, Donald, Grosset-Touba, Kaur, "The Social Tax" (2025)
https://t.co/8t2pVszaV4

detailed_thread.text: A serious problem in the developing world is that the moment anyone makes anything, their family and relations show up demanding a claim. The "social tax" is so large that 60% of workers take a bank account they can't use for months, just to keep others from knowing! 1/ https://t.co/mOLOgkwa5s

The experiment is at a cashew processing plant, where workers scrape the plant husk off the nut. The compensation scheme is a pure piece-rate system -- workers are paid by the kilogram. These are almost entirely women.

The experimenters offered a savings account which paid no interest, and prevented you from accessing it for some number of months. This account would only be for income above expected. Unlike wages, which are paid publicly, no one would know how much got deposited.
Remarkably, this was extremely popular. 60% of offered workers took up the account, despite the fact that (with discount rates) they are essentially choosing to lose money.
This makes more sense when one realizes how ubiquitous demands for money are. They can only be refused when you can credibly claim to have nothing to give. https://t.co/Em9sLj4jl9
Being offered a private account causes worker earnings to go up by 9.4%. https://t.co/Ius9SHr5yU

To put this in perspective, we can calculate how large the implicit social tax on earnings is. Obviously, we don't know the exact labor supply elasticity, but we can estimate a range of somewhere between a 20 and 65 percent tax on earnings. This is astonishingly large!

The range is coming due to uncertainty over what the right estimate is for how much people respond to a tax. Either way, it's harmful -- either the tax is small, but especially distortionary, or it's large if not especially distortionary.

They establish that it's the privacy, not self-control benefits, which are responsible for the results by offering a "public" account, which alerts their family. (They claim that this is for promotional purposes). Wayyyyy fewer people take this up. https://t.co/1UeDk3cH8l
These basic results apply to the Black community in America too. There, too, is considerable pressure to redistribute good times to others. https://t.co/oPCslm6i1E

One more thing. It's possible for transfers to be welfare enhancing in the presence of credit constraints and a lack of insurance. But the private accounts didn't change transfers at all! So this *must* be welfare improving. https://t.co/dwdbOmomjD
Really big fan of this study. We have not considered enough the possibility of a culture of generosity holding back development. So be stingier!

Carranza, Donald, Grosset-Touba, Kaur, "The Social Tax" (2025)
https://t.co/ecSKRJGF3z

papers.entry_text:
Cramton, Gibbons, Klemperer, "Dissolving a Partnership Efficiently" (1987)
https://t.co/3WExob94S1

detailed_thread.text: Famously, there is no mechanism which can guarantee an efficient outcome when one person owns an asset fully. However, if people have partial ownership, we *can* implement a mechanism which efficiently allocates the good to only one person. This is a really cool result! 1/ https://t.co/ry7gf1Goro

We have n bidders who own a share r of the good, and has some valuation drawn from a known distribution. Owing to the revelation principle, we only need to worry about incentive compatible mechanisms, which is as follows: https://t.co/91qNlhQo8m

Suppose we have symmetrical bidders. Then, everyone reports their valuation. Everyone pays their bid, and then gets back the average of everyone else's bid. The highest bidder gets the good, and pays a larger amount than anyone else.

If bidders are asymmetric, then we precede it with side payments in order to bring them to even. Small shares pay more, and large shares get paid. Given the knowledge of the valuation, we can make them even.
This doesn't work for Myerson-Satterthwaite type problems, where ownership shares are 1 and 0, because it would violate individual rationality.

Cramton, Gibbons, Klemperer, "Dissolving a Partnership Efficiently" (1987)
https://t.co/Iz0ChvBSE0

papers.entry_text:
Dubois, Gandhi, Vasserman, "Bargaining and International Reference Pricing in the Pharmaceutical Industry" (2022)
https://t.co/MLMjBLUdOn

detailed_thread.text: When the rest of the world pays lower prices than us on pharmaceuticals, a go-to option is to say "we pay the same prices as everywhere else". What people fail to consider, though, is that such a policy doesn't lead to lower prices here -- it leads to no drugs sold elsewhere! 1/ https://t.co/WljoBs0zEU

Having your prices in Canada affect the prices paid in America actually gives you a stronger bargaining position. Depending on the exact parameters, it can even increase a company's total profits. https://t.co/gIWfUxyP86

To be exact, though, we need to estimate the parameters, which we do through a Berry-Levinsohn-Pakes (1995) type random-coefficients logit demand model for the demand side, and by finding the Nash bargaining equlibrium on the supply side.

So, in BLP, individuals have "random" utility from the good beta, and disutility from the cost alpha. These coefficients are drawn from some distribution, which the coefficient describes. Xi represents unobserved shocks, which are conveniently distributed. https://t.co/eT4JrsSlql

To trace out the demand curves, we need shifters of supply unrelated to demand. They argue that number of drugs in a class in a quarter suffices, because drug entry is decided far in advance. https://t.co/4J1DV4NEba
Now on the supply side, we need to decide on how pharmaceutical companies bargain. In America, it's just Bertrand (or price) competition. https://t.co/6zFXeKG7Xc
Identifying generics is simplified greatly by just assuming that they are produced at marginal cost. https://t.co/8cYw7l9YCW
To see how much bargaining reduces the price, they compare the Nash-Bertrand outcome to the observed price, and see what the coefficient to multiply it would be.

Now that we have demand and bargaining, we can see what would happen if the United States tied their prices to Canada. Our prices would fall by 7.5%. Their prices would rise by 216%. (!!) https://t.co/88PGmadH9p

These results are for reference pricing with Canada. Given what we would expect about demand in the poorest countries in the world, tying our prices to theirs would result in drugs simply not being sold at all.

Yet, while not effective at reducing prices -- profit margins actually increase -- this is probably much better than the United States negotiating! Drug discovery is a function of R&D, and R&D is a function of expected profits. I don't think we really *want* cheaper drugs.
I really like this paper, and I think I would teach it as an example of BLP style demand estimation. It's very clean.

Dubois, Gandhi, Vasserman, "Bargaining and International Reference Pricing in the Pharmaceutical Industry" (2022)
https://t.co/lyqYK9GraM

papers.entry_text:
Miyauchi, Nakajima, Redding: "The Economics of Spatial Mobility: Theory and Evidence Using Smartphone Data" (2025)
https://t.co/wiu4AFwnxZ

detailed_thread.text: Quantitative spatial models have conventionally been limited to modeling an origin and destination, without considering itineraries. Allowing for this changes the welfare gains of central city transport vs commuter rail. Even more importantly, it requires clever methods. :) 1/ https://t.co/Mp2Yhdq4kU

Ordinarily, spatial economists think of people as picking a home and a workplace, and traveling between the two. We can't consider intermediate stops, because we simply don't have the data for it. All we have are things like the LEHD. Cell phone data solves this.
The big implication is that there are now consumption externalities. A rise in the utility of a destination affects the outcomes of things along the way, which standard models would miss.

Given the number of possible outcomes, the estimation method needs to simplify. Start with the basics here: on workdays, the itinerary must include home at the beginning and end, and include work at some point. https://t.co/oAyW00geoJ

Every day, people wake up and face an idiosyncratic preference shock to the utility of different locations. These shocks are drawn from a Frechet distribution, which has nice properties. https://t.co/RTxS9hNYtF
All of these draws are independent, as they must be in order to keep the model tractable. https://t.co/z15PJBOMY2

Now, how do we calculate the possible itineraries? We have an inside loop (the agent's problem) and an outside loop (the unknown parameters). Our method is to just solve the inside loop with an arbitrary distribution, and then adjust by a known amount as we learn the outside loop https://t.co/ofaqXItFPj

Solving the outside loop is basically just guess and check. Try out values, adjust the inside loop accordingly, fit to data. Adjust the guess, try again. This is simulated method of moments, and it gets used a lot in IO. https://t.co/2lvgxjxb9F
Since we assumed a constant elasticity of substitution, we can just read off retail markups as sigma. https://t.co/itFO2gtYS9
Importantly, we can learn how much people reduce their travel as costs rise. https://t.co/3p3R9VLuZW
Given all these parameters, there exists a system of equations in the appendix. You can solve for general equilibrium, if you know all of these. https://t.co/AtsQtTfVlt
It's not as complicated as it looks. Most of these parameters have answers they can just look up. https://t.co/Pw1lAasQo3
The first cool thing we can do is show how consumption shifted out toward the suburbs because of work from home, something an in and out model would have totally missed. https://t.co/xLo5L1omBo

The other cool thing we can do is simulate building a railway which is a loop around sub-centers in the city, versus going from suburbs to city. The gains for the former are much larger in this model! https://t.co/4q0DZpTnhD
If there is one thing I would like people to take away from this fantastic paper, it is that mobility in the city is extremely valuable, and we should build more subways.

Miyauchi, Nakajima, Redding: "The Economics of Spatial Mobility: Theory and Evidence Using Smartphone Data" (2025)
https://t.co/jcTia7MYwM

papers.entry_text:
Peter Maxted, "Present Bias Unconstrained: Consumption, Welfare, and the Present-Bias Dilemma" (2025)
https://t.co/Lo0aAHdZMk

detailed_thread.text: Interesting approach to modeling present bias. Basically, if people treat their present self as a different person than future self, we are now estimating a dynamic discrete game -- very hard! If instead we are in continuous time, all of this goes away, and we can model it. 1/ https://t.co/GdXJJjybFT

We want to have some way of dealing with people behaving in time-inconsistent ways. With present bias, people favor consumption this literal moment, and treat it different when in the future. https://t.co/sZVlErTVU9
Someone with preferences like these will give a different answer to "today or tomorrow?" and "a year or a year and a day?". They have time-inconsistent preferences. https://t.co/V8D8H5Kwhh

The issue with including this in a model is strategic behavior. As someone who has been interested in industrial organization, which frequently has stuff like this, this is a complete pain in the ass to deal with. https://t.co/pQKFv5plou
So let's set time period to zero. We're in continuous time now. https://t.co/THxIKqlxGf
No comments on the model itself! But something I didn't know for a long time is why we were ever bothering with continuous time. Well, that's why.
The main results are these: first, if the agent has any access to liquid assets at all, commitment devices don't do anything. https://t.co/ML22j3Wqnf
The commitment devices would just lower future consumption, not present consumption. https://t.co/xH68YVLJym
It implies that a commitment device has to be perfect to be used -- explaining why they are so infrequently used, in spite of theory. https://t.co/HrBwfWPXWZ

Certainly I can give some intuitive support from dieting. I was able to lose weight when I had basically totally run out of money. I was not able to lose weight by imperfectly restricting my access to money when I had it.
Second, the losses are pretty big. https://t.co/SUNjjytLtf
They're independent of lots of things you'd expect them to be related to, at least in his model. This is something you can go test! https://t.co/hifjCRj9rx

Finally, the policies which benefit people are only the ones which benefit standard agents. In some sense, present bias matters, and it does not matter. It imposes a loss, and yet what we can do about is incredibly limited. https://t.co/bOKUiA3NWW

Peter Maxted, "Present Bias Unconstrained: Consumption, Welfare, and the Present-Bias Dilemma" (2025)
https://t.co/lArn4cFm9i
@alexolegimas Not my area, but I thought it was neat.

papers.entry_text:
Cingano, Palomba, Pinotti, Rettore: "Making Subsidies Work: Rules vs Discretion" (2025)
https://t.co/KRzEPoAQ93

detailed_thread.text: Letting politicians be in charge of which firms receive subsidies would massively increase the cost of the program. If we are to do industrial policy -- and it seems like we are insistent -- then we must keep politicians far away from the allocation! 1/ https://t.co/IXA9GzlfGQ
The policy in question is Italian Law 488, which spent tens of billions of Euros financing tens of thousands of projects. Who received funds was a mix of rules and discretion. https://t.co/Eg28SFnWgo

Importantly, these rules are opaque before entering. Therefore, some firms will fall just above and below the line to receive funding, which would allow us to pull out what the effect of those funds are. https://t.co/XH9PF7Du0J
The jump here is fairly obvious. https://t.co/kWWZsJHBIV
And it remains for some time afterward. https://t.co/gkblM9vHeS
If that's all they were doing, it would be a fine applied micro paper. We want to incorporate the political discretion, though.

We can exploit the fact that the first two rounds of the program did not give any room for "regional development considerations", which (let us be clear), meant that the Southerners could spend money on whatever they please. https://t.co/7oOCHatjWV
Graph's a bit confusing, but it's basically the effect size as a function of objective rules (x axis, high numbers better), and political discretion. (Y axis). https://t.co/z37crbhVBc
Giving jobs only according to political will would increase the cost by 42%. https://t.co/gpH7RbqlmC

Now, it's possible that this result is unique to Italy. Perhaps the Southern Italians are a uniquely venal, troublesome race. I think it suggests something important even about America, though -- personalized negotiations for jobs and trade deals are pretty bad!!

Cingano, Palomba, Pinotti, Rettore: "Making Subsidies Work: Rules vs Discretion" (2025)
https://t.co/iHvFvmFlyr

papers.entry_text:
Einav, Finkelstein, Mahoney: "Producing Health: Measuring Value Added of Nursing Homes" (2025)
https://t.co/2KTkvBRIN0

detailed_thread.text: Medicare spends $30 billion a year on nursing homes, at a cost of $470 per patient-day. Reallocating patients from the worst to best nursing homes substantially improves health and reduces stays, potentially saving billions. 1/ https://t.co/sC5FQnfZWT

The big trouble with estimating the value-added of nursing homes, or SNFs, is that, much like with schools, there is considerable selection of who is in them. The first issue is selection in -- some nursing homes might be more attractive for healthier patients.

Now, the good thing is that they have good data to work with. Nursing homes keep unusually detailed and organized records. (Medical privacy laws keep us from doing this for other things -- they are bad!). https://t.co/NHNaDsKtQI

To deal with selection in, we need an instrumental variable, which shifts the relative costs of a nursing home without being correlated with characteristics. Here, they use distance from a nursing home. https://t.co/5GXnKv0Tsg
The instrumental variable  here seems plausible. I dunno. https://t.co/VyexLCMcE8

The other concern is selection out. If we're measuring value-added, holding on to healthy patients longer would decrease value-added, even if the hold-up is due to unobservables. They're just estimating a discharge threshold for every nursing home. https://t.co/grXNLVUtEU
So with the preliminaries out of the way, there is considerable variation in quality of nursing homes. https://t.co/KWlnzqmg41
Nursing homes do add value, but the very best ones add way more. https://t.co/v43k1CiWMY
Moving from the 10th to the 90th percentile nursing home saves $2600 a year. There are, for context, around 1.5 million nursing home stays a year. https://t.co/3CZGFj36p6

So why is this heterogeneity here? How can we predict better facilities? Well, first off, the difference is not largely regional. There are excellent and bad facilities everywhere. https://t.co/VQomqDY7CA
The average does differ by region, to be clear, with the Mountain West, upper New England, and (for some reason) Michigan doing the best. https://t.co/dzCxRyLgWT

Unfortunately, and this does make me concerned for the whole exercise, is that value added is substantially correlated with patient characteristics, but not supplier characteristics. https://t.co/kkSURKiGiL
I would quite like for them to release a list -- perhaps updated? -- of the best nursing homes in America.

Einav, Finkelstein, Mahoney: "Producing Health: Measuring Value Added of Nursing Homes" (2025)
https://t.co/IXVIU3dyVy

papers.entry_text:
Cheng and Heckmann, "Patient Peer Effects: Evidence from Nursing Home Room Assignments" (2025)
https://t.co/FFKRwGkspP

detailed_thread.text: It's possible to reduce mortality in nursing homes by 5.2% simply by intelligently assigning rooms. Alzheimer's patients do better with a cognitively normal roommate, while normal patients do better with private rooms. We can use this! 1/ https://t.co/1wYWO7pu71

Roommates are obviously endogenously assigned. To get around this, we know who is in the home on a given day. If all the rooms are empty, they'll get a private room. If all are filled with the cognitively impaired, they'll get that. With enough data, anything works. https://t.co/TJRkEUoYar

Alzheimer's patients do worse in an empty room, and better with a normal roommate. Normal patients do worse with a roommate -- importantly, with any roommate -- and better with a private room. The gains from trade are obvious -- save private rooms for normal, and mix types
Importantly, these effects approximately balance each other out, such that not accounting for heterogenous impacts by type finds no effect on anyone.
We also have a moral dilemma -- what sort of room system should we choose? I'm happy that there is an answer which is a Pareto improvement.

Cheng and Heckmann, "Patient Peer Effects: Evidence from Nursing Home Room Assignments" (2025)
https://t.co/u0qlQoZo27

papers.entry_text:
Dutz et al, “Selection in Surveys” (2025)
https://t.co/B0za9EMowA

detailed_thread.text: This is an extremely nifty paper which proposes methods for correcting survey non-response bias. The standard method, of assuming no differences after controlling for what we can observe, is insufficient. Worse, bigger incentives can actually worsen non-response bias! 1/ https://t.co/LePBbKUyXS
They are using a Norwegian labor market survey, which allows them to connect to Norway’s astonishing administrative data, and have a sense of what the ground truth should be.
To assess the degree of non-response bias is simple — changing payouts for completing the survey should have no effect if it’s unbiased. https://t.co/Em4WQdFnKo
We are not fully able to control for non-response bias with standard methods. Worse, the marginal participants are less representative than the marginal non-responders. https://t.co/TeL3Z1mKR1

Dutz et al, “Selection in Surveys” (2025)
https://t.co/sTsLW0LEWh

papers.entry_text:
Juan Camilo Castillo, "Who Benefits From Surge Pricing?" (2025)
https://t.co/iQ9bbGLApB

detailed_thread.text: Uber famously changes prices in response to demand, hiking prices when more people want the service. Who benefits? The consumer. Drivers lose out slightly compared to a world where Uber sets a uniform price. Total surplus goes up. We shouldn't be afraid of higher prices! 1/ https://t.co/EWnKDcEF4w

It is not ex ante obvious whether surge pricing will be better or worse for the consumer, or even overall. One can imagine a world where the demand elasticity is changing over time, and so dynamic pricing let's the company charge bigger total markups.

On the other hand, this is a two-sided market. Higher prices induce drivers to come out, leading to more people being able to travel. It also allocates drivers to people who have a higher willingness to pay.
He is using a thin slice in time, as he has extraordinarily detailed data. https://t.co/DfApVowg1z

Each hexagonal grid cell is about 400 meters across. He observes the location of drivers every few seconds, and lumps trip requests into two minute periods. He can tie 77% of customers to a probable home census tract, and proxy their income. https://t.co/1qciV8qg1e
Looking riders and available drivers covary, with surge pricing being highest Friday and Saturday night. Drivers can be basically divided into full time and part time drivers. https://t.co/I6G6prV9DK
At the time, this was how Uber set their prices. https://t.co/OYSqyMN6fq

So we need to estimate a model to do anything, which is complicated by their being two margins along which people adjust. They have the short run elasticity, where they open their phone, observe the price, and choose whether to book. There's also the long-run elasticity.
For the short run elasticity, we use the rounding method from Cohen et al (2016). The surge multiplier gets rounded to the nearest tenth, which provides a jump in prices. https://t.co/37F4SJKEip

Because these are only small jumps, we have an absolutely necessary assumption that the demand curve stays the same shape, and that we can trace the whole curve out by bringing together lots of small discontinuities. If you don't believe that, you don't believe the paper. https://t.co/5ISiytFOL5
We can also get a sense of the value of time by seeing how much people's decision to get a ride is affected by pickup time. https://t.co/gnswZfMihj
People react negatively to both prices and pickup times, putting an implied price of $2 a minute on their time. https://t.co/gqIUEc5TpU
The value of time is especially high for high income consumers who want to go to the airport. https://t.co/HAWTK5qKvf
But of course, we also need the long run elasticities. Here he uses estimates from experiments which uber ran in Latin America. https://t.co/XgeKqXIxe9
Utility from a ride is the characteristics of the ride a(x, l, h), minus the price p and wait time w, plus idiosyncratic error. We've decomposed the original error term e. https://t.co/SSyw9k35kr

This is a bit formal, but we're stating what we covered earlier -- the rounding introduces discontinuities to an otherwise smooth demand curve, which can shift in and out by retains the same shape. https://t.co/USUTwcUu7Y
Therefore, we can separately pull out the price and wait time coefficients. We have to make some assumption about the form of the variables though. https://t.co/Pt4XYgOTx3
Specifically, we need no unobserved heterogeneity, and we need linearity in the pick up time coefficient. Price can vary. https://t.co/SXafCEknLc
The main results for the demand side are higher income individuals have extremely inelastic demand, showing little response to changes in price. https://t.co/hcsvOEEfp2
Now the supply side. First, drivers choose location. He is again using the rounding to pull out how responsive drivers are. https://t.co/8LMDJPLGBq
Drivers really do locate near higher demand areas. Column 1 is just an OLS regression, while column 2 is using only the variation in rounding. https://t.co/fBIki2wR6V

The long-run elasticity is given by estimating how likely drivers are to exit, conditional upon pay. We aren't bothering with estimating a dynamic discrete game, just thinking of this in continuous time. https://t.co/mFjrcrRzqe
Part time drivers would extend their shift five minutes for a guaranteed five dollars. Full time drivers, and women, would only extend it 2.5 minutes. https://t.co/g2SgYCSeri
Finally, drivers enter. This he pulls from the estimates in Caldwell and Oehlsen. https://t.co/hG6FBJFl2v

So now we can finally report welfare effects! Total welfare would go up if prices were higher. Surge pricing substantially increases consumer welfare right now (by about 3%) but actually has little effect on profit. https://t.co/dw9lSbpf00
It should be obvious why, in hindsight -- riders care about where the rides are going, but drivers care way less. https://t.co/Z7mhQx6c6b
Prices would go up under uniform pricing. Because it's a two-sided market, too few drivers can lead to the whole market unwinding. You'd rather have high prices. https://t.co/GdYtElj1ze
So, complaints about surge pricing by riders are quite simply total nonsense. Even if all you care about is price, you still benefit considerably. https://t.co/LpggQsBylW

papers.entry_text:
Garin, Koustas, McPherson, Norris, Pecenco, Rose, Shem-Tv, Weaver: "The Impact of Incarceration on Employment, Earnings, and Tax Filing" (2025)
https://t.co/JJvSX0KxaZ

detailed_thread.text: How much does incarceration reduce earnings? Not much! Incarcerated individuals weren't going to be earning much of anything anyway. They earn less, and are incarcerated, because they are bad people -- incarceration doesn't lower earnings. 1/ https://t.co/Ww9o1LABDD

The paper uses two standard methods for finding the effect of incarceration: judge IVs, and discontinuities in sentencing guidelines. Hopefully two strategies makes the results more robust. Judge IVs first. https://t.co/c8fma8QvzT

In Ohio, arrestees are randomly and immediately assigned to judges. These judges have discretion over punishment, and vary in their harshness. Therefore, we can get plausibly random variation in sentence length, and whether one goes to prison at all, from the random judges. https://t.co/RDmARXqnDf
The other is from North Carolina. Your prison outcome is determined by a checklist of factors, which will cause sudden jumps in punishment severity. https://t.co/tE5ZOmYlTD
You can see these jumps here. https://t.co/m0lBhZQbYC

Excitingly, the results from the states line up, suggesting that the treatments are comparable and can be aggregated together, as well as that we are estimating an economically meaningful estimand. https://t.co/TKfPkYoAcT
Results time! As could be expected, earnings fall when you are in prison. However, they swiftly return to what they were earning before -- about $5,000 a year. https://t.co/L8hihKB4C6
We can rule out any adverse effects on income greater than $231. https://t.co/87BgE4Gar3

This ties in with a lot of literature which seriously tries to measure the effect of incarceration. The people who are incarcerated are poor because they are the sort who are incarcerated, not because they were incarcerated. To put it bluntly, they're bums. https://t.co/lTrsF2VRae
The results don't vary with whether someone was employed or unemployed before they went to jail. https://t.co/rISu57rZbT
In short, eliminating incarceration would do approximately nothing for labor earnings. https://t.co/GygsIeojZF

papers.entry_text:
Bursztyn, Handel, Jimenez-Duran, Roth, "When Product Markets Become Collective Traps: The Case of Social Media" (2025)
https://t.co/9zjktBwPgD

detailed_thread.text: Might social media be a trap? If our utility depends on who else is using the good, then we might choose to use social media even if we genuinely wished it never existed at all. 60% of Tiktok users would pay to have everyone deactivate the app! 1/ https://t.co/N5iFJq7GUF

If you want to use what other people are using -- or in other words, you suffer negative utility from someone else using the product -- you can get social losses even with fully rational humans. https://t.co/ptT9U1vsVd

Theory is insufficient, of course. We need to actually test it. The experimenters elicit the subjects' willingness to accept a payment to deactivate their account, and then ask what they would accept if part of everyone having their account deactivated. https://t.co/N9IAmXJTwI

They claim to be part of a much larger experiment. Hopefully this was believable. I'm actually slightly concerned about the ethics of this experiment -- not that it is harmful, but that it may degrade credibility. https://t.co/z8jjzlozpa

Turning to results: the net social surplus is positive. However, basically everyone would accept a lower payment in the world where everyone deactivates, and there is a great deal of heterogeneity in valuation.
A very large fraction of people, including current users, would like everyone to deactivate.

Unfortunately, though, the data is largely in the realm of hypotheticals. We don't know how seriously they took it. We can plausibly eliminate misunderstanding and regretting their choices after. https://t.co/B3sVk7XYm0

Bursztyn, Handel, Jimenez-Duran, Roth, "When Product Markets Become Collective Traps: The Case of Social Media" (2025)
https://t.co/MYlWMRifRz

papers.entry_text:
Munoz and Otero, "Managers and Public Hospital Performance" (2025)
https://t.co/w87BhzVOAP

detailed_thread.text: Replacing hospital managers who are doctors with MBAs reduced mortality by 8%. That works out to two fewer deaths per every thousand patients. Managerial quality really does matter, and their pay is justified by the value created. 1/ https://t.co/YaGnL08hAG
In 2003, Chile began selecting executives for public programs through an open and competitive process. This would go on to be used to fill 3,400 roles. https://t.co/3X1jOnq3f6

The first thing to try is just to compare hospitals which adopted the new policies, compared to those that didn't. We have, then, a fairly standard diff-in-diff, with mortality declining after, especially in the long run. https://t.co/vlCUlVn4Ih

We would naturally be concerned that managers who are good on paper will be appointed to hospitals which are already better. To get around this, they use the rotation of managers across hospitals to estimate the particular effect of the manager.
You're essentially comparing the change in R^2, which is percentage of variation explained, when you have manager fixed effects and when you don't. https://t.co/NLfodom3nG
Low and high quality CEOs consistently affect the death rate. https://t.co/Mi0CMJecmL
And looking at who came in when the selection process was open, it was actual managers replacing doctors. https://t.co/oqeDcBQLvp
It was medical doctors without management training who were pushed out. https://t.co/K9EiKMaUHl
The improvement in performance came entirely through CEOs trained in management. https://t.co/AX93jU5YyG
This would be, of course, a surprise to the doctors. https://t.co/epS0imgHUu

Munoz and Otero, "Managers and Public Hospital Performance" (2025)
https://t.co/jjEFZuGqov

papers.entry_text:
Chyn and Daruich, "An Equilibrium Analysis of the Effects of Neighborhood-Based Interventions on Children" (2025)
https://t.co/nVb8q2Bhs4

detailed_thread.text: The neighborhood you are in matters, but it was hitherto not clear whether paying people to move was good or bad on net. We know, from Moving to Opportunity and others, that moving benefits the mover, but what of everyone else? Chyn and Daruich find it's a net positive -- hooray! https://t.co/Gv13w7J6cF

Some background: we think the peers you are around matters. Some places -- popularly referred to as the ghetto, the hood, the projects, and so on -- are bad for you. So many poor people being around each other causes them to spiral into lawlessness and destitution.

To prevent this, what if we paid you to move? We have a number of experiments tracking the earnings of children later on, and we found that it greatly increased later wages -- a 31% improvement! https://t.co/UnykDktw4B

We cannot be sure that this is the true effect for society as a whole. The reason is that, for every poor kid no longer around poor kids, there are richer kids now around poor kids. Conceivably, these could balance each other out, or in fact be a negative.

We need to write down a model for this. We have wages be determined skill acquisition, and the degree of skill acquisition determined by endogenous spillovers from the neighborhood. https://t.co/9BW5R3w56s

Now, we have to be careful when we go through the model. It would be all too easy for your assumptions to deliver the results you want, which is hardly a useful or insightful exercise. Nevertheless, it is genuinely important to try.

We have an overlapping generations framework. Each generation chooses where to live, how much they're paying, and how much to transfer. In each stage, there is uncertainty -- people face shocks to skills and to wages. https://t.co/UpDpD7sJ84
Workers choose how much to work and save. Wages are equal in each neighborhood, so the only thing which matters are skill spillovers. https://t.co/11mOIFcWJ4
Parents have exactly one child (no coupling) and chooses how much to invest in them. Skills are correlated generation to generation. https://t.co/GEWI0sJcGY

Since wages (conditional on skills) are identical in each place, wages depends entirely on skill. Parents are taken to solve for maximizing expected value (which I skip over, the equations become a bit soupy). https://t.co/hgIQDY2YT0

Skipping over production and the housing market, which are fairly standard, we can identify what frictions it is that the government is solving. First, parents cannot borrow against the child's future income. This leads to government funded education. https://t.co/dcvR3fAyUN

There might also borrowing constraints in general, and there is also uncertainty due to shocks and externalities due to parents not internalizing their effect on neighborhood quality. Any intervention is balanced against taxation being inefficient. https://t.co/e0IAvhiQTt

We have a complicated model, and we want to find the right parameters that (when run) match the observed data. We will do this with simulated method of moments, which is essentially guessing, checking, and converging to the highest point. https://t.co/vvoRy64vKj

They greatly simplify the neighborhoods to just the-hood and not-the-hood. Meanwhile, estimating time use by parents comes from finding the elasticity of time investment with respect to wealth in surveys that actually have time use, and then generalizing to everywhere. https://t.co/9InDinTY2B
As noted, we guess, check, and converge to what we believe to be the optimal match. https://t.co/WKeRdtGVbu

Now we can compare policies! Suppose you have a housing voucher. Moving people out of the neighborhood increases their investment by much more than it decreases others, leading to substantially increased welfare, exceeding even the cost of taxes to pay it. https://t.co/npHQdUNaec
They're even making things harder for themselves by requiring that the government balance the budget in each period. https://t.co/Wcfu3OsQNm

For robustness, they show that a one percent change in all of the parameters is not enough to change much of the results. I would have liked to see what a larger change, like 5%, would imply. https://t.co/OHQS7tbBQg

They then change how the models work to test other specifications. What might happen if, for instance, different income families have different impacts on skill acquisition? Even here, the general equilibrium effects are still positive. https://t.co/uEVygM6pKv

The main thing which matters is housing supply elasticity. Cities with harsh zoning codes see less of a benefit from getting people to move, because wherever they go they're likely to push someone out. Instead, a wage subsidy is better -- though I'm skeptical in practice. https://t.co/CgYBeYAMyO
In short, housing vouchers work. We can support them without fear that they are making us worse off. https://t.co/mCCqVGPkV6

Chyn and Daruich, "An Equilibrium Analysis of the Effects of Neighborhood-Based Interventions on Children" (2025)

https://t.co/LV1E53IILe

papers.entry_text:
Aggeborn and Ohman. "The Effects of Fluoride in Drinking Water" (2021)
https://t.co/CIvtkG2vE0

detailed_thread.text: This is conclusive evidence on the effect of water fluoridation. It improves dental health with no effect on intelligence -- and if anything, raises labor income. 1/ https://t.co/eTOtjyTAKY

Fluoride naturally occurs in water, but the size varies due to geographic conditions. Granite bedrock, for instance, will raise fluoride content. Even within a narrow area, we can get substantial variation in fluoride. https://t.co/uXO5qJD5Yr
Here is a map of what they believe to be the fluoride level in each municipality. https://t.co/Uix31mQrDh
And since they don't have the location of each water treatment plant, here is how they tie plants to specific municipalities. https://t.co/g0FtBqoy77
What makes this possible is Sweden's mandatory IQ test as part of conscription. https://t.co/bLwr4troEN

They have a definite and quite large impact on dental health -- if fluoride increased to one milligram per liter (which is about midway through the range of possible levels) the likelihood of needing a dental repair would fall 3.4 percentage points. https://t.co/kKKn4SHD3o
On the other hand, they get unbelievably small and largely insignificant results on intelligence. With controls, they actually think the effect is positive. https://t.co/kYVtgeRy6w

They get small effects on labor income, which, interestingly enough, are only found among lower income populations. This is very consistent with better dental health helping people work! Where richer people brush their teeth anyway. https://t.co/k4HQiiDUIE
So what's going on with other studies? Pretty much, they don't actually have exogenous variation. https://t.co/oV5cJu2yPD
So, there's no reason to have absurd amounts of fluoride in the water. Of course not. But in small doses, it helps tooth health, without impairing functioning.

Aggeborn and Ohman. "The Effects of Fluoride in Drinking Water" (2021)
https://t.co/eSHjaRGUTG

papers.entry_text:
Kang and Vasserman, "Robustness Measures for Welfare Analysis" (2025)
https://t.co/wVCL302Jcc

detailed_thread.text: Suppose we observe the effect of 2% tax. To extrapolate to 10%, we need to make assumptions about the form of the demand curve. How much does this matter? Can we put bounds on how much it deviates? 1/ https://t.co/ThVfekdUxg

We can characterize any demand curve as a function of two parameters governing its gradient and curvature. Then, we can say what percentage of demand curves which pass through the observed points will overturn our linear results. https://t.co/mVKBvGKs9B

Given bounds on what values the demand curve can pass through, we get actually quite simple formulas for maximum welfare loss -- it's just the average difference in observed price times quantity, plus or minus the what the average difference would be for the two extreme points. https://t.co/IflWlmjJYK

We can boil down how much we need to constrain the gradient of the demand curve to a single statistic, r. Btw, all of these demand curve so far require *something* to be constant -- whether that is slope, or price elasticity, or what have you. https://t.co/DZmhAtx8HK
We can also allow for curvature. We can just see how much the demand curve would have to bend to overcome the conclusions. https://t.co/XnPIiEirey
Those are the convenient and useful parts for researchers. To be quite frank, I do not follow the proof. Its subtleties are lost on me.

The empirical application considers the 2018 tariff wave. A bunch of studies considered the effects, but when they disagree, it's not clear that it's because their assumptions disagree, or because their parameter estimates disagree. https://t.co/FFERzJnNFx

Amiti, Redding, and Weinstein assumed linear demand curves. We can quickly show, now, how the estimates would differ if the demand curve assumptions changed; and that these assumptions will consistently change the results. https://t.co/KyyRmZ5xvn
We can nevertheless conclude that the tariffs were bad; the relevant demand curvature would have to vary by an enormous amount to break even. https://t.co/EQpdatSq0g

Kang and Vasserman, "Robustness Measures for Welfare Analysis" (2025)

https://t.co/HlwQOY2rpl

papers.entry_text:
Almagro, Barbieri, Castillo, Hickok, Salz: "Optimal Urban Transportation Policy: Evidence from Chicago" (2025)
https://t.co/2GiA57Hbd7

detailed_thread.text: This paper is an absolutely monumental piece of work. Given incredibly detailed data on how people travel in Chicago, can one calculate the optimal prices of vehicles, roads, and public transport? It turns out -- free buses are correct! And we need massive taxes on vehicles. 1/ https://t.co/5MMKeunyOd

To simplify the analysis, we will skip over endogenous reallocation of people, and we are only dealing with pricing the modes which we have now. People are taken to flow between each of the 77 Chicago neighborhoods -- we are not getting more granular than this. https://t.co/YY6E6oC5Cx
To preview the results -- since the government is budget constrained, we will not get optimal results without cross-subsidization. https://t.co/5R55PBFl5V

The data is miraculous. It's genuinely astonishing. They get car rides, which are usually unobserved, from cellphone records, after subtracting out other modes of transportation. No one else has this! Ever! You never see this!! https://t.co/ULuwP19p4w
They're using the BLP form, with instrumental variables coming from straightline distance. https://t.co/X0xFN4StTI

If you recall from my coverage of Castillo's job market paper, we also use the rounding of the surge pricing multiplier.
https://t.co/iQ9bbGLApB
Chicago is a starkly segregated city, with most of the rich people on the North Side. 70% of trips are taken by car, which are considerably faster than either trains or buses. https://t.co/NkXa0SgMmz
Buses never, ever, ever beat cars, although we would like more people to use them if only to benefit the car drivers! https://t.co/6P13823Of1

Traffic congestion starts off flat in the number of vehicles, then sharply picks up when it gets too high. (It should be clear from this why taxes might be so beneficial for congestion). https://t.co/ito63C8oML

I'm going to skip over estimation. Each consumer draws their coefficients from some distribution, and then pin down that coefficient with plausibly exogenous variation. You can then see how much a change in the price of one mode causes substitution to another.

Here are the key results. Value of time is higher in the rich parts. Outside good is the percentage who would stay home instead of traveling if they couldn't use their preferred mode. Most car drivers simply won't go if they can't drive. https://t.co/h4UbnCh2m3

Optimal non-budget-balanced policy with no road taxes is 5 minute bus wait, 3 minute train wait, with them being at a negative cost. Welfare increases by $3.89 million a week, with consumer surplus up $26.87 million, but the deficit increases by $21 million a week.

If you have to balance the budget, fares go up and frequency goes down. To make up for it, turn to road pricing. It should cost about $15 a day to drive if that's the only policy available. https://t.co/rPk9IUpEMJ
Adding it all together, the road tax can fund transport, and welfare can increase by $7 million a week. https://t.co/JlRBA12ujH

Zohran Mamdani is not crazy with regard to free buses! Although the estimates cannot be ported over without consideration. Pulling people off of the road and onto the bus is good for everyone, and it's even better if the congestion surcharge can fund that and quality improvements

Almagro, Barbieri, Castillo, Hickok, Salz: "Optimal Urban Transportation Policy: Evidence from Chicago" (2025)
https://t.co/Akj0yBCAIZ

papers.entry_text:
Kalyani, Bloom, Carvalho, Hassan, Lerner, Tahoun, "The Diffusion of New Technologies" (2025)
https://t.co/IS9mTaxvz7

detailed_thread.text: AI might reshape everything we do at work. Let's remember, though, what happened before with new technologies -- they take a very long time to disperse, and take the longest in the highest skilled professions. Actually implementing matters almost as much as inventing! 1/ https://t.co/02pwYpxGgu

Defining a "new technology" is a surprisingly squishy task. We would like the paper to be comprehensive, and not just a series of case studies. For this, we need to identify language specific to technologies, which we can reference on Wikipedia. https://t.co/FzIzRvgUEk
I think it's worth going into more detail on how they do this. The text is massive, so you chop off the stop words, and then anything in usage before 1970. https://t.co/6MmpmfBHZR

The matching to Wikipedia pages was automated -- you don't actually need textual analysis which is all that complicated. They will run a robustness check in VII.C, and it won't change anything important. https://t.co/JAk1ecjGxB
With these identified words and phrases, we can look for them in an extraordinary number of of job postings, and also in the text of corporate earnings conferences. https://t.co/5OMxRudM1n

Something I found interesting to just gawk at is the list of the top technology for each year -- a couple of them do seem a bit generous as technologies (active user? financial instrument?) but it's not totally unreasonable. https://t.co/ARnUWyRzD2
Just a few locations are responsible for a majority of the most impactful patents. https://t.co/jbKV8SAzmk
And if you set the threshold for "impactful" higher, it only makes their advantage stronger. https://t.co/c4nfHx8tTf
Technologies start around where they are invented, and only gradually spread across the country. https://t.co/xfnhGCGX5I
Higher skill professions see a slower spread, but this is likely due to the predominance of higher skill jobs in the better parts of the country. https://t.co/hAiFhEEG46

Kalyani, Bloom, Carvalho, Hassan, Lerner, Tahoun, "The Diffusion of New Technologies" (2025)
https://t.co/3Fp4Xg5N4K

papers.entry_text:
Adams, Huttunen, Nix, Zhang "The Dynamics of Abusive Relationships" (2024)
https://t.co/3PXlEq7iMC

detailed_thread.text: It's even in the best econ journals -- domestic abusers pressure their partners to exit the activities which give them an option to leave. Please consider that the desire for monogamy is an offspring of a jealous, grasping, diseased mind! 1/ https://t.co/B2zsKm5Y2q

The paper itself is in that genus of papers made possible by Finland's miraculous data access. You can, in fact, just tie every police report to the employment and domicile of every single person in Finland. https://t.co/ixxCvxUJc6
Women who enter an abusive relationship were on their way up before, which makes them more desirable to a man; that stops once they start living with the partner. https://t.co/rx2QhTc9w7

This is what it looks like in the data. An improving trend gets immediately turned around, and though it will partially turn around later, it will never catch up to those who didn't. These are extremely large changes, btw. https://t.co/6BKxkizUQC
Obvious concern is that someone might know that they have "gotten lucky" in their labor market outcomes, and thus want to drop out of the labor force more.
We get around this by comparing women who did have a police report to their closest matches on a wide range of observables who did not get a police report. https://t.co/PpKX9ksFJ8

We can also compare outcomes within the same woman across multiple relationships. We don't want to just be picking up something about the type of woman who enters an abusive relationship. https://t.co/P30b7vsei8
Yes, really, this is special to the abusive relationships. It's not just the type of woman, it's the man. https://t.co/PvouZBhCQ2

So why might we do this? Consider that the worse a woman's outside labor options are, the less able they are to leave. If one were a man who wished to have power over their wife, you would make it hard to leave.
Consider, then, what the expectation of monogamy is. You are making leaving more difficult. Jealous men will demand that their woman stop even talking to other men -- the better to control them.

Consider too that a social expectation of monogamy makes it harder for women to leave bad relationships, by reducing the pool of possible suitors. All of this would be obvious in any other market -- so why not in the labor market?

Adams, Huttunen, Nix, Zhang "The Dynamics of Abusive Relationships" (2024)
https://t.co/jcYRyOQQEG

papers.entry_text:
Andrew and Adams, "Revealed Beliefs and the Marriage Market Return to Education" (2025)
https://t.co/mGx53qXIcY

detailed_thread.text: It is believed that women in India pursue education in large part because it improves their marriage prospects. How important is this? And how do we elicit meaningful forecasts of future prospects, when the average years of education in their sample is 1.5? (Not a typo!!) 1/ https://t.co/Xn4pnmP2nS
Andrew and Adams have a sample of 4,500 caregivers to young girls in Rajasthan. The sample is extremely poor and uneducated -- only 10% can read a complete sentence. https://t.co/lYITkoNzJ5

We need to infer beliefs about both the quality of the pool of suitors, and the rate at which offers arrive. They will have two basic experiments. The first is comparing two possible matches. The other will ask whether one would accept or reject an offer now.

These two are sufficient to then map onto an optimal stopping problem. Starting with Rust (1987), given the observed behavior at which one accepts an offer, you can infer the underlying parameters which govern it.

The survey allows for lots of combinations of attributes, so we can compare what adding just one attribute does to the likelihood of acceptance and the quality of match which could be gotten. https://t.co/trMmUpMXrk

So, results: in obvious stuff, parents prefer wealthier families. Something striking is that they have a *really* strong preference for government jobs, however, which does not seem like a good sign for India. https://t.co/2zuJQF7Z4j
Also striking, despite 1.5 million girls below 18 being married a year and the law being largely unenforced, parents do seem to incorporate the law into their preferences. https://t.co/fZQiHq1yQF
The main result, however, is this: if parents did not think that education would get their girl married, they would not do it. https://t.co/tAaDOjzDyP

Now, an obvious concern is that these hypotheticals do not match up with reality. Five years later, then, the experimenters caught up with the same girls, and observed their trajectories. The predictions of the model were the same as reality. https://t.co/yzQdaBxdj7
I am usually acutely suspicious of hypothetical preferences! But one must yield to overwhelming evidence, and here they have it.

Andrew and Adams, "Revealed Beliefs and the Marriage Market Return to Education" (2025)
https://t.co/AULSrQ9JHw

papers.entry_text:
Orchard, Ramey, Wieland: "Micro MPCs and Macro Counterfactuals: The Case of the 2008 Rebates" (2025)
https://t.co/hiu2qzYr1J

detailed_thread.text: The marginal propensity to consume (or MPC) is one of the most important parameters to know if we want to estimate the effect of fiscal stimulus. Yet, we may not be able to know the real effect -- even from perfectly identified studies! Stimulus is less effective than we think.1/ https://t.co/nUDpwyA5Lr

Previous micro work on the MPCs take two basic paths. With Parker et al (2013), the identifying variation is coming from random variation in which people actually received their checks. https://t.co/JBjUC6HBwe
Alternatively, you can have a randomized experiment, as in "Five Facts about MPCs". In both cases, they find very large propensities to consume immediately, rather than save. https://t.co/TjwoHG3Unv

This is a necessary condition for stimulus to do anything, btw. If people save their check from the government in order to pay for the higher taxes which would be needed to balance the budget, then a condition called "Ricardian Equivalence" would hold, and stimulus does nothing.
When we have a model of the economy, we would like it to match the important facts (called "moments"), and this is one of them. https://t.co/xmDklC7WDc
Orchard, Ramey, and Wieland call bull on this. If you match the moments, you are forced to believe patently unrealistic counterfactuals. Something has got to give. https://t.co/V4cZoizaw4

If you believe it, then you would have us believe that, for one month, U.S. spending on automobiles would have suffered the greatest crash in history in 2008, and then immediately return to normal. Nobody thinks, then or now, that would happen. https://t.co/AXLGtjuSIE

The model itself is a fairly standard two-agent NK model. One agent saves, while the other lives hand to mouth and spends profligately. I'm going to skip over the details, but the only way to reconcile the figures is a crash which is over in a flash. https://t.co/bJOpSG3mgN

It really should be obvious when you think about it. The tax rebates didn't lead to an enormous boom, which is what the high MPCs imply. Therefore, the only way to balance things out would be to crash.

So why? Why are micro MPCs so much higher? First, we deal with Parker et al, who have the whole economy but shaky identification. It can test the permanent income hypothesis, but cannot be intepreted as an MPC. https://t.co/T9AE8Qw8LY

And what about the micro-RCTs? Well, they're perfectly accurate when a few people get them, but not when everyone gets them. If everyone rushed out to buy, they wouldn't be able to, reducing the portion spent. https://t.co/FTOmJUM30J

This is a persistent problem for scaling micro to macro! The micro estimates are based around an assumption of no spillovers, which obviously will not hold! Everyone should read this by @BrianCAlbrecht 
https://t.co/F6ARkgyai0

Orchard, Ramey, Wieland: "Micro MPCs and Macro Counterfactuals: The Case of the 2008 Rebates" (2025)
https://t.co/Z52wwZc36b

papers.entry_text:
Dube, MacArthur, Shah: "A Cognitive View of Policing" (2024)
https://t.co/cZ4HwBJgzI

detailed_thread.text: This preposterous series of sentences was published in the QJE. The police pursuing someone who flees for no reason is *precisely* what the police should be doing, and that this is framed as "unnecessary" arrest shows how disconnected the liberal view of policing is. 1/ https://t.co/I2NMLjHgci
Why might he be scared? It's pretty easy to not be arrested, don't run. https://t.co/xA1NV7MtIx

This whole paper is so monstrously distorted in what it regards as good and bad. On what basis is reducing the arrests of Black suspects a good thing? This paper makes no attempt whatsoever to quantify what the effect on crime is. https://t.co/q6iakrvuqv

None! Zero! Zilch! Nada! I had to go through the paper twice. How on earth was this published in the QJE? It has no business being there -- it's a run of the mill RCT which cannot even answer a question of importance. Best they have is a proxy for other activity.

Notably, how this index will be constructed is left undefined in the pre-analysis plan. Turning to the appendix in the working paper, we can track it down. Their interpretation is reasonable, but they should not have left themselves latitude to weight components. https://t.co/NjrnuI1HTk

They argue that the program paid for itself simply by having fewer officers injured in the course of duty, and thus miss work. But why stop there? We could cut injuries to zero if we simply had them not arrest anyone!

They explicitly note that officer injuries was not in their pre-analysis plan, and they cannot even tie the injuries to the arrests. What are we even doing here? Why do we have a pre-analysis plan if you're not going to follow it? https://t.co/DjO7G9XgX0
My takeaway from it is this. The city told its officers not to arrest so many Black people. And lo, they did not arrest as many Black people.
Officers, of course, understood what the city of Chicago wanted, and so were much *less* likely to ascribe criminal intent to a Black suspect. https://t.co/huoGEkGydJ

Why are we elevating work like this? It's a very shallow paper. It does not generalize, and it does not make any claims about it being welfare improving. It is a fine RCT. It has no business being in the QJE.
What's more, publishing this sort of stuff, for which the standards were so obviously lowered, is dangerous to the reputation of economics as a whole.

Dube, MacArthur, Shah: "A Cognitive View of Policing" (2024) 
https://t.co/GjFdxiEZNx

papers.entry_text:
Seibold, Seitz, Siegloch: "Privatizing Disability Insurance" (2025)
https://t.co/FoRXBasbYz

detailed_thread.text: What might a better system look like? Instead of having the government provide disability to insurance -- and be unable to say no, even to fraud -- why not have military members purchase private insurance? When Germany did this, welfare went up -- let's see why. 1/ https://t.co/hUiXoJoOZ2 https://t.co/9Z4Uo3bmfp

In 2001, Germany got rid of disability insurance which paid out if you were unable to work in your prior profession. Instead, you would have to get general disability -- you can't work anywhere at all. https://t.co/GuwNmxuaRY
The researchers have all claims on the public system, and the entire records of a large German insurance corporation (which chose to remain anonymous). https://t.co/Hv3TCkyn9J
Obvious things first -- after a spike of people getting their disability claims just in time, public claims went down, and private insurance was bought more often. https://t.co/NaCTdZ9lhL

If the people who are more likely to take up insurance are also riskier, this is bad. It will lead to the price of insurance being higher than what it would be in a world where everyone must get it, pricing some people out.
We can test for this in two different ways. First, are the people who take up insurance more likely to be disabled? https://t.co/CM7YIWlHz9
They find, no. If anything, it's the opposite. https://t.co/WYYGnWy4xZ

The other is to use methods from Einav, Finkelstein, and Cullen, where you see if the marginal cost curve is different from the average cost curve.
https://t.co/Yrfj0StGSG
If you have plausibly exogenous shifts -- and they have these from discrete changes in risk-evaluation -- then you can trace out the supply and demand curves for insurance. https://t.co/ZsGlICZnzz

Considering this, you can calculate the impact on welfare. Due to advantageous selection, we are able get insurance for less than a public mandate would cost. The authors seem somewhat averse to this conclusion, and note that "equity" could justify a mandate.

If we consider in the context of the military, though, this argument would be fatuous! Lower income people don't take up the insurance (because they don't value it). In the military, though, everyone is paid reasonably well.

So, yes -- just abolish the VA, and abolish veterans disability benefits. Scrap them! Pay people in the military more now, and let them buy insurance for the risk of disability. It would force them to be honest, and for the program to match our expectations.

Seibold, Seitz, Siegloch: "Privatizing Disability Insurance" (2025)
https://t.co/9WsTpCftmO

papers.entry_text:
Alessandra Fenizia, “Managers and Productivity in the Public Sector” (2022)
https://t.co/ErbTG69YNz

detailed_thread.text: Management matters, even in the public sector when they cannot control payroll or even easily fire anyone. A manager one standard deviation better than average raises productivity by 10%. Yes, really, managerial pay is justified. 1/ https://t.co/fWmmqyS7P7

We are in the Italian Social Security Agency, which does much the same thing as our SSA does. They need to evaluate a lot of claims as quickly as possible. Since the tasks are the same everywhere, we can pretty exactly measure output as a function of workers. https://t.co/EAepbZCMWV

The key piece allowing us to identify the managers is the statutory requirement that they change offices every five years, for anti-corruption purposes. If we can reasonably rule out sorting due to trends (we can adjust for levels) our model is identified. https://t.co/7QPyT7kL5o

First pass are two-way fixed effects — adjusting for the average of that time period, and the average for that office. Next, moving to bad managers is symmetric with good managers, and there are no pre-trends. https://t.co/Efixm2vSWB
When good managers take over, output goes down while the number of workers stays the same. But how? They cannot fire anyone, only allocate tasks. https://t.co/1I5akxyv5y
What seems to be happening is that they’re pissing off older workers, who then retire — and good riddance! https://t.co/eEZrVnKDfE

Optimal policy involves assigning the best managers to the best offices, and raises productivity by 6.9%. (Remember, twfe adjusts for levels, so differences in which offices they’re allocated to are not a threat to the paper). https://t.co/hLFzVPZcBt
This effect is not due to gaming the system for which tasks are done. The composition of tasks does not change at all. https://t.co/ehzThMNJVI
Last, something that jumped out. Managers are wayyyyy more likely to be from Southern Italy. https://t.co/GUBJ9flsl6

Alessandra Fenizia, “Managers and Productivity in the Public Sector” (2022)
https://t.co/m3ybtMDgDN

detailed_thread.text: People often feel obliged to hide how much they make, not only from their family, but even from their spouse. They fear the other person will use the knowledge to better extract resources. Interestingly, the effect on productivity is the opposite for men and women. 1/ https://t.co/ge6yoboljm https://t.co/qg9T4JhMUo

Unfortunately, the experiment here is not as good as in "The Social Tax", but it is reasonable. Since the sample is largely garment workers, they gave them a task common to where they work, with variable pay as a function of production. https://t.co/G6fw2wtp4t
When women have their income disclosed, they reduce their output. Men do the opposite, and increase it by quite a lot. Why? https://t.co/KQL9M2IGWv
The authors argue that this is explained by men having greater bargaining power. https://t.co/TxoigRNyOa
Worth noting that "The Social Tax" was on an almost entirely female sample! So their sharply negative results could be affected by this.

Zhou and Mahadeshwar, "The Impact of Intra-Household Income Hiding on Labor Productivity" (2024)

papers.entry_text:
Amaral, Borker, Fiala, Kumar, Prakash, Sviatschi: "Sexual Harassment in Public Spaces and Police Patrols: Experimental Evidence from Urban India" (2025)
https://t.co/qSlleLRTdG

detailed_thread.text: Do the police reduce crime? Well, yes. We have really clean evidence from this RCT in Hyderabad, where the police randomized which hot spots of sexual harassment they patrolled. Uniformed officers showing up reduced serious crime, without even having to write a ticket. 1/ https://t.co/nGMNWTxHZm
Sexual harassment in Hyderabad, as with India in general, is unfortunately quite common. https://t.co/KtiMNDWD2K

To combat this, the police had turned to both plainclothes policemen, as well as uniformed officers. The patrols they are studying are just 15 minutes jaunts through the area, 45 minutes a week. What effect they have on crime is likely
To measure crime, they had enumerators discreetly visit the hot spots, totally unaware of the police interventions. https://t.co/8okKc70StO
Simply showing up regularly was enough to substantially deter criminality. It became known as a place where people show up sometimes, and not a safe place to grope a woman. https://t.co/faC4C3s4Vq
Surveying the officers, we find that those who feel like mild sexual harassment is no big deal do, indeed, not deter mild sexual harassment. https://t.co/YRyAPZIF3T
So, this is another piece of evidence on the pile that conspicuous policing will reduce crime. It is altogether weaker, though, than say, Klick and Tabarrok (2005).

Amaral, Borker, Fiala, Kumar, Prakash, Sviatschi: "Sexual Harassment in Public Spaces and Police Patrols: Experimental Evidence from Urban India" (2025)
https://t.co/ebHS1Pn1uL

papers.entry_text:
Not quite a paper, but if interested, I have a sideline as "opera critic"
https://t.co/82mYjNaeow

detailed_thread.text: "Palestrina", by Hans Pfitzner, is my choice for the greatest opera of all time. This is in spite of the artist being notorious misanthrope and anti-Semite, who was only not a Nazi due to personally offending Hitler in 1923. What wonderful music it is! 1/
Born 1869, he grew up in Frankfort. He made the unfortunate mistake of taking a job in Strasbourg, in Alsace; when the French took it back, he was largely down on his luck for the rest of his life.

He was a difficult man. Actually, it wasn't just that. He was wildly anti-Semitic, if only against Jews as a concept rather than personally. In his defense, however, he was not a Nazi. The Nazis wouldn't take him.

He had met Hitler in 1923, while both were in hospital. Hitler was so offended by how disagreeable he was that during the whole 1930s, he was largely blacklisted from conducting jobs. He strikes me as someone we might describe now as autistic

Palestrina, written over 6 years in his forties, should be seen as a self-insert piece. He saw himself as part of preserving the traditional German Kunst, from Beethoven to Wagner. He worked in a heavy idiom, brooding, dark, and cold; I shiver to hear much of his work.

"Palestrina" is about a legend concerning the eponymous Italian composer of polyphony. At the Council of Trent, it was said that many wanted to ban polyphonic music; and that it was Palestrina who wrote a mass so moving that he single-handedly swayed the pope, and saved music.

That mass, the Missa Papae Marcelli, will lend us some of the themes upon which Pfitzner develops.
