We begin at night. Palestrina, whose wife had recently died, cannot sleep. We hear an unornamented theme, based on ascending fifths, and the winding of voices in the orchestra.

The second theme draws strength from the rumble of distant drums, and a single line of melody, clear and sharp as glass, introduces the theme for the Council of Trent. And then -- the two fit together! They interlock, before the light begins to break over the horizon.

We skip over the scene setting music of Palestrina's young song, Ighino, and his pupil, Silla, as they practice their singing, to the entrance of the Cardinal Borromeo, announced by dark grumblings in the basses underneath the song of the children.

He has called upon Palestrina to commission a mass. The Council, he says, wishes to return to the chanting of Pope Gregory. And he traces out the stakes -- the death of art itself.

But Palestrina refuses. He cannot write. He has no inspiration left in him. Not without his wife.

The Cardinal Borromeo is a domineering man. He cannot believe that the Palestrina he knew would refuse it. So he rages,  prophesizing darkly, and storms out, cloak flapping in the wind, horns snarling through fast descending scales. Palestrina is left, alone.

But not for long. For from dusk's gloom, come the voices of spirits. They are the ghosts of composers long dead, whom he greets, and apologizes. They tell him what he must do, and leave. Next come a band of angels, singing Kyrie. He picks up the theme. His wife is among them, and…

So he writes. Toil. The music is of a simple church bell like interval, grown monstrously distorted. Exhausted, he collapses to sleep. His children come in, and see the manuscript. The mass. It is the mass. And bell like interval sweeps us away.

The second act is the Council of Trent. I will only highlight one passage, besides the also excellent prelude. The first half is largely waiting for everyone to arrive, before the business can begin. When, however, they are finally assembled, the master of ceremonies speaks.

When he has finished speaking, we have, simply, the finest moment in Western classical music. It sounds like being bodily levitated down the nave of cathedral so vast the ceiling is lost in darkness.

https://t.co/8OxYry6JHp

In the third act, Palestrina is visited by the Pope, after much worry and anxiety. Borromeo throws himself down to beg forgiveness, but Palestrina does not want to punish him. Rather, he wants only to compose music. So he turns to the organ, and, we fade down to nothing at all.

For recording, there is no alternative. It must be the Kubelik recording. There is none better -- I have listened to every one.
https://t.co/yRMRf9JEAW

Please do not be afraid if you do not like it the first time! It gets better when you know it inside and out. It has the sustained mood of, when you are grieving, you have cried more than you have tears to cry; and so you sit, rigid and still.

papers.entry_text:
Autor, Beck, Dorn, Hanson: "Help for the Heartland? The Employment and Electoral Effects of the Trump Tariffs in the United States" (2024)
https://t.co/3QVNx0pMBc

detailed_thread.text:
So it turned out the Trump tariffs:

1) Didn't increase employment
2) Led to retaliatory tariffs, which hurt employment
3) Nevertheless, increased Republican vote share

Why must the voters reward politicians for bad policies which hurt them? https://t.co/R2Ru7aDDfE

Autor, Dorn, and Hanson (here joined by Anne Beck) have been using the  shift-share approach to international trade for a while. I wrote about it on my blog back in May, here.
https://t.co/UaE3gkOccX
Here's where the tariffs and farm subsidies landed. https://t.co/RBIMGGuuuF
And here the effect of tariff protection on the left, and on retaliatory tariff exposure on the right. https://t.co/tTctItgsl8
Two big reasons for the different effects. First, we protected products with with inelastic demand, and were ourselves tariffed on products with elastic demand. https://t.co/N7XiLa85TN
The other big reason is that these were often products in a supply chain. Greater employment in an upstream industry comes at the cost of lower employment in a downstream one. https://t.co/HsK93kIpAc

As for politics, they have a couple proxies for sentiment, but the big one is vote share. The increase in tariff exposure raised Trump's vote share by about 0.67%. The retaliatory tariffs lowered vote share, but enough to outweigh it. https://t.co/X5n29KklLA
It is telling that the voters think it wasn't particularly beneficial, but support it anyway. https://t.co/It3O2zgbIy

Autor, Beck, Dorn, Hanson: "Help for the Heartland? The Employment and Electoral Effects of the Trump Tariffs in the United States"
https://t.co/5ZnNEsP4wm

papers.entry_text:
Ali, Mihm, Siga: "The Political Economy of Zero-Sum Thinking" (2025)
https://t.co/GyAqwEepv9

detailed_thread.text: Why we so often behave as though the world is zero-sum when it isn't? This paper shows that even the slightest bit of asymmetric information is sufficient to cause us to go away from the optimal outcome, and lead to people to vote for things they don't want in expectation! 1/ https://t.co/Gh80eordYk

Consider three voters, Alice, Bob and Carol. They are deciding whether to enact policy P, and policy P*. P is a payoff of zero to all people. Policy P* gives two voters a pay off of 2, and one voter a payoff of -3. Which voter loses depends on the state of the world.

In expectation, the loser is random. With no information, all three vote for P* -- it's what maximizes their payoffs, on average. If everyone knows the state of the world, it passes 2-1. And if all three have the same amount of info, it will pass most of the time.

What happens if Alice knows the state of the world, and Bob and Carol don't? Well, they know that Alice would vote for P* if she's a winner, and P if she isn't. Thus, if B and C vote for P*, it's more likely to pass when they are losers. Knowing this, they always vote for P.

What is striking about this is that *any* amount of information is sufficient to cause things to unwind. If Alice possesses any information, then the only equilibrium is voting for the policy in which no one gains anything at all.
This explains why it is so hard for people to trust experts, who need not have preferences perfectly aligned with voters. In all, a neat paper.

Ali, Mihm, Siga: "The Political Economy of Zero-Sum Thinking" (2025)
https://t.co/43yvGhze3N

papers.entry_text:
Djourelova, Durante, Martin: "The Impact of Online Competition on Local Newspapers: Evidence from the Introduction of Craigslist" (2025)
https://t.co/Vwh5K3XFGa

detailed_thread.text: What killed the local newspaper? Craigslist. The actual reporting function of the newspaper was never what made it profitable -- it was simply a way to get people to look at the classified advertisements. With that gone, the work with positive externalities died. 1/ https://t.co/WZ8rRbYC44

This is a classic staggered roll-out paper -- if you are willing to buy the assumption that Craigslist expanded to different markets in a way that is unrelated to future trends, conditional on basic demographic controls, then you buy the paper. https://t.co/0oA8ClFjO5
This is certainly supported by the evidentiary record -- I must point out, it is *extremely hard* to win lawsuits that a company controller is not maximizing company profits. https://t.co/JYiaPWahkr
Also importantly, Craigslist is not a national website. It has a bunch of local advertisements, specific to each city it's in. https://t.co/ZMDomusZmo
When Craigslist enters, the number of classified ads in the local paper falls off a cliff.

For newspapers, both circulation and jobs fall. If having a classified manager proxies for the importance of classified ads, then the fall is larger for more dependent newspapers. https://t.co/oHyxSC16k3
And, coverage of politics falls for the more dependent papers. https://t.co/wCeS1IBznL
In particular, coverage of Congress falls. https://t.co/tvUGzni8ni
To put precise numbers on it, here's what one more year of exposure gets you. https://t.co/B4xZU0kaOA

Does it matter? The main effect seems to be that there is a small decrease in split ticket voting, and that in specifically regions with a classified manager, extremist candidates were more likely to win. https://t.co/YQ4wNVPGep

This is, unfortunately, a tough paper to evaluate. There are a lot of ways to cut up the data. I am choosing to believe that they have been basically honest in their analysis, and their methodological choices do not seem unusual.

Djourelova, Durante, Martin: "The Impact of Online Competition on Local Newspapers: Evidence from the Introduction of Craigslist" (2025)
https://t.co/A8bFTL47Pk

papers.entry_text:
Colonnelli, Pinho Neto, Teso: "Politics at Work" (2025)
https://t.co/mfUOlbbxns

detailed_thread.text: Employers (in Brazil) are much more likely to hire people of the same party -- even within a narrowly defined labor market, same party matches occur 50% more often than expected. This is at least four times as high as race and gender discrimination! 1/ https://t.co/G6jXvRZaY1

The effect size here is *really big*, and it is not simply due to people of one party being more prevalent in different areas. The researchers know this, and so control for it. https://t.co/rSkWaQ5cKQ
To give a sense of scale, here is it compared to race and gender. The closer to 1 or to 0, the closer to purely random matching it is. https://t.co/TlmAU42o02
This effect is, predictably enough, driven by the small firms. https://t.co/78KbzD1rOr

There's a few possible mechanisms. Maybe it's due to social networks. Maybe it's due to taste based discrimination. Maybe it's due to beliefs about who works most efficiently with others. Maybe it's political quid pro quo.

The simplest way is just to ask people. These results are kind of a muddle, although nobody thinks that it's a quid pro quo. There simply aren't enough people who might receive such favors. https://t.co/TdwiM7QD9C
And, consistent with no quid pro quo, there's no real change when parties are more or less powerful. https://t.co/iZw3CqyWST

An audit study, where they ask managers to evaluate resumes (for some reason they put the accents on resume, which makes it annoying to read) shows that managers rate resumes from their party higher. Notably, they have party already, so they don't need to ask, and prime them. https://t.co/lo6OyOgpaL

To test it being social networks, they see if occupations which require more interpersonal relations are more discriminatory -- and indeed they are. Assortative matching is also stronger when they come from that municipality, which indicates social ties. https://t.co/ZZGcekHUVe

Against that, though, they consider when an owner changes parties. Presumably, their social networks stay the same (I hope!), but the workers hired changes radically. There is evidence of an upward trend, consistent with people changing their minds before they change parties. https://t.co/zxXvatnJ7n
Being the wrong party makes you less likely to be promoted within the firm. https://t.co/3NRNQncAnL

What the paper is missing, and what future work should take up, is a consideration of the macroeconomic impacts. How much less efficient does it make the economy? What they can do is show that copartisans are less qualified. https://t.co/WYBHszt4Oq
And that more homogenous firms grow less, even after controlling for size! https://t.co/3HPyUcPmuM

Colonnelli, Pinho Neto, Teso: "Politics at Work" (2025)
https://t.co/YTiTaWUGE8

papers.entry_text:
Bursztyn and Jensen, "How Does Peer Pressure Affect Educational Investments?" (2015)
https://t.co/UcHoVomyXf

detailed_thread.text: One cause of worse academic performance among minorities may be a culture which penalizes being successful -- e.g. "acting white". In this RCT, when one's performance became public, effort plunged off a cliff -- with the largest losses among the erstwhile high performers. 1/ https://t.co/LUTMbdg6J4

The first experiment concerns a computer program for students taking remedial English and math. They are studying for an exam they must pass in order to get a high school diploma. https://t.co/yQO9OgSmIc

On September 20th, 2011, the company behind it introduced a leaderboard. Now you get points for answering questions correctly. What's more, you can see how you're doing relative to other students, and who the best students in the school are. https://t.co/6kAFTLQCpl

For the worst performers, nothing much changes, although there is a significant drop due to the number of observations. They were doing nothing beforehand, and they did nothing after. https://t.co/uO7v5NxvNB
For the top performers, they now average almost two correct questions per day per person fewer after the change. https://t.co/Ftqja1zUAW

To test if it was actually the change which was responsible, they re-run this regression for 218 other dates during the school year, and find nothing which gets as large of an effect. https://t.co/TTHKP9UvZr
The vast majority of the change is due to students exerting less effort, now that they are going to be known as successful. https://t.co/KC3VYi9tfF

The second experiment is in the poor parts of LA, where they offer free test prep for the SAT. The only difference between the forms they had to sign was that, in one, their enrollment would be kept private from the other people in the room, a change of one word. https://t.co/wpNX9O94q8

They run this experiment both in honors and nonhonors classes. For honors classes, there's no difference. For non-honors class, it's just on the edge of significance -- 11 percentage points lower. 93% of the time, there's a real difference. https://t.co/ngjrT7pEGh
We can then look at the non-honors students, and whether actually activating the account differs by whether they regard popularity as important. https://t.co/aZj0l4ED5U
This is not there among honors students. To me, this is fairly compelling evidence that they have a real effect, and they have pinned down the mechanism. https://t.co/SPXOLO8bhH

This cultural influence on outcomes has been long commented on -- including by President Obama. I do not have any solutions for it, though, besides merely saying that it is bad, and we should be against it. https://t.co/fuFvBZl0eG

Bursztyn and Jensen, "How Does Peer Pressure Affect Educational Investments?" (2015)
https://t.co/jNnoGlwiMb

papers.entry_text:
Liyan Shi, “Optimal Regulation of Noncompete Contracts” (2023)
https://t.co/0gsWIZ1Jey

detailed_thread.text: Optimal policy may be to ban non-competes, though assumptions are needed. Reading the paper, I found it to be extremely clarifying on *why* this might be the case. Essentially the firm and the worker implicitly collude together against new firms, to extract a higher valuation. 1/ https://t.co/6ujAhf0dEI

The model is in continuous time. Everyone is risk-neutral, and discounts the future at the same rate. Firms and workers match at some rate. Once they match, they agree to a contract of some length. Firms invest in the worker’s human capital, which can be ported from firm to firm.
In order to protect this investment, firms can insist upon a non-compete. These prevent the worker from going to another firm, unless that firm pays a buyout. https://t.co/NaE2cNrJRG
Because we assume risk-neutrality, the contract will be optimal for both the firm and the employee. What will make it good or bad for society is how it affects new entrants. https://t.co/iYBlzl01b9

Another key assumption is that workers can observe the current productivity of their worker-firm pairing, but can’t observe the productivity of themselves if they matched with another firm. Otherwise, the worker would always be able tp pay the buyout to the firm.

Instead, the non-compete now forces entering firms to pay a higher price. Here’s the thing — this *need not* go to the firm at all! It could go to the workers. However, it makes total production go down, and discourages new firms from entering.

Ultimately the paper rides on this assumption. I would tend to believe it, insofar as we don’t know productivities perfectly; however, as far as I can tell the model would deliver longer non-competes as it becomes more observable.
He had data on executives. Executives with a non-compete are less likely to leave, but are also better paid. https://t.co/7AwhW26xZz

He then estimates the model with what appears to be generalized method of moments. If you don’t know what that is, that’s okay. We basically have a bunch of features of the data which are parameters in the model, and we’re picking combinations till we get as close as possible.
With the model, we can then say the optimal non-compete length is 1.5 months — which is obviously nothing at all, and is pretty close to a ban. https://t.co/yP2ekI8pAw

I really would like to urge caution around the result. Totally asymmetric information is driving the result, and it would tend to be longer otherwise. However, the mechanism is extremely clever. I did not think of *employees* being in the wrong for non-competes.

Liyan Shi, “Optimal Regulation of Noncompete Contracts” (2023)
https://t.co/BaXPWYebrg

papers.entry_text:
Geruso and Layton, "Upcoding: Evidence from Medicare on Squishy Risk Adjustment" (2020)
https://t.co/qd0lleZT8r

detailed_thread.text: Medicare Advantage led to an increase in patient "risk" equivalent to 40% of consumers becoming diabetic overnight, or 6% becoming paraplegic. This is entirely due to discretion over coding patient health, and has led to tens of billions of dollars in pure waste. 1/ https://t.co/xi9UKhLqRz

Medicare Advantage is a program for providing health insurance to consumers, including the chronically ill. Medicare pays the insurer an amount based on the patients health status, such that the expected costs of all patients are the same. https://t.co/Pz2ZztemyF

The idea behind this is that, otherwise, insurers would want to screen out unhealthy patients. In the classic example, they put the health insurance office at the top of a four story walk-up, but there are many other ways to distort the benefits.
Of course, now there's an incentive to diagnose people with a condition they do not have, or to exaggerate the severity of the conditions which they do have. https://t.co/dCCvzZUAP2

We can tell these apart by comparing the fee-for-service public option, or the private Medicare Advantage plan. Inside each county, all consumers face the same options, and the subsidy to the insurer is determined by the risk status of each consumer.

The concern is that people will still select into different plans. Their method is to interact market penetration of MA plans with the average risk in a county. If there's no upcoding, it should make no difference to the average risk whether there's MA or not. https://t.co/Boiu2PWLlB

We can reasonably be concerned that there's endogeneity down there somewhere. Hopefully for this paper, there's no evidence of any difference in things which can't be manipulated. https://t.co/GPE5gYdWX4
As I said initially, the change in coding is absolutely enormous. This implies that we were spending $10 billion a year -- or $650 per person! -- in excess payments. https://t.co/xv3y5DDETG

What's more, we can track what happens to individuals when they reach 65, and can now shift to Medicare Advantage. Their risk score immediately jumps in a way that is simply totally implausible. https://t.co/VThaJD3jU2

This is not innocuous caution. When the insurers own the health insurance providers, they increase patient risk scores by almost three times as much. It is a deliberate effort to defraud the government. https://t.co/XLZRq8L6Wb
We are much too soft on healthcare fraud and waste. It is absolutely everywhere, yet we cannot easily touch it. Who wants to be the one to tell seniors no? Yet, we must.

Geruso and Layton, "Upcoding: Evidence from Medicare on Squishy Risk Adjustment" (2020)
https://t.co/sbnVCHPj4N

papers.entry_text:
Leon Musolff, “Algorithmic Pricing, Price Wars, and Tacit Collusion: Evidence from E-Commerce” (2026)
https://t.co/jKxFoZ40fA

detailed_thread.text: With online shopping, the cost of changing prices is very low. Yet, there is no reason to expect this to improve things for the consumer — in fact, we should expect things to worsen! We are currently saved only by the rudimentary nature of pricing algorithms. https://t.co/6E982gLVP2

Consider two sellers who can produce goods at constant marginal cost C, and are competing through the prices they set. Assume the goods are identical, so people always choose the cheaper. If price changes are costly, then we’d expect few of them, and for P to equal C.

Thus, even with two sellers, we get the competitive outcome of price equalling cost of production. Suppose, tho, that firms trade back and forth on price, but change costlessly. Then, each firm will undercut each other to try and claim the whole market.

Once we get down to cost, though, the sellers are incentivized to go up, and then fall down again. This is called an Edgeworth cycle. These aren’t humans, btw — they’re simply adaptive price algorithms. https://t.co/4RoT721fOh
In the limit, if price changes happen instantaneously, then everyone enter with the monopoly price. You never actually gain for lowering your price.

The only thing foiling this is the lack of sophistication on the part of the sellers. Here, Merchant C set on price and walked away — but A and B are still trying to induce a price rise, like kids trying to get a sick friend to play outside with. https://t.co/drIA01lxBH
I am concerned about this. It not clear what anti-trust can do, now.

Leon Musolff, “Algorithmic Pricing” (2026)

https://t.co/7HhvYmplkA

papers.entry_text:
Daniel Herbst, "Asymmetric Information in Wage Contracts: Evidence from an Online Experiment" (2025)
https://t.co/I9OsSMHVym

detailed_thread.text: No taxes on tips would be a terrible idea, it turns out, for the same reason that Obamacare is good. This comes from an elegant experiment pulling out losses due to moral hazard, and losses due to adverse selection, in different types of wage contracts. 1/ https://t.co/P9jdqEISzn

The simplest way to incentivize someone to do something is to pay them for producing it. This implies paying some schedule of prices per unit of output, or a piece-rate. However, this isn't quite what workers want -- they would like insurance. Fixed wages provide this.

But it does so at a cost. Now you have an incentive to do less work. This is called moral hazard.

Still though, this could be efficient, were it not for adverse selection. Suppose you have to offer higher wages to attract more productive workers, but have to pay the same to all.

Then you have a problem similar to monopsony. The wage paid must be equal to the average value, not the marginal value (efficiency would be paying each person their marginal product). https://t.co/5lqJBBbFmg

Herbst separately estimates moral hazard and adverse selection in order to test counterfactuals with an online experiment. He has online workers complete a data entry task, in exchange for either a piece-rate, or a randomized hourly wage, which pulls out moral hazard.

To identify adverse selection, he has some of the people who took a low wage hourly rate get offered a higher wage. Thus, they have the same ultimate conditions -- the only difference is what they selected. https://t.co/mVvxMGpiXV

Consistent with this, working under an hourly wage lowers productivity of a worker. Offering a higher hourly wage raises the marginal productivity. We can trace out the supply and demand curves, and then use it to evaluate counterfactuals. https://t.co/P2u3DF3mn6

Which, how do we get taxes on tips from all this? Simple -- it's less distortionary than a tax on hourly wages. We would all like fixed wages to provide us insurance, but piece-rates cause this to unravel. Taxing tips is like Obamacare.

Daniel Herbst, "Asymmetric Information in Wage Contracts: Evidence from an Online Experiment" (2025)
https://t.co/CUpEHqEl8t

papers.entry_text:
Einav, Finkelstein, Mahoney, Okun: "Racial Differences in Nursing Home Value Added" (2025)
https://t.co/77qyJMdEmF

detailed_thread.text: Black people have much worse health than white people. Why? Even after this, it's still not clear, but we can rule one thing out -- it is *not*, at least in nursing homes, due to different access to facilities. Reallocating Black patients would close, at most, 5% of the gap. 1/ https://t.co/6h9fpkcWzN

The starting point for this are the health value-added scores for nursing homes, which were developed in their prior paper. These account for selection in and out, which simply looking at inputs won't pick up on.
https://t.co/2KTkvBRIN0

Nursing homes are not hospices. The point is to provide short term care, before discharging back into the community. Black patients nevertheless stay much longer, despite being younger. This is largely due to them being sicker when coming in. https://t.co/6ybf4WMyWS

That isn't our outcome of interest, though. We want to see how much the patient improves, after accounting for selection. When we do so, Black patients see 30% less value added from nursing homes than white patient do.
We can test what's explaining this. Redistributing patients does nothing. Giving them the white value-added of a given facility (row 1) https://t.co/t2DXRmsjCg

The paper is largely silent on why the within nursing home difference occurs happens. They do eliminate the possibility of it being due to the difference in marriage rates, because the gap doesn't close once controlling for that.

I think the most likely explanation is poor adherence to health plans. There's evidence from the military, where Black patients with Black doctors see way better mortality outcomes, driven largely by Black people taking their medication as directed when the doctor's black. https://t.co/NiKilTZ2NB

I think a racism explanation is unlikely. First, it would have to be strikingly homogenous across facilities. Second, nursing homes with more Black people do see less of a gap -- but it's through whites doing worse, not anyone doing better.

Einav, Finkelstein, Mahoney, Okun: "Racial Differences in Nursing Home Value Added" (2025)
https://t.co/2hs0AFzaoP

papers.entry_text:
Raval, Rosenbaum, Wilson, "How Do Machine Learning Algorithms Perform in Predicting Hospital Choice?" (2021)
https://t.co/r5kB4zxEnz

detailed_thread.text: In order to predict the effect of mergers, we need to estimate demand. When we do so, we make definite predictions about what would happen if one of the hospitals were not there -- because of, for instance, a tornado. How accurate are these predictions? And is ML better? https://t.co/uXkXm9h5k4

If you have not hitherto believed an exogenous shock before, here's one you should believe. With normal, BLP style demand estimation, to oversimplify greatly, we're estimating the distribution of consumer preferences, and then simulating what happens if choices available change. https://t.co/39P9LcLCch

Importantly, we're inferring the most basic stuff imaginable, which then leads to observed behavior. These are called "primitives". Ideally, this allows us to be extremely flexible -- if our model is right, we can answer the results of *any* change in the world.

Machine learning, by contrast, is agnostic as to what the root cause is. It's only predictive. We would expect that it might do better for small changes, but worse for larger changes. It also can't tell us anything about what to change, if we're trying out policies.

And this holds up in the data! Compared to baseline, which is BLP random coefficients (estimating the primitives), various ML methods do better for small changes, but fall off for large changes. https://t.co/QoIOC04xYA
The baseline model gets the choices of 40% of consumers right. I would like to defend this -- BLP is primarily about aggregate outcomes, not individual predictions. So, I think this is good.
There is much to be said for implementing these in policy functions, because they are so, so much faster than BLP, which takes a week to do once. https://t.co/YuHK8IRtHu

Raval, Rosenbaum, Wilson, "How Do Machine Learning Algorithms Perform in Predicting Hospital Choice?" (2021)
https://t.co/KqVKuxRZUR

papers.entry_text:
Jacob Moscona, "The Management of Aid and Conflict in Africa" (2025)
https://t.co/oR9ia384HB

detailed_thread.text: Management matters everywhere -- including in preventing conflict. Moving from a 25th percentile quality manager to a 75th percentile manager reduces the likelihood of a conflict by 15%. Regardless of your view on Maduro, Trump running it can make any project bad. 1/ https://t.co/S7A9L93A6W

We need a few ingredients to make this paper. We need data on conflicts, data on aid, a way of measuring manager quality, and some way of randomly assigning managers to projects. The first two are easy, and are generally available already. https://t.co/cUmQrBzEWK

Manager quality comes from the assignment of managers to multiple projects, which are then rated by a review board. We can then estimate how much of quality is explained by the manager, conditional upon narrow geographic regions.

Finally, we need for managers to not be sorted in ways correlated with quality. Anecdotally, this seems to be the case; the sorting would also have to be into subnational units, not by region or country.
He has an instrument for the effect of aid in aggregate, where we're only counting variation due to change in World Bank capacity. https://t.co/7s0u1PDBW2
Receiving a well-run project reduces conflict. https://t.co/TcowYx1ZMg
Splitting into well-run and poorly-run projects, we can see the effect on anything to do with conflict. Good management is good. https://t.co/SQdu8yMYiJ
God help us if they leave Stephen Miller in charge of Venezuela.

Jacob Moscona, "The Management of Aid and Conflict in Africa" (2025)
https://t.co/H2j5ottybF

papers.entry_text:
Adam Soliman, "Disrupting Drug Markets: The Effects of Crackdowns on Rogue Opioid Sellers" (2025)
https://t.co/A0xJCQaqS4

detailed_thread.text: Despite the abstract, this paper is incredibly strong evidence that the DEA cracking down on prescription opioids is what is actually responsible for overdose deaths. If you want to actually do something about the opioid crisis, legalize opioids! 1/ https://t.co/mNftPI7OqT
Prescriptions for opioids are driven by the right tail of doctors, and issuing even one enforcement action causes a precipitate decline in the amount prescribed. https://t.co/HgNXqYdMSX
Note that cracking down on pharmacies does not do the same thing. Other pharmacies begin dispensing opioids more, and have more behavior flagged as suspicious.

Comparing to places which had suspicious prescription practices, but did not see an enforcement action, there is a small but non-significant change in total overdoses. The change in opioid deaths is counterbalanced by substitution to heroin. https://t.co/Agz7CRwTy9
This one is significant -- given that the sample mean is .633 deaths per 100,000, this is an increase of almost 50%! https://t.co/JgozpXIU8z

So how can we square the circle? The end of the sample period is 2014. Fentanyl starts picking up in 2013. Pushing people off of safer prescription drugs onto unregulated street stuff left them vulnerable to a massive increase in the danger of pills. https://t.co/1dkPvK03Yd
Two solutions. Either we allow people to use relatively safe drugs, or we actually arrest users and throw them in prison. Trying to stop smuggling a drug with 10,000 doses a gram is pointless.

If you want to solve the opioid crisis, we need people to use drugs which do not randomly kill them. The simplest way is just to give people drugs which do not randomly kill them. This seems better than arresting everybody.

Adam Soliman, "Disrupting Drug Markets: The Effects of Crackdowns on Rogue Opioid Sellers" (2025)
https://t.co/kq2rzKyw0C

papers.entry_text:
Maria Micaela Sviatschi, "Making a Narco: Childhood Exposure to Illegal Labor Markets and Criminal Life Paths" (2022)
https://t.co/PNHTnoL1vP

detailed_thread.text: This is a really incredible paper on why people end up becoming criminals. Children who work in growing coca for illegal purposes are more likely to end up in prison later. Conditional cash transfers can get kids in school, and out of gangs later in life. 1/ https://t.co/2CfFxdJoIk
Obviously, everything is endogenous. Her identification is from price shocks from actions taken by Colombia, which cracked down on coca production between 1997 and 2003. https://t.co/2p2fOjWqGm
Kids are more likely to go to work in the fields instead of going to school, in the areas which are more suited for growing coca. https://t.co/grdpA4v2sc
This leads to them dropping out of school... https://t.co/X9fUTq5raL

And going to prison later on. People working in the legal industry were no more likely to go to prison for violent crimes, although the effect remains for drugs and overall criminality. https://t.co/AhwLXyDkFs
No other commodity has this effect. https://t.co/gLPYccrkWO
This tracks with field interviews. https://t.co/d03GH0xwPS
Providing cash, conditional on the child attending school every day, pulls them out of the fields and into the schools, reducing later criminality. https://t.co/CLmTFxCk2i

Maria Micaela Sviatschi, "Making a Narco: Childhood Exposure to Illegal Labor Markets and Criminal Life Paths" (2022)
https://t.co/QKaWbMiam0

papers.entry_text:
Jacob Moscona, "Agricultural Development and Structural Change, Within and Across Countries" (2019)
https://t.co/7brG1TWKKd

detailed_thread.text: A somewhat negative take on the Green Revolution: while it increased agricultural productivity in India, this kept people from moving to the cities and was sort of a cul-de-sac for further growth. I'm not sure I agree, but it's well-argued 1/ https://t.co/YiK4prcbWj

Between 1960 and 2000, global food production doubled -- from 1 billion to 2 billion tons a year. The Green Revolution was primarily about introducing new, genetically engineered or selected varieties of crops, often complemented with more intensive use of inputs.
One strain of rice could produce five times as much rice in less time, even allowing for a second crop to be grown. https://t.co/9WNO1AnIil
This really was a revolution -- adoption was extremely fast. https://t.co/gOm6uDSuL3

The variation which drives the paper is the difference in natural conditions between districts. Crop varieties are more effective in different areas. If which varieties were developed is uncorrelated with change in growth rate, then the roll-out tells us the real effect.
There exists data on potential crop yields for the whole world, from the FAO GAEZ. https://t.co/WEVvMYMDxV
Agricultural productivity increased where there were more potential from the new varieties -- and this was not due to a latent increasing trend beforehand. https://t.co/lTS6UJ2vUv
Naturally, agricultural wages increased, as did road access and healthcare availability (although primary and high school attendance did not). https://t.co/HHeROqzdb7

But not all is perfect. People reallocated from non-farm to fam labor when productivity increased, but this is kind of a dead-end for future growth. This is strongest in places which are more open to trade, as those districts specialize only in agriculture.

Now how about we consider countries? We lack the detailed data to do the district analysis again, but we can compare countries as a whole. If we look at total GDP, there's no association between being exposed to the Green Revolution and higher GDP -- if anything, it falls. https://t.co/Or1O2pAAzZ

Instead, agricultural productivity increases, but everything else falls. I'm inclined to believe something similar in India. It's actually one of the least urbanized countries, given its level of development, and would likely be better off if people reallocated spatially.

Jacob Moscona, "Agricultural Development and Structural Change, Within and Across Countries" (2019)
https://t.co/oXnuPopzYs

papers.entry_text:
Moscona, Nunn, Robinson: "Segmentary Lineage Organization and Conflict in Sub-Saharan Africa" (2020)
https://t.co/2lLzjCiwPK

detailed_thread.text: One cause of conflict in Africa are obligations to your family members. In ethnic groups which compel you to come to the aid of distant family members, conflicts can become much larger. A slight insult turns into a fight with cousins, which turns into tribal war. 1/ https://t.co/ObXosGt52K

Somalia is notorious for this. Society is organized into clans who all trace ancestry back to a single founder. If faced with an outside threat, everyone is bound to support their nearest family members. Conflict just keeps going. https://t.co/OpXo3ybgVN

This should lead us to predict that ethnic groups which are not organized this way -- they do not have segmentary lineage -- should have fewer conflicts, and that conflict should drop off when we cross from one place to another. https://t.co/gEWCDxE7Xq
Of course, we cannot just take these boundaries for granted. Survey data bears out the ethnic classifications, though -- identification jumps abruptly at a border. https://t.co/P8CRCfK4dc
So, we can compare records of violent incidents, and the places they occurred at, and compare the difference between them at the border. https://t.co/IVbHyojVVD
There is definitely an effect here, an effect you can see without the regression lines and just from the dots. https://t.co/GKe5aJqGuA

The ethnic groups in the two groups, and comparing inside and outside the sample, appear very similar on observable characteristics. They also test to see if a pile of different characteristics change at the borders -- they do not. https://t.co/vMs0H6iG7l
Why does this happen? Exactly as the hypothesis predicts, conflicts, once they start, last longer. There are now more people who get involved, and it just drags on as other people arrive.

Family loyalty is the antithesis of civilization. A good world is one where you treat your family like you would anyone else. It really annoys me when right-wingers fetishize helping your family out -- it is fundamentally anti-Western.

Moscona, Nunn, Robinson: "Segmentary Lineage Organization and Conflict in Sub-Saharan Africa" (2020)
https://t.co/Ht5yVePmqS

papers.entry_text:
Jacob Moscona and Karthik Sastry, "Does Directed Innovation Mitigate Climate Damage? Evidence from U.S. Agriculture" (2022)
https://t.co/gRFpB5wv3Q

detailed_thread.text: Projections of the cost of climate change are often incomplete, because they do not consider how the direction of technical change is affected by the threat of climate change. While it reduces damages, it does not offset them -- but do know that this is an underestimate! 1/ https://t.co/wb2bzs80fG

Most prior work does not actually address climate change at all. Rather, they use energy cost shocks in order to identify it, such as in Popp (2002). Climate need not increase oil prices, though! https://t.co/BDtSSbePjA

This line of literature is really only useful for assessing the effect of policy changes, like a carbon tax! Obviously useful, but we'd really like to know what would happen without action. https://t.co/VqoFjw5gi2

The first step is that we have the productivities of every crop for every combination of soil and climate characteristics. This is like if each place now were instead a different grid. Finding the base case of no innovation is simple.

The direction of innovation is trickier. What exactly is the exogenous variation to be? They kinda just assume they don't really need it -- they can simply see if crops more exposed to higher temperatures see more innovations.
They do find that this is the case. This is not a small or subtle effect. https://t.co/PimGY3XET2
The growth from 1980 is not predicted by changes beforehand. https://t.co/D8zsmEJk5l

They do various robustness checks which, in the interest of time, I'm pretty much just glossing over. Now, how much has it reduced climate change damages by? And how much will it reduce it by? We can plug in our parameters and get an answer.

One big caveat -- assuming that climate-change driven technologies have no value otherwise is a pretty big assumption. Unfortunately, we just have no grounds for any particular number, so it always be a little made up. These are thus conservative estimates. https://t.co/XWnWokRKtw

A reasonable defense of this is that we know from other work that technologies developed for one context are not all that great in other contexts, at least in agriculture. Induced innovation against heat is certainly not always valuable. https://t.co/nbXm0QtMbA

Innovation has mitigated at least 20% of the damage from climate change, and 13% of future damages. Note that this is all partial equilibrium. Crops might be getting better *in general* -- this is *only* innovation induced by climate change. https://t.co/LwVbq9PLrX
Other literature is pretty clear that we don't need to worry too much about crop switching and trade -- they have only a minor impact on outcomes under climate change. https://t.co/F8kZWXUvCf

I both love and hate this article. I love the work it did. I really quite dislike the framing of the specific numbers. These are, by construction, conservative numbers. As a professor once told me, when you put a number in the abstract, the paper becomes about the number.
So you'd better be pretty darn sure you want policymakers reading that number, and nothing else! Heck, they won't even read this thread -- good luck with them understanding the paper.

Jacob Moscona and Karthik Sastry, "Does Directed Innovation Mitigate Climate Damage? Evidence from U.S. Agriculture" (2022)
https://t.co/BHQeAeGx9F

papers.entry_text:
Moscona and Sastry, "Inappropriate Technology: Evidence from Global Agriculture" (2025)
https://t.co/v8K3TcQCzU

detailed_thread.text: Developing countries can't adopt all the technologies of the developed world. Often, the treatments for crop pests and pathogens are specific to one region or one country. Differences in innovation incentives are responsible for 15-20% of agricultural productivity differences. 1/ https://t.co/oZuBjBlmfb
Crop pests and pathogens are a big deal for agriculture, eating up a substantial part of output each year. https://t.co/xIsJTKHBTh

Some technologies affect pests of all kinds, but many are specific. In particular, breeding varieties of plants to be resistant need have no overlap at all with any other pest or pathogens. https://t.co/x4HN3eLZ25

This paper is the first to add up the total effects of this. For each leader country, which produces the majority of research, we can see how similar or dissimilar the follower countries are. https://t.co/tMyISiLfRX

There exists data on every crop variety out there, as part of an international intellectual property. Country of origin is defined as first use, to prevent corporate headquarters being misleading. https://t.co/SseTn0xVeS

Adding fixed effects indicates that both a cheaper cost of doing research locally, the big markets being richer, and some markets having greater IP protections, increase the rate of technological development. https://t.co/elh3pxOnO8
If you're like the big country, that's great. If you aren't, this is pretty bad for you. https://t.co/ObbbD4L93b

This is pretty specific to crop varieties, because technologies like a mechanical harvester (which are the same everywhere) transfer just fine. At least, they are not affected by ecological characteristics. https://t.co/YizRRcR1im

Bringing it all together, they find that ecological mismatch reduces global agricultural productivity by 57%, and is responsible for 15% of the gap between developed and undeveloped countries. https://t.co/1zyN94X7V4

My takeaway is that the case for weakening IP law in developing countries is weaker than it seems. The technology often can't be adopted anyway, and must be researched separately. It also argues for patents being good.

Moscona and Sastry, "Inappropriate Technology: Evidence from Global Agriculture" (2025)
https://t.co/f6UxkI2tzf

papers.entry_text:
Roussille and Scuderi, "Bidding For Talent: A Test of Conduct in a High-Wage Labor Market" (2025)
https://t.co/tUvVwHVzJy

detailed_thread.text: An exceptional paper using super cool data. I fear that their methods will not generalize beyond the incredible dataset they have, but they illustrate something important -- perfect competition in the labor market exists approximately nowhere. If you like methods, read this. 1/ https://t.co/WrTAIKOXnO

The outcomes of imperfect competition are dependent upon "conduct", which is how firms compete with each other. For example: do firms set wages independent of each other, or do they interact? And do they individualize the wage, or offer the same to everyone?

Typically, these facts are just assumed. They are not tested. Yet, they have a substantial impact on measured markdowns, and thus on normative interpretations. These have different policy implications. https://t.co/4PqrIaRxQ3

We need to do a few separate things, most of which are normally impossible without very detailed data. First, we need to estimate the amenities of firms. Otherwise, we would interpret firms with higher non-wage amenities as a markdown.

Then, we need to estimate a labor supply curve, taking into account that there may be many types of workers and different amenities at firms. Finally, if the model of conduct is right, things that shift labor supply should be uncorrelated with the productivity residual.

To do this detailed stuff, you need the possible matches which workers choose not to accept, and this is where hired dot com came in. They allow firms to bid on workers -- mostly software engineers in SF -- and so we can observe all of the possible matches for a worker. https://t.co/Kq9p1okwEI
Amenities are done by revealed preferences. There seem to be three types, which correspond roughly to preferring startups, established firms, and being neutral. https://t.co/gIkv9pklUr
Key to this is the thickness of bidding for workers. We can get https://t.co/vZIhICRSnX

To test conduct, what should happen is that there is no correlation between the instrument and the residuals. As we can see, this is much more more true for monopolistic competition than oligopoly, without personalized offers, and this is robust to different instruments. https://t.co/xjWqIngwLh

So what does this all mean? Well, it looks like we're safe not considering strategic interactions. That part is always very fiddly, and simplifying is a great help. Markdowns are also lower than we would otherwise think, though not insubstantial.
Above all, though, there is no such thing as perfect competition in the labor market. It is always imperfectly competitive.

Roussille and Scuderi, "Bidding For Talent: A Test of Conduct in a High-Wage Labor Market" (2025)

https://t.co/c55qOUDkdu

papers.entry_text:
Desmond Ang and Sahil Chinoy, "Vanguard: Black Veterans and Civil Rights After World War I" (2026)
https://t.co/hUVX5FkSVz

detailed_thread.text: The experience of joining the Army during the First World War led African-American males to become more supportive of Civil Rights, and to be more likely to join the NAACP n https://t.co/BPKFBru4qZ

That there was some sort of relationship between the war and interest in civil rights jumps off the page in the time series -- there was a big spike in membership after the wars. https://t.co/zofKvA2JA4
This, however, is the first paper to seriously examine the hypothesis from the ground up. They digitize the draft records of millions of veterans, and link them to the census. https://t.co/y0cujjlAVZ

People who were randomly selected to join the Army were three times as likely to become members of the NAACP. This effect is especially strong when they applied for an exemption, but were denied it by the draft board. https://t.co/MRrHRpMCv8

This is not due to a change in occupational status, or strikingly, due to a change in civic involvement in general. The interest in organizing was restricted to the NAACP, and not to other kinds of fraternal organizations. https://t.co/EZmpt0fzuz
Instead, it seems driven by discriminatory treatment during the war. More biased training camps led to more activist veterans. https://t.co/cC8LxaJDcu
In surveys after the war, people who went to more discriminatory camps were way more likely to mention "injustice", and considerably more likely to vote. https://t.co/NsgHxpxJrI
This is a really cool paper. In my view, it is an example of how all good history can and should be quantitative.

Desmond Ang and Sahil Chinoy, "Vanguard: Black Veterans and Civil Rights After World War I" (2026)
https://t.co/f3DJw1Gylz

papers.entry_text:
Finkelstein, McQuillan, Zidar, Zwick, "The Health Wedge and Labor Market Inequality" (2023)
https://t.co/VqTxS3CkqG

detailed_thread.text: Health insurance being through the employer means that there is an effective regressive tax on low wage employees, because health insurance costs the same for each person. This is a big deal! Financing through a payroll tax would increase non-college earnings by $1,700 a year! 1/ https://t.co/vbOiQ5O8qO

It's a really big deal. Health insurance premiums cost about $12,000 a year, no matter the worker -- an enormous sum for low-wage workers, and something trivial for high earners. https://t.co/A47AbudHBB

Solving for the full general equilibrium implications of changing the system would be an ambitious exercise. They set their sights lower -- holding fixed the choice to provide health insurance, how elastic is employment to cost?

The whole reason we have employer healthcare is because, due to an historical quirk in WWII, we made it tax free. Naturally, employers provide pay in the form of healthcare, and we spend too much. https://t.co/z3NCjaLcc0
Absolutely nobody likes this. It is untouchable politically, but economic insanity. https://t.co/eDK2g7OMHm
Onwards to the model, it's pretty simple. We just have an estimated labor elasticity, and a change in tax rates.
For the results, they have this in table 3 -- college educated wages fall, and non-college wages rise. https://t.co/dgaw6OlBXq
We really need to abolish this darn thing! It's the silliest thing imaginable.

Finkelstein, McQuillan, Zidar, Zwick, "The Health Wedge and Labor Market Inequality" (2023)
https://t.co/5L8uz5t2DY

papers.entry_text:
Markevich, Naumenko, Qian: "The Causes of Ukrainian Famine Mortality, 1932-33" (2025)
https://t.co/yMAInero3i

detailed_thread.text: The Holodomor was a genocide against the Ukrainians, and not an accidental result of natural causes combined with a poor state response. 1/ https://t.co/oi34Ago06J

The basic concern is that, if Ukrainians are disproportionately in agricultural regions, then the famine affecting those more might be spuriously associated. It might seem odd, but a common pattern in state-caused famines is for the rural regions to be more affected.

The state sets some quota for agricultural surplus to come from each region in proportion to the surplus produced before. Thus, the cities are never expected to give anything at all. If production is lower than expected, the real tax rate for the countryside goes way up.

The debate on whether it was a genocide had stalled out because there were no smoking guns. Stalin did not issue a direct order to kill the Ukrainians in the way they did the Poles in the forest. https://t.co/QgFps6bin2

They assemble a database (my understanding is that this took years) which enables them to answer the questions others can't. In particular, they rebuild the (manipulated) aggregate statistics out of local production figures. https://t.co/iA6yMq76eg
The key results are these: Ukraine had enough to feed itself. Places with more Ukrainians, all else equal, had higher procurement targets and greater famine deaths. This extended outside Ukraine.

Markevich, Naumenko, Qian: "The Causes of Ukrainian Famine Mortality, 1932-33" (2025)
https://t.co/O61dNbiVLr

papers.entry_text:
Atalay, Frost, Sorensen, Sullivan, Zhu: "Scalable Demand and Markups" (2025)
https://t.co/vckk6dDgMX

detailed_thread.text: It is plausible that not only did market power by firms increase, but also that "shrinkflation" is to blame! 1/ https://t.co/mNuyx6vuh2

When it comes to estimating market power, the supply side is (for a variety of reasons) not believable, and the demand side is not feasible with modern methods. By returning to primitive methods, however, we can say something about aggregate markups.

Modern demand estimation grows out of the framework of BLP 1995. Consumers have heterogenous preferences over characteristics and price, represented as a distribution. That allows us to have realistic patterns of substitution.

Otherwise, substitution would be only on the basis of market shares. The entry of an identical product to another would cause massive changes, when it shouldn't. You can get around this by nesting, where you have different elasticities for close products and further bundles.

However, this was largely discarded as primitive and arbitrary. The (much more computationally intensive) BLP methods have generally prevailed. 

So it might be a bit surprising, then, to have the authors return to the primitive and arbitrary solutions.

Well, when you have limited data on products, or when you need to do it at scale for thousands and thousands of products, you need to make things simpler. This paper's advance is using micro data to guide the nesting.
They have Nielsen Homescan data, both the retail scanner and the consumer panel dataset. These have tens of thousands of products with billions of transactions. https://t.co/g8YzbjwZYZ

We assume that there are stable preferences, but that sometimes people change what they purchase on visits due to external shocks. Note that products can actually belong to many overlapping nests. https://t.co/gUjZZMtfZj
Looking at the clusters to sanity test it -- yeah, these totally make sense and line up with what I would expect. https://t.co/fpDo0jlS1D
Carbonated soft drinks check out too. https://t.co/afZZVrDpXo
Now that we have this, we can estimate how markups have changed over time, which previously we simply couldn't do at scale.
Between 2006 and 2018, mean markups (weighted by revenue) increased from 0.37 to 0.52. The markup formula is (P-MC)/P. https://t.co/vujj2ww0EZ

So, why? First, consumers are growing less price sensitive. This could be happening for benign reasons (as people get richer, they pay less attention to prices) or for less benign reasons. Specifically, the average size of food containers shrunk.
If this was companies figuring out that consumers were quite price sensitive, but not size sensitive, this is  plausibly bad. https://t.co/ekve4Fy5OM
They rule out changes in sample composition. Instead, reductions in package size was associated with increases in markups. https://t.co/toRyAcQfbt

I am inclined to believe this more than I am the production approach to markup estimation, which suffers from a host of issues (the screenshot here is from a forthcoming blog post). https://t.co/1NrDATiERu
You'll also note the touch of Alan Sorensen here -- I swear, everything he does has something to do with clustering to simplify the estimation of substitution patterns.
Don't be scared by the page count of the paper, btw! The vast majority is the appendix, in which they report all of the clusters of all of the products.

Atalay, Frost, Sorensen, Sullivan, Zhu: "Scalable Demand and Markups" (2025)
https://t.co/5Uw7fyZl3u

papers.entry_text: https://t.co/CMy0uPQEgv

detailed_thread.text: This is suggestive evidence for a theory of the world I've had for a while. I think we achieve only what we believe is possible, and so culture can have an enormous impact on economic growth. If we change people's beliefs about the future, everyone would be better off. 1/ https://t.co/vNvz6azkYh

The setting is Ethiopia. They randomly assign some people to watch a documentary of how peasants just like them where able to rise out of poverty, and others to watch light entertainment (and others just to be surveyed). https://t.co/qb7vXMXKcA
These are, to be clear, enormous effects -- so enormous that my mind throws up red flags. I am trusting the QJE here to rule out the worst! https://t.co/KPzWITkRVe
For mechanisms, the improvements to material conditions came from increasing their aspirations for the future. https://t.co/KdeNNrai8X

The main concern, I think, for the study is that the area they ran the study in is ridiculously poor and isolated. Even for Africa, this is extreme -- 4% of people had a cellphone, and absolutely no one had a TV. https://t.co/kDkmh3TdsI

I don't think that raising aspirations would be a bad thing anywhere. I simply think it's going to be much harder than just watching a documentary, when for many people this might be the first time they had *ever* watched a screen.

What this reminds me of is work on who innovates, and why. Studies like Bell et al find that innovation is greatly affected by exposure at a very narrow level. I think this is because it changes people's beliefs about what is possible. https://t.co/WzWHIq1Z2E

Bernard, Dercon, Orkin, Schinaia, Taffesse: "The Future in Mind: Aspirations and Long-Term Outcomes in Rural Ethiopia" (2026)
https://t.co/4upIrESNoW

papers.entry_text:
Fajgelbaum, Goldberg, Kennedy, Khandelwal "The Return to Protectionism" (2020)
https://t.co/ewiV5oLkFt

detailed_thread.text: What happened when Trump imposed tariffs the first time? The burden of the tax fell entirely upon U.S. consumers, and GDP fell by $7.2 billion. 1/ https://t.co/W1WMlygalb

The tariffs in 2018 were much smaller than the ones of the second Trump administration -- starting with solar panels, washing machines, and aluminum, and then sweeping tariffs on China. Today they are on all products of way more nations. https://t.co/Z9oxFWRTN9

The starting point for any study of tariffs is a comparison of before and after -- what is called a regression-discontinuity. We impose tariffs, and there is an immediate decrease in imports and an increase in prices. The same holds for exports. https://t.co/5SSMMTatsz

To figure out the aggregate effects, we need a model. Since we are in trade, we'll be working with constant-elasticity-of-substitution (or CES) preferences, where people gain for having a variety of goods, and, conditional upon "nests", all varieties are equally good.
Something nifty is that the import tariffs, if we believe them to be exogenous, can simultaneously identify the elasticities for import demand and export supply. https://t.co/LVoaBR3gLS
If we look at who was affected, the import tariffs were designed to benefit largely Republican voting places. https://t.co/SFzewtjYyf
Meanwhile, the retaliatory tariffs fell largely upon agriculture in the Great Plains. https://t.co/Tzcdbvnmdn
Putting this together, we can see how very Republican places saw smaller tariff increases, and much larger retaliatory tariff increases. https://t.co/D5XiiMVeZ3

They find that the tariffs caused losses of $51 billion to domestic importers. However, we do have to consider reallocation to domestic producers, although we are going to simplify a bit and not consider how tariffs on upstream goods might be especially deleterious.
Thus, you get the headline figure of $7.2 billion in real income losses. This does, to be clear, include government tax revenues.

Fajgelbaum, Goldberg, Kennedy, Khandelwal "The Return to Protectionism" (2020)
https://t.co/sx5HSxm4v2

papers.entry_text:
Eliason, Heebsh, McDevitt, Roberts: "How Acquisitions Affect Firm Behavior and Performance: Evidence from the Dialysis Industry" (2020)
https://t.co/vEjkE9bwC2

detailed_thread.text: Mergers and acquisitions greatly increased the cost of dialysis to the government and worsened outcomes, but interestingly, not through the exertion of market power. Rather, it came through learning about which drugs Medicare was overpaying for, and bilking the hell out of it. 1/ https://t.co/LZazZy6UvC

Consider EPOGEN, which stimulates the growth of red blood cells, at the cost of $8,000 per patient. (In 2009 -- that's $13,000 today!). Facilities bought by chains more than doubled their prescriptions for it. https://t.co/AvxkZmd9ES
This change is immediate, and jumps off the page. https://t.co/Dh98Iz07Rg
The reason behind using Venofer is particularly scummy. Venofer came in bigger vials, so you could use part of a vial, and then charge as if they had used the whole thing. https://t.co/bn9DNJ9Zha
They also increase patient load per worker, and reduce the number of skilled employees. Mortality increases, and hospitalization rates increase too. https://t.co/8m0uhGQVhO
The identification strategy is within-patients differences-in-differences, where the same patients are tracked over time. https://t.co/zzmdTWs5P2

They argue against this being market power by comparing what happens when an acquisition does and does not meaningfully change market concentration. Since patients hardly ever change providers due to quality changes, the effect of an acquisition is the same.

It is not at all clear what we should do about firms learning to be unethical as a matter of competition policy. We can't rule out the possibility that firms would have eventually learned how to defraud Medicare on their own.
What it does show, along with many other pieces of evidence, is that Medicare is absolutely riddled with fraud. Every dollar spent on auditing saves $24 in wasteful care. https://t.co/K0rp2mevz9

Eliason, Heebsh, McDevitt, Roberts: "How Acquisitions Affect Firm Behavior and Performance: Evidence from the Dialysis Industry" (2020)
https://t.co/xjDLHKkS83

papers.entry_text:
Benjamin Enke, "What You See is All There Is" (2020)
https://t.co/cQA0WLpiJA

detailed_thread.text: A neat little experiment. People tend to act as though they are receiving all relevant information, even when they should know they are not. Thus, they over update small bits of info, which explains why we are ruled by anecdotes. 1/ https://t.co/5LzJ3BayCD

The experiment is simple. Six numbers are drawn, centered around 100. One of those numbers is shown at random. People are then asked the odds that the average of the numbers is above 100. https://t.co/kc1QCr4ukr
If people are updating correctly, they should have smallish changes if the first draw is 110. Yet, people make too large of a change.

Then, the interface will condition on what the subject first chose. They will show all signals above 100, if that is what the subject chose, but only some of the signals below. The subjects know that this will be case, but still update too much based on what they see.
The experiment took place in person, which is a small point in its favor (to me). https://t.co/Npp8ezTV7H

A lab experiment, but one with interesting implications. I think our intuitions are often the taking of naive averages, and so we need to be more willing to outsource thinking through such things to credible statisticians.

Benjamin Enke, "What You See is All There Is" (2020)
https://t.co/iXfgGVdVcn

papers.entry_text:
Joshua Blonz, "The Costs of Misaligned Incentives: Energy Inefficiency and the Principal-Agent Problem" (2023)
https://t.co/n5zvPbrHoG

detailed_thread.text: Many states have programs to encourage energy efficiency by replacing appliances for low-income households. California is one such, and replaced 41,529 refrigerators for free. The only trouble? Half of them were provided under fraudulent pretenses. 1/ https://t.co/pgFeZsRUfS

Contractors were incentivized by a piece rate system. If they weren't eligible for the program, they wouldn't be paid anything besides the fee for assessing. This incentivized them to do as many repairs as possible. https://t.co/ptPsgJUZNy

In 1993, the federal government tightened standards for energy efficiency in refrigerators. As such, the California Energy Savings Assistance program would, as part of a general retrofit program, replace only the models below 1992.
And what do you know, somehow everyone had fridges built just before the cutoff! https://t.co/pxzTwLRbEE

You might be worried that it just happened to be true by coincidence -- but how unlikely is it that when the cut-off was changed to 1998, everyone now has a refrigerator manufactured in 1997? https://t.co/jIITg7Vepk
Going month by month, when the threshold changed the reporting behavior of contractors changed immediately. https://t.co/QZgig9PJ4z
The utilities company for San Diego had a similar program, which did not feature the structural incentives to misreport. There is no such hump. https://t.co/3sFXMdMylX
The misreporting was far larger for refrigerators without a model number. https://t.co/wBnAfQnup7

And was, naturally, much larger when companies both diagnosed and replaced the fridge themselves. (There was still a payment to assess the fridge as needing replacement, of $25, but this is much less than the $222 paid for replacement). https://t.co/t75uVu6cmA

This fraud was a really big deal. Using a social cost of $0.10 per kWh, replacing fridges which really were built before 1992 would generate benefits of $60 per years. The fraudulent replacements had a social benefit of negative $106 per year.

Why didn't anyone catch it? Because the auditing was *only* concerned with whether the replacement was done. They didn't check until after the fridge was switched out. I am unable to find anything on how California changed their policy in response to this. I hope they have.
We have a much too cavalier attitude toward eligibility. Medicaid work requirements being bad and ineffective should not polarize us against caring about whether we should be redistributing or not.

Joshua Blonz, "The Costs of Misaligned Incentives: Energy Inefficiency and the Principal-Agent Problem" (2023)
https://t.co/36i6WtAMVl
@ArmandDoma do you know anything about whether they have improved since?

papers.entry_text:
Maria Micaela Sviatschi, "Spreading Gangs: Exporting US Criminal Capital to El Salvador" (2022)
https://t.co/UDvM4U8xH8

detailed_thread.text: We shouldn't deport criminals -- we should imprison them. When the U.S. dumped hundreds of violent, dangerous men back on El Salvador, we created the gangs which terrorized the nation for decades. If someone is too criminal to be in our country, they're too criminal to exist. 1/ https://t.co/5zcptAEbVy

El Salvador had a civil war throughout the 1980s. Many of the people who fled went to Los Angeles, where they learned how to organize gangs from the Crips and the Bloods. Obviously, we don't want Barrio 18 or MS-13, so when the war ended, we deported hundreds.

These people had no criminal record in El Salvador, so they were released onto the street. These were the nucleus of the gangs. I've covered the effects here -- they were really, *really* bad.
https://t.co/7eVzaluCSQ

In order to make a causal argument, we need data with variation. This will be a standard differences-in-differences paper, where municipalities exposed to more deported gang members will have more gangs later on, but be similar beforehand. https://t.co/HIesSt0EfS
After 1996, crimes committed by people in the gangs increased, while non-gang crimes stayed flat. https://t.co/RzjLfB86SY
This was especially strong for people who were younger at the time they were exposed to the gangs. https://t.co/iMRt7jm3FX

This is why I view deportation of criminal aliens as a ridiculous exercise, the equivalent of dumping toxic waste on your neighbor. If people are so violent that they cannot remain in our country, we should simply incarcerate them forever.

Maria Micaela Sviatschi, "Spreading Gangs: Exporting US Criminal Capital to El Salvador" (2022)
https://t.co/UEQerhPVck

papers.entry_text:
Mayshar, Moav, Pascali, “Origin of the state” (2022)
https://t.co/LlWta3sBGv

detailed_thread.text: What makes a state? It is not a surplus which matters, so much as the appropriability. Thinkers like James C. Scott have theorized that cereals like wheat allowed the state because it could be easily stolen. Here is an empirical test. 1/ https://t.co/0cUglhOlOG

The two basic views are these. On the one hand, it is possible that it is an agricultural surplus which leads to the state. Only with excess can an unproductive, parasitic elite survive. https://t.co/ELRXH8q0Ry

On the other hand, perhaps it is how easily it can be taken which matters. Tubers like potatoes can be dug up around the year, but spoil quickly. You cannot ship it far at all. Thus, you need flour. https://t.co/AC2zJ9b6PQ

Key to everything empirical are the potential yields. These are assembled from crop experiments in particular soils and climes. Land can vary both in absolute productivity, and in the difference between cereals and tubers. https://t.co/bO2gGHDtI2
Using these as an instrument, it is the difference in productivity which predicts a state, not the absolute level.
When the Columbian exchange happened, it was changes in relative productivity which mattered. https://t.co/C5AoZKi1fw

Mayshar, Moav, Pascali, “Origin of the state” (2022)
https://t.co/BOMDd7VRmD

papers.entry_text:
Tabakovic and Wollmann, "The Impact of Money on Science: Evidence from Unexpected NCAA Football Outcomes" (2019)
https://t.co/okHbxPUNRH

detailed_thread.text: Spending more on academia really does lead to more useful science being done. When schools perform unexpectedly well in football, the university as a whole gets more money. This plausibly random variation leads to more spending on materials, and thus more discoveries. 1/ https://t.co/gBWChvhub6
Part of this additional funding is directly from the football team, but a lot of it is also from fundraising. There is, to be clear, a lot of money involved. https://t.co/NH2f80XXXb
It impacts the spending on research from the universities own funds, but has no impact on federal funding, ruling out a possible confound. https://t.co/X0aFDEKeTh

Using this as an instrument, an increase of dollars by 10% increases patents and citations by 3.1% and 5.9%. Notably, simply regressing patents on dollars would show no effect, but this obviously would be inaccurate. https://t.co/v4dpsWMNL9

In order to interpret this as a general equilibrium effect, and not simply changing where the research takes place, we need to assume that the funding shocks only change materials, and don't lead to reallocating professors. This is believable to me.
It's so important that we fund scientific research. It is the root of our future economic growth.

Tabakovic and Wollmann, "The Impact of Money on Science: Evidence from Unexpected NCAA Football Outcomes" (2019)
https://t.co/rPs7twdg2r

papers.entry_text:
Amil Petrin, "Quantifying the Benefits of New Products: The Case of the Minivan" (2002)
https://t.co/L4IlXbR1G7

detailed_thread.text: The wonderful thing about capitalism is all the companies introducing new products in order to seize a momentary advantage. The minivan is one such product, leading to a gain of $2.8 billion for consumers over the first five years! 1/ https://t.co/KIGE5MpnWo
Notably, Ford had the idea first, but did not want to introduce it. They feared it cannibalizing their profitable station wagon business. Such is the beauty of competition. https://t.co/j2FSQG5INP

But I'm not going to talk about the minivan, really. Sorry. Gotcha there. Instead, we're gonna talk about methods. Given a demand curve, consumer surplus is equal to the integral under the curve. So, how do we find it? Let's start with Berry-Levinsohn-Pakes 1995, aka BLP 1995.

Consumers have choose between discrete goods. They gain aX, where X is observed characteristics, pay bP, where P is price, and also gain unobserved characteristics (and error). a and b govern a distribution which each consumer draws from -- it allows for flexible substitution.

(What happens if we don't have these distributions? The substitution is entirely driven by market shares, and we cannot account for the effects of different vs similar products entering. This is the red-bus/blue-bus problem -- a different color isn't a different model!)

You need instruments for price, which you can get from your knowledge of the industry. This pins down the mean of a and b. You also need variation in the choices available to pin down the distribution. BLP just assumes that characteristics are exogenous, and no complementarities.

Basically they're arguing that the characteristics of other products affect utility only through the competitive effect on price, and not any other way. (Original BLP paper does not have direct cost-shifters, that's more of a modern thing).

Look, no one believes this literally. Of course firms are competing in product characteristics. The trouble is that estimating competition in product space is ~impossible~. It's all suppositions. If you don't like it, you can use instruments for product availability.
Then, given an assumption about how firms compete in the industry, you can tell what the markup will be, and thus consumer surplus, and lots of other cool statistics of interest.

The trouble is the variance. With only market share data, we have to make distributional assumptions. We have no way of distinguishing "some love it, some hate it" from "everyone likes it". These have very different implications for welfare!
BLP 2004 would use an unusual dataset, where companies asked people what their second choice car would have been. Obviously extremely powerful, but not a very common source. What might be easier?

And that's where Petrin comes in. Given observable characteristics about the buyers, you can try and get these "micro-moments" to line up. The biggest one, clearly, is size of family -- you can see how much demand correlates with this.

Adding this in actually sharply reduces the consumer surplus from the minivan, making it plausible. The compensating variation in the first row is the amount needed to switch to the second choice car. (The decrease implies stark heterogeneity in preferences). https://t.co/jsi5a25yXK
Note too, that 43% of the increase in welfare came from decreases in the price of closely related competing station wagons. https://t.co/ZMwzIpotTP

Amil Petrin, "Quantifying the Benefits of New Products: The Case of the Minivan" (2002)
https://t.co/qSPf0MdZMF

papers.entry_text:
Bajari, Houghton, Tadelis, "Bidding for Incomplete Contracts: An Empirical Analysis of Adaptation Costs" (2014)
https://t.co/YJPDyvaC3V

detailed_thread.text: In infrastructure procurement, by far the biggest cause of wasted money is needing to redesign the contract mid-project. By comparison, the traditional concerns of the competitive auction literature generate a small distortion. A better plan could cut costs by 10%. 1/ https://t.co/ai1xTXnNmZ

In government procurement, companies bid a price per unit of work done -- such and so for a mile of asphalt, and something different per curb, and so on. The contractors are also told how much will need to be done.

The actual quantity is basically always different, for three main reasons. First, there might be a very large difference -- say, 10,000 tons more asphalt than expected. This is presumed negligence, and Caltrans will haggle over how much to pay.

Second, the scope of the project might change. The company gets to the road, and discovers they actually need to dig a culvert. Who pays? Well, Caltrans and the company hash it out, a costly and risky procedure.
A brief pause for an anecdote: https://t.co/k6LiXglW6x

Third, Caltrans will deduct from the payment for poor performance. This will lead to a dispute, arbitration, and possible a lawsuit -- all very costly! So, if contractors expect one item to underrun and the other to overrun, they can bid more on the latter and less on the former.
We cannot directly measure the effect of changes because of this hedging in the bids. The degree of overrun is likely to be correlated with the bids. https://t.co/0Vs2RwQGOe
Further, there are both fixed and variable adaptation costs. https://t.co/wIbgWkNhk1
So instead, they explicitly model the beliefs of the contractors, and then use variation relative to this baseline to estimate the cost of adaptation.
Their results imply a cost $80 wasted negotiating for every $100 in adjustments, which is insanely high. https://t.co/2IhbRTyuXh
Every additional workday costs contractors $116 for every $100 they get compensation for. https://t.co/GtQCHS5um8
Parenthetical: the math in the auction literature intimidates me. No interpretation here will be ventured. https://t.co/x693siXgQz
Now the results, after plugging in the costs into the structural model. The industry is very competitive, with low profit margins. https://t.co/rVNxd0tjBl
And yet, the markups over average costs imply very high profit rates. The difference is in the pure waste from adaptation costs. https://t.co/79L5kjOZiz
In other words, between 7 and 14 percent of every contract is frittered away on reassessing what to do. https://t.co/GR3ZTLBtdZ
The main implication of this is that, if you can trust the government to not be corrupt, there's a very good case for negotiating contracts rather than auctioning them off. https://t.co/PpUDnY6xaC
Alternatively, we would be very well-served investing more in our state engineering departments! A better assessment of what is to be done is likely to be repaid many times over.

Bajari, Houghton, Tadelis, "Bidding for Incomplete Contracts: An Empirical Analysis of Adaptation Costs" (2014)
https://t.co/KUDr5LZxYP

papers.entry_text:
Thomas Wollmann, "How to Get Away With Merger: Stealth Consolidation and Its Effects on US Healthcare" (2025)
https://t.co/iRCLulfE0Z

detailed_thread.text: How do you get away with merger? Being just below the threshold of needing to report to the FTC leads to substantially worsened quality in the kidney dialysis sector. On the margin, we need more antitrust involvement in healthcare. https://t.co/DyRrSlJV6q

Some background. There are many, many companies which merge or acquire each other each year. We need some way to screen them, so the simplest one is size of transaction -- if you're small enough, you can merge.

In 2001, they raised the threshold from $15 to $50 million, which led to a sharp decrease in the number of notifications and the number of merger reviews. The ones who did not need to report led to much greater market concentration than those that did. https://t.co/MoKSCzmC8Q
Survival rates decreased and hospitalization rates increased. https://t.co/kHDNBBHKCq

This is arguably due to cuts in the number of employees. Since kidney dialysis is entirely government paid for, this is essentially just defrauding the government by providing a shoddier product. https://t.co/UMuhejWxCE
We recover marginal cost and demand with basic BLP methods which I have explained elsewhere. Government efficiency is estimated by change in caseloads per person. https://t.co/Akwg3BNijy

Moving up the threshold saved around five million dollars, and cost between $235 and $527 million, an enormous gap. This is, to be clear, not able to address costs to the merging parties, but we have reason to believe that they are much smaller than this. https://t.co/DbRNVJ9guJ
It is certainly much better to do this in advance rather than after the fact. https://t.co/f5arV42dIn

When an industry is advancing technologically, we should of course be cautious in advocating antitrust remedies. The high profits of now may be necessary for companies to innovate at all. But when an industry is a stagnant government contractor, not auditing is malpractice.

Thomas Wollmann, "How to Get Away With Merger: Stealth Consolidation and Its Effects on US Healthcare" (2025)
