https://t.co/0ihmLzJqzo

papers.entry_text:
Larcom, Rauch, Willems: "The Benefits of Forced Experimentation: Striking Evidence from the London Underground Network" (2017)
https://t.co/8c312TXbvf

detailed_thread.text: Do you experiment enough? Probably not. When When an unexpected rail strike shut down part of the London Underground, many people were forced to use different routes -- and they kept them, saving many minutes on their commute. Try new things! You may like them. 1/ https://t.co/7b2AZ8hACP
A February 2014 strike shut down 171 out of 270 stations, with the shutdowns being scattered around the city. People had to try out new routes. https://t.co/g5uqWUqCGH
We have data tracking each card and where they got on and off, allowing us to know who is a worker. https://t.co/DL0IZqSpKy
Workers got off a different stations during the strike. When it ended, some of them went back to the old routes, but others kept the new ones.
It's not crazy that people had non-optimal routes. The London Underground is a sufficiently confusing system for people to not really know where things are. https://t.co/QJHDPJ7wzf

The average person saved 20 seconds per journey, with this obviously being a two-peaked distribution. Multiplying by the prevailing wage, this implies a savings of at least $49 per person. https://t.co/yzWL2GLeR0

Larcom, Rauch, Willems: "The Benefits of Forced Experimentation: Striking Evidence from the London Underground Network" (2017)
https://t.co/YVEjy19Qei

papers.entry_text:
Chandra and Staiger, "Identifying Sources of Inefficiency in Healthcare" (2020)
https://t.co/MwxgbAjhz7

detailed_thread.text: Some hospitals do more procedures than others. Maybe it's because they are better at it. Maybe, though, it's because they are wrong. Chandra and Staiger separate out the two, and show that eliminating inaccuracies treatment would raise welfare by 44%. 1/ https://t.co/jTNMHY6Yyp

A heart attack is when one of the arteries to the heart is blocked by a clot, generally due to a rupture of the plaque which builds up in the artery. Reperfusion, the restoration of blood flow to the tissue, is needed immediately.

There are two basic ways to do this, drugs or surgery. Both can be use. The drugs, thrombolytics, break down blood clot; the surgery, an angioplasty, involves inserting a catheter with an inflatable balloon to push open the artery and restore blood flow.
The main variation is in whether using drugs or not. It will clear out the clots everywhere, so patients in danger of excessive blood flow can be actually harmed by it.
The authors have substantial information on patient symptoms and characteristics from Medicare, if admittedly old. https://t.co/0u34lnt7qw

Their method to separate out private information from hospitals and error is to take the characteristics of patients, and see how it predicts treatment across the whole sample. We then compare similar patients across different hospitals.
Patients who exogenously receive more treatment live shorter. https://t.co/ym1aIJjiPg
Reperfusion does work, but it works less among patients who need it less. https://t.co/84PtrBhr5f
Putting numbers on the gains from optimally treating patients suggests it would increase welfare by about 44%.

Chandra and Staiger, "Identifying Sources of Inefficiency in Healthcare" (2020)

https://t.co/u3kh71k1Bp

papers.entry_text:
Asil, Ramos, Starc, Wollmann: "Painful Bargaining: Evidence from Anesthesia Rollups" (2026)
https://t.co/zQVGdraHlz

detailed_thread.text: The FTC uses several "screens" to evaluate whether to investigate a merger, including what the effect on market concentration would be. Transactions below a certain size are also exempted. This gives firms latitude, though, to structure their transactions to avoid scrutiny. 1/ https://t.co/17hUAy8LZw

Asil, Ramos, Starc, and Wollman consider anesthesia providers. Anesthesiologists generally work for practices who negotiate with a hospital to provide services, though sometimes they employ the anesthesiologist directly. These practices employ 10-300 people.

Billing is separate, and the patient doesn't choose. Surprisingly often, when the anesthesiologists and hospitals don't agree on the price they bill it as out of network and dump it on the patient. https://t.co/KHcMqLQ1ej
Both entry and exit are rare. Anesthesiologists are highly paid -- around $500,000 a year -- in large part because of the enormous barriers to entry. https://t.co/woamwDJoYQ

Because of the high barriers, horizontal consolidation reducing competition is a big concern. What firms noticed, though, is that you can evade review by making each successive step fall under thresholds used to evaluate a case. But how big is this, really? https://t.co/X0N0zqceEv
First thing we can show is that there were a lot of them. Out of 339 practices, 85 were acquired between 2012 and 2021. https://t.co/bmVynS8wzM
Prices don't rise with the first entry of a private equity firm into a market, but do with subsequent acquisitions. https://t.co/Jrsp3BQtXA
If you regress the change in concentration on the change in price, it is distinctly positively associated. https://t.co/yDdRJ5HxDo
Is this an econometrically significant exercise? I would argue yes, because the response comes so immediately after, and there is uncertainty over when the merger will be consummated.
I view the famous warnings against regressing HHI on price as being really about the cross section. You obviously can't take all the markets, run a regression, and recover anything meaningful.
Measures of quality do stay the same, so this is only a a change in price. https://t.co/UA4G9x0bDn
They then turn to a formal bargaining model, the details of which I will elide. The important thing is that we observe the bargaining split, and thus can infer their relative power in negotiations.

They can then show what might happen if many of the mergers were unwound. Note that, unlike almost everywhere, the egg can be unscrambled -- the practices remain functionally independent and can be easily divested. https://t.co/qSaXSOeaNQ
Unwinding them would save payors 4% a year, or $96 million. https://t.co/2ZMgqrmjDS
Deterring future acquisitions would save payors $40 a million a year, largely because the most lucrative markets have already been rolled up. https://t.co/Eq5tqfHB2N

And what would happen if we removed some of the barriers to entry that firms face? Note that some of these are self-created -- we have companies secretly paying each other millions of dollars not to enter markets. https://t.co/9USIjxp7Y8

It turns out that barriers to entry across markets don't matter much when anesthesiologists are kept from training for it. Transferring from one market to another just wouldn't do much -- the gains would be partially offset by a reduction in competition elsewhere. https://t.co/9fqzv8l0Jk
The big policy change we need, I think, is to flexibly adjust merger review thresholds to consider the full history of transactions which a firm is engaging in.

Asil, Ramos, Starc, Wollmann: "Painful Bargaining: Evidence from Anesthesia Rollups" (2026)
https://t.co/xzKtOmbTHe

papers.entry_text:
Michael Anderson, "Subways, Strikes, and Slowdowns: The Impacts of Public Transit on Traffic Congestion" (2014)
https://t.co/sqxPa3AmdE

detailed_thread.text: It turns out that public transit is extremely important for reducing traffic. Because the relationship of traffic and congestion is extremely non-linear, a shutdown of transit can cause massive increases in congestion -- justifying substantial subsidies for trains and buses. 1/ https://t.co/FBY2pZPTkD

tThis is a methodologically simple paper, but very clean. In October 2003, the LA public transit workers went on strike for 35 days. We can consider the change in congestion, after sanity testing it, to be the effect of the MTA.

During peak hours, the average delay increased by about 50% relative to what free-flowing traffic would be. That's (roughly) going from 1:24 per mile to 1:36, or the equivalent of making a 28 minute commute last 32 minutes instead. https://t.co/VeK5VizsKX
That's for traffic everywhere, though. Traffic along roads where the subway ran saw much starker changes in travel time. https://t.co/Uto04k0ziO
The implied benefit of transit in 2003, just from reducing congestion, was somewhere between 1 and 4 billion dollars a year! https://t.co/gXiiKfOl9q

Michael Anderson, "Subways, Strikes, and Slowdowns: The Impacts of Public Transit on Traffic Congestion" (2014)
https://t.co/9tuEbP5Guk

papers.entry_text:
Gabriel Kreindler, "Peak-Hour Road Congestion Pricing: Experimental Evidence and Equilibrium Implications" (2024)
https://t.co/c7cTrNd1MP

detailed_thread.text: This is both a cautionary note on road pricing, arguing that (in developing countries) the deadweight loss of congestion is actually very small, and an incredible example of how RCTs can be used for deeper work. A must read: 1/ https://t.co/T9B2Pzgrao

The city is Bangalore. It is a city which looks something like this: needless to say, traffic is notoriously bad. In 2014, traveling speed was down to 5.7 miles per hour, with citizens stuck in 240 hours of traffic jams a year. Kreindler estimates average speed of 10.3 mph. https://t.co/LRxOT1f0rz
Rush hour is between 7 and 9 am, with the travel delay increasing by 1.38 minutes per kilometer. (In other words, a 40 minute trip turns into an hour).

What could we do? Placing a tax on the roads would be helpful, for several reasons. The first is that traffic is a non-linear function of people on the road, so you can big changes in traffic from small reductions in people on a road. The other is sorting for higher value usages.

Public transit is limited in Bangalore, so the main margin of adjustment is in the time in which people start their trips. If more price sensitive people started at 6 am, everyone traveling during regular hours would be better off.

There is considerable variation in when people start their travels, but, we cannot infer from this that it means people are flexible. They could have an inflexible need for travel, which varies over time. https://t.co/lALpqs3AS2

What we will do is write down a model describing people's preferences over travel time, the cost of arriving late, and the distribution of arrival times, and implement an experiment to exactly identify each of the parameters.

This is the beauty of randomized controlled trials. You can choose a design which identifies exactly what you want it to. One of the knocks on RCTs is that they need not generalize, because you do not know the channel -- if you test x on y, you do not know the effects of ~x on y.
Now, so long as you have an estimate of the parameters, and you believe that the model describes the world, then you can answer any counterfactual policy.

As for the model itself, it's really not that complicated. I would urge you not to be intimidated by the visual complexity of economic models -- they are simply a way of bringing data to interesting questions, and so can be decomposed into a few main things.
The experiments for departure time and for congestion charges were as follows. https://t.co/po0QzRUMUO
We can show that people do respond to the changes, and get the implied values they place on time and price. https://t.co/TgjNpBDzC9
He gets the effect of traffic density on travel time from GPS data from Google Maps. https://t.co/V3qcu4KewK
Interestingly, he finds a linear relationship between density and delay. https://t.co/Ti3TP1w3qD
So, now we can model traffic as people with the preferences identified from the experiments bargaining, and solve. https://t.co/J2hOAvqPYi
Solving is done iteratively. Some small portion of agents choose when to leave, and then the optimal responses are calculated. https://t.co/DNBohDWu8i

The main results? Because people have definite preferences over when they leave, the welfare gains are almost 6%, and much smaller than a model which would rationalize the heterogeneity in leaving time as due to people being very flexible. https://t.co/3wSsDFwggR

As implied by what I said before, though, replacing the linear relation of traffic with road density with a non-linear one will sharply increase the cost of traffic, and thus the gains from an optimal tax. https://t.co/MTNp6k8Vks

If you recall "Optimal Urban Transportation Policy: Evidence from Chicago" they found a sharp and non-linear traffic function.
https://t.co/2GiA57Hbd7
What's going on? I think Chicago has times when there is no traffic, but Bangalore *never* has times of no traffic. https://t.co/K3qq5ZBHZT

Gabriel Kreindler, "Peak-Hour Road Congestion Pricing: Experimental Evidence and Equilibrium Implications" (2024)
https://t.co/vXdqrFU56l

papers.entry_text:
Card, Dobkin, Maestas, “Does Medicare Save Lives?” (2009)
https://t.co/kxPEuIh9a1

detailed_thread.text: Medicare saves lives, and specifically through the channel of having more procedures. People just above 65 are way more likely to be on Medicare, and when they enter a hospital with emergency conditions, they have a 7% reduction in mortality a week after the event. 1/ https://t.co/L89oesEpYN

This is what is called a “regression discontinuity” design. We assume that people’s health does not change suddenly when they turn 65, only their health insurance does. Thus, we can infer the effect of Medicare on health.
As expected, total insured jumps sharply at 65. https://t.co/FdgkfZ2nrf
And health risk, as best we can measure, is unchanged. https://t.co/V5YBXHrCAJ
A concern, though, is that the number of elective procedures also spikes, as it becomes more affordable. We can’t just look at the effect on people who came for any reason. https://t.co/Gk9lGljtJL
Instead, we’ll look at serious emergencies, where the decision to go is unrelated to insurance — heart attacks, strokes, and bronchitis. https://t.co/BFHACjLDqz
There is an increase in the number of procedures. https://t.co/lxQ1TO08jm
And a decrease in mortality. https://t.co/2hqRl5kq3I
This is not purely an artifact of fitting the datapoints with a quadratic — you can fit with anything. https://t.co/TYrk6QcEiA

Card, Dobkin, Maestas, “Does Medicare Save Lives?” (2009)

https://t.co/fbEZUUwURj
